Gross/Weapon System Cost ($ in Millions)                                                           114.568                    25.793                   48.393                 51.257             0.000             51.257

                            (The following Resource Summary rows are for informational purposes only. The corresponding budget requests are documented elsewhere.)
Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                    -                 -                  -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                    -                 -                  -



 Description:
 Procures AN/BLQ-10 and subsystem Product Improvement Field Change Kits (FCK). One (1) set of AN/BLQ-10 FCKs is procured in each fiscal year that includes variable quantities and types, procured from a
 variety of vendors.

 Procures MMM Payload Module antennas to support submarine Fleet required improved Unmanned Aerial System (UAS) communications and Electronic Warfare (EW) capability to meet emerging threats and
 provide new capabilities required by the Fleet.

 Procures Submarine Tethered Expendable Buoy (STEB), a 3" Launcher deployed, expendable, free-floating buoy that will communicate with the submarine through a fiber-optic tether.

 Procures the Automated Range Finder (ARF) Collision Avoidance System for SSBN (OHIO) Class submarines. This system will increase situational awareness while operating at periscope depth, improving the
 safety, stealth, and security of the submarine.

 Procures Virginia Class Mission Configurable Bay (MCB) update to receive a Multifunction Modular Mast (MMM), ensuring all SSNs can support payload required for Fleet priorities.

 Description:
 The DDG Modernization (DDG MOD) Program is required to upgrade the in-service Flight I, II, and IIA DDG-51 Class ships in order to keep the DDG Class relevant and operational. The DDG MOD Program
 is composed of a series of improvements in both Hull, Mechanical, and Electrical (HM&E) and Combat Systems. Modernization changes are aligned with New Construction design to increase commonality and
 capitalize on Economic Order Quantities across all programs. In FY26, DDG MOD continues DDG MOD 2.0, the next phase of combat capability enhancements bringing Integrated Air and Missile Defense
 (IAMD) capability to the DDG 51 Class. In addition to the DDG MOD 1.0 HM&E and Combat Systems upgrades, DDG MOD 2.0 ships will be equipped with SLQ-32(V)7 Surface Electronic Warfare Improvement
 Program (SEWIP) Block III (funded in 0204228N OPN 2312 AN/SLQ-32), SPY-6(V)4 Air and Missile Defense Radar (AMDR) with updated AEGIS Baseline 10M / Ballistic Missile Defense (BMD) 6M, and High
 Efficiency Super-Capacity (HES-C) Chillers. DDG MOD 2.0 upgrades are planned for each ship's mid-life availability.

 PB26 reflects updated ship schedules and resourcing as summarized below:
 DDG 97 (USS HALSEY) resourcing in FY25 vice FY24
 DDG 98 (USS FORREST SHERMAN) resourcing in FY26 vice FY25
 DDG 101 (USS GRIDLEY) availability shifted from FY26 to a future FY
 DDG 102 (USS SAMPSON) availability shifted from a future FY to FY26
 DDG 109 FY25 procurements and future FY availability deferred
 Prior year procurements from availability deferrals continue to support installations on DDG 102 in FY26 and DDG 101 in a future FY.
 Incorporation of updated private sector man-day rates.

 Installation quantities starting in FY26 are as follows:
 HM&E Modernization: - 1/0/0/0/0
 DDG MOD 1.0: 1/0/0/0/0
 DDG MOD 1.5: 2/1/0/0/0
 DDG MOD DMP2: 1/1/2/0/0
 DDG MOD 2.0: 0/0/0/2/2

 The definitions for DDG MOD installations are listed below:

HM&E Modernization: HM&E
DDG MOD 1.0: HM&E + C/S
DDG MOD 1.5: HM&E + C/S + SLQ-32(V)7 (SLQ-32(V)7 is funded outside of DDG MOD 2.0)
DDG MOD DMP2: SPY-6(V)4 + HES-C (these hulls have already received DDG MOD 1.5 in a previous availability)
DDG MOD 2.0: HM&E + C/S + SLQ-32(V)7 + SPY-6(V)4 + HES-C



HM&E modernization is comprised of Commercial Off the Shelf (COTS) technologies for Ship Control and Engineering Plant Control Systems. New systems provide increased situational awareness, reduce
manpower workloads, and address reliability and obsolescence challenges with legacy systems.

C/S modernization upgrades the Aegis Weapon Systems (AWS). The upgrades enable the DDG-51 Class to conduct Ballistic Missile Defense simultaneously while conducting Air Defense within its assigned
theatre. Upgrades allow for the use of AEGIS Open Architecture (AOA) replacing legacy signal processors with a Multi-Mission Signal Processor (MMSP) for DDG MOD 1.0 ships. For DDG MOD 1.5, DMP2 and
DDG MOD 2.0 ships, C/S modernization establishes the foundation for upgrading the DDG-51 Class from the SPY-1 radar array to the SPY-6(V)4 solid-state radar, enabling the DDG-51 Class to remain tactically
competitive against emerging threats.

DDG MOD installations traditionally utilize a Firm Fixed Price Contracting Strategy for both the East and West Coasts of the United States. Installation costs require multiple years of funding per ship to support
Ship Checks (S/C), Ship Installation Drawing (SID) development, Long Lead Time Material purchase, and physical installation. Installation costs are budgeted based on the ship's homeport man-day rates as
promulgated by the Navy.

[P40A / DM002 - LAND BASED ENGINEERING SITES]: Land Based Engineering Sites (DM002) upgrades shore facilities utilized for pre-installation testing and crew training of HM&E and Combat Systems
alterations.

[P40A / DM012 - CEC]: Procures Cooperative Engagement Capability (CEC) combat systems that includes equipment procurement, recurring engineering, equipment integration and Integrated Logistics Support
(ILS) products for DDG 51 Class ships. Flight IIA DDGs have previously received CEC installation.

[P40A / DM013 - CONJUNCTIVE ALTERATION DEFINITION AND INTEGRATION]: Provides Conjunctive Alteration Definition and Integration including equipment procurement, non-recurring and recurring
engineering, equipment integration and Integrated Logistics Support (ILS) products for DDG 51 Class ships.

[P40A / DM6IN - INSTALLATION]: Provides for installation of Fleet Modernization Program alterations that are associated with DDG MOD. Alterations include Fuel Oil System Control upgrades, Lube Oil Purifier
improvements, Digital Indicators modifications, Vertical Launch System (VLS) Magazine ventilation changes, Microwave Tubes (DM006) and Ballistic Missile Defense (BMD) Cards (DM008). Upgrades address
obsolescence and reliability problems with existing systems, support SM-3 missile operations, enable BMD missions, and improve operational availability of the DDG 51 Class ships.

[P40A / HELO HANGER DOOR (HHD)]: Provides HHD upgrades for DDG-51 FLTIIA ships (DDG 113 and follow) to resolve documented HHD reliability issues.

[P40A / OFFICER OF THE DECK SCREEN (OOD)]: Replaces the Interior Communication/Standard Module alarm panels in the 3 OOD stations and Combat Systems Maintenance Center with Multi-Function
Color Repeaters for standardization of components and commonality with DDG 105 and follow design.

Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                         P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 01: Ships Support Equipment / BSA 9: Other 0900 / DDG Mod
Shipboard Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                      Program Elements for Code B Items: 0204228N                              Other Related Program Elements: 0604567N, 0604307N,
                                                                                                                                                0204228N
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 [P40A / AWS VIDEO DISTRIBUTION SYSTEM & CIRCUIT 3 TV]: Replaces obsolete and unsupported legacy topside surveillance camera CKT-3TV Systems on HM&E MOD Avails (including sensors, pan/tilt,
 unit, controllers and other HW) with shock and environmentally qualified Commerical Off the Shelf (COTS) Camera System.

[P40A / DIGITAL VOLTAGE REGULATOR (DVR)]: Replaces the obsolete legacy Excitation Control Panel (EXCOP) for three onboard generators and achieves commonality with DDG 113 and follow design.

[P40A / COMBAT SYSTEM SHIP QUALIFICATION TRIALS (CSSQT)]: Provides for test planning and execution of live-fire testing enabling validation of the newly installed C/S equipment. CSSQT is required
by Navy policy when major modifications are made to the C/S to ensure the kill chain functions properly and can support national tasking. It additionally provides real world training for the ship's crew and combat
watch standers.

[P40A / AEGIS MODERNIZATION TESTING (AMT)]: Provides on-site engineering and technical support for AEGIS and C/S Modernization. AMT teams support AEGIS-system Light Off (ALO), C/S verification
and alignment, and crew training for operations and maintenance of new AEGIS Combat Systems. AMT efforts support availability and installation planning efforts, engineering design products for upgrades,
shipboard installation, and system testing.

[P40A / DMCA5-BROMINE-FREE WATER PURIFICATION SYSTEM CONGRESSIONAL ADD]: Provides for design engineering and installation planning for installation of bromine-free water purification systems
on DDG 51 class ships. Ship water purification systems historically used bromine as an antimicrobial. The bromine is introduced via scrubber cartridges, and these cartridges have supply chain risks and come
with special hazardous material handling requirements.

[P40A / DMCA8 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE - CONGRESSIONAL ADD]: Congressional add for artificial intelligence for ready relevant learning. This initiative will enhance the Virtual-Total Ship Training
Capability (V-TSTC), a virtual performance support training tool aimed at increasing individual Combat Systems Operational Sequencing System (CSOSS) familiarity and Advanced Training Domain (ATD)
system readiness.

[P3A / DM001 - DDG MODERNIZATION HME (GEDMS)]: Gigabit Ethernet Data Multiplexing System (GEDMS) replaces the obsolete copper machinery control Local Area Network (LAN) infrastructure with a
new system based on the widely used Ethernet industry standard.

[P3A - 2 / DM001 - DDG MODERNIZATION HME (MCS/DCS)]: Machinery Control System (MCS) replaces the obsolete control consoles while also delivering enhanced capability to the Fleet through improved
situational awareness and reconfigurable user stations. The new consoles use Commercial Off the Shelf (COTS)-based computers and software to replace the now obsolete Versa Modular Eurocard (VME)-
chassis technology. The architecture is anticipated to be supportable for the remaining service life of the ship and will enable computer upgrades within the chassis thereby mitigating future obsolescence
concerns.

[P3A - 3 / DM001 - DDG MODERNIZATION HME (DVSS)]: Digital Video Surveillance System (DVSS) is a network of video cameras integrated into the Machinery Control System. This system provides
watchstander situational awareness which supplements the workload reduction benefits of the new MCS.

[P3A - 4 / DM001 - DDG MODERNIZATION HME (WIRELESS COMMUNICATIONS)]: Shipwide Interior Wireless Communication System (SIWCS) provides point-to-point coverage throughout the internal
spaces of the ship as well as topside, topside-to-interior, line of sight ship-to-ship & line of sight ship-to-shore communications capabilities.

[P3A - 5 / DM001 - DDG MODERNIZATION HME (IBNS)]: The Integrated Bridge & Navigation System (IBNS) replaces obsolete bridge consoles and introduces flexibility with how the pilot house is managed
during navigation. The key benefit is the ability to operate with fewer watch-standers, reduced watch standard workloads, and improved safety of navigation. Navigation system upgrades transitions from IBNS to
the Ship Control System (SCS) and DDG Electronic Charting Display Information System (ECDIS) Navigation hardware beginning with installation in a future FY.

[P3A - 6 / DM001 - HES-C]: High Efficiency Super Capacity (HES-C) Chillers modernize the 50-year-old Air Conditioning and Refrigeration (AC&R) systems installed on the DDG-51 Class. This upgrade replaces
five AC&R systems on each ship receiving the SPY-6(V)4 Air and Missile Defense Radar (AMDR), to be installed conjunctively.

[P3A - 7 / DM003 - MK 160 MOD 15 GWS]: Provides MK 160 Mod 15 Gun Weapon System (GWS) upgrades including equipment procurement, recurring engineering, equipment integration, installation, and
Integrated Logistics Support (ILS) products for DDG 51 Class ships.

[P3A - 8 / DM004 - AWS UPGRADE]: Provides Aegis Weapon System (AWS) Upgrades including equipment procurement, recurring engineering, equipment integration, installation and Integrated Logistics
Support (ILS) products for DDG 51 Class ships.

[P3A - 9 / DM005 - MULTI-MISSION SIGPRO]: Provides the Multi-Mission Signal Processor (MMSP) to enable Ballistic Missile Defense capabilities for ships receiving C/S modernization upgrades and retaining
the SPY-1(D) radar. Includes equipment procurement, recurring engineering, equipment integration, installation, and Integrated Logistics Support (ILS) products for DDG 51 Class ships. MMSP is not required for
DDG MOD 2.0 ships receiving the SPY-6(V)4 radar upgrade.

[P3A - 10 / DM006 - MULTI-MISSION SSA/CWI MICROWAVE TUBES]: Provides the Multi-Mission Signal Processor (MMSP) to enable Ballistic Missile Defense capabilities for ships receiving C/S modernization
upgrades and retaining the SPY-1(D) radar. Includes equipment procurement, recurring engineering, equipment integration, installation, and Integrated Logistics Support (ILS) products for DDG 51 Class ships.
Microwave Tubes are not required for DDG MOD 2.0 ships receiving the SPY-6(V)4 radar upgrade.

[P3A - 11 / DM008 - MULTI-MISSION BMD CAPABILITY CARDS]: Provides Multi-Mission Ballistic Missile Defense (BMD) Capability cards including equipment procurement, recurring engineering, equipment
integration, installation, and Integrated Logistics Support (ILS) products for DDG 51 Class ships. BMD cards are not required for DDG MOD 2.0 ships receiving the SPY-6(V)4 radar upgrade.

[P3A - 12 / DM009 - VLS UPGRADES]: Provides Vertical Launch System (VLS) Modifications, Evolved Sea Sparrow Missile (ESSM) VLS Modifications, SM3 Operability Heating Ventilation Air Conditioning
(HVAC) and VLS Modifications for SM3 Block 3 including equipment procurement, recurring engineering, equipment integration, installation and Integrated Logistics Support (ILS) products for DDG 51 Class
ships.

[P3A - 13 / DM011 - AN/SQQ-89 A(V15)]: Procures improved AN/SQQ-89(V)15 with Multi-Function Towed Array (MFTA) to replace the installed MIL-STD AN/SQQ-89(V) including equipment procurement,
recurring engineering, equipment integration, installation and Integrated Logistics Support (ILS) products for DDG 51 Class ships.

[P3A - 19 / DM014 SOLID STATE RADAR EQUIPMENT]: SPY-6(V)4 solid-state radar provides the latest capability to the DDG-51 Class for Air Defense and Ballistic Missile Defense. The SPY-6(V)4 upgrade
replaces legacy SPY-1 radar arrays with SPY-6 arrays to deliver AEGIS Baseline 10 with Integrated Air and Missile Defense (IAMD) capability. SPY-6(V) 4 upgrades are applicable to DDG 51 Flight IIA ships,
DDG 91 through DDG 124 & DDG 127. SPY-6(V)4 fielding plans are integrated with SEWIP BLK III (BLI 2312) in order to rapidly deliver enhanced capability to the DDG-51 Class required to pace new threats by
international competitors.

Justification:
The FY 2026 request for BLI 0900 DDG MOD includes $686,787K of discretionary and $310,698K of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $997,485K. The mandatory funds augment procurement, engineering,
installation, and shipboard testing outlined by this exhibit to provide unmatched combat capability via modernization for the in-service DDG-51 FLT IIA Fleet. Further information for this reconciliation request is
provided in Section 2002 (Shipbuilding) and Section 2010 (Readiness) of the Reconciliation Exhibit.

The FY 2026 request was reduced by $52.913 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
"Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

Footnotes:
(1)
      Land Based Engineering Sites (DM002) upgrades shore facilities utilized for pre-installation testing and crew training of HM&E and Combat Systems alterations.
(2)
      Installation efforts are accomplished via the Alteration Installation Teams (AITs) and the AEGIS Modernization Testing Team resourced in DM6IN.
(3)
    Combat System Ship Qualification Trials (CSSQT) provides event planning and execution for validation of newly modernized and installed systems prior to a ship's return to Fleet operations. This effort spans multiple years and the CSSQT
is conducted at the end of a ship's modernization availability.
(4)
   AEGIS Modernization Testing (AMT) provides support from design engineering and space arrangement prior to an availability through equipment installation, validation, and checkout for comprehensive combat system testing during a
ship's availability. This effort spans all years of planning and execution for planned modernizations.

 Description:
 Gigabit Ethernet Data Multiplexing System (GEDMS) replaces the obsolete copper machinery control Local Area Network (LAN) infrastructure with a new system based on the widely used Ethernet industry
 standard.

 Engineering Services:
 Production Support Services are phased over a three (3) year span starting two (2) years prior to installation on a per hull basis. It includes five (5) elements that are related to the production and delivery of a
 usable end-item:
 Production Engineering, Quality Assurance, Product Improvement, Acceptance Test and Evaluation, and Contractor Engineering and Technical Services (CETS). Due to the phasing of the requirement over a
 three year period,
 engineering services will fluctuate depending on the number of ships in each year.

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                    -                 -                      -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Millions)                                                           -                         -                         -                    -                 -                      -



 Description:
 Machinery Control System (MCS) replaces the obsolete control consoles while also delivering enhanced capability to the Fleet through improved situational awareness and reconfigurable user stations. The
 new consoles integrate Commercial Off the Shelf (COTS)-based and software to replace the now obsolete Versa Modular Eurocard (VME)-chassis technology found in prior variants. The new architecture is
 anticipated to support the remaining service life of the ship and will enable computer upgrades within the chassis thereby mitigating future obsolescence concerns.

 Engineering Services:

 Production Support Services are phased over a three (3) year span starting two (2) years prior to installation on a per hull basis. It includes five (5) elements that are related to the production and delivery of a
 usable end-item:
 Production Engineering, Quality Assurance, Product Improvement, Acceptance Test and Evaluation, and Contractor Engineering and Technical Services (CETS). Due to the phasing of the requirement over a
 three-year period,
 engineering services will fluctuate depending on the number of ships in each year.

 Description:
 Digital Video Surveillance System (DVSS) is a network of video cameras which are integrated into the Machinery Control System (MCS). This system is primarily a watch-stander situational awareness tool
 which supplements the workload reduction benefits of the new MCS.

 Description:

 sight ship-to-shore communications capabilities.

                            (The following Resource Summary rows are for informational purposes only. The corresponding budget requests are documented elsewhere.)
Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                    -                 -                      -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Millions)                                                           -                         -                         -                    -                 -                      -



 Description:
 The Integrated Bridge & Navigation System (IBNS) replaces obsolete bridge consoles and introduces flexibility with how the pilot house is managed during navigation. The key benefit is the ability to operate with
 fewer watch-standers, reduced watch standard workloads, and improved safety of navigation. Navigation system upgrades transitions from IBNS to the Ship Control System (SCS) and DDG Electronic Charting
 Display Information System (ECDIS) Navigation hardware beginning with installation in a future FY.


 Engineering Services:

 Production Support Services are phased over a three (3) year span starting two (2) years prior to installation on a per hull basis. It includes five (5) elements that are related to the production and delivery of a
 usable end-item:
 Production Engineering, Quality Assurance, Product Improvement, Acceptance Test and Evaluation, and Contractor Engineering and Technical Services (CETS). Due to the phasing of the requirement over a
 three-year period,
 engineering services will fluctuate depending on the number of ships in each year.

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Millions)                                                           -                         -                         -                    -                 -                  -



 Description:
 High Efficiency Super Capacity (HES-C) Chillers modernize the 50-year-old Air Conditioning and Refrigeration (AC&R) systems installed on the DDG-51 Class. HES-C provides increased cooling capacity while
 reducing mechanical complexity making it lighter, more efficient and improving operational availability. This upgrade replaces five AC&R systems on each ship receiving the SPY-6(V)4 Air and Missile Defense
 Radar (AMDR), to be installed conjunctively.
 FY25 procurements transition the HES-C system from a two-year production lead time to a three-year production lead time. Hardware procurement costs in FY25 and follow years have increased based on FY24
 contract award for DDG-91 equipment. Installation requirements have increased beginning with initial installation on DDG-91 in FY26 based on technical maturity of installation design.


 Engineering Services support development and delivery of logistics support products, training support, etc. for the HES-C system.

 Description:
 Procures equipment for the AEGIS Weapon System (AWS) Upgrades that includes equipment procurement, recurring engineering, equipment integration, installation and Integrated Logistics Support (ILS)
 products for DDG 51 Class ships.

 Engineering Services:

 Production Support Services are phased over a three (3) year span starting two (2) years prior to installation on a per hull basis. It includes five (5) elements that are related to the production and delivery of a
 usable end-item:
 Production Engineering, Quality Assurance, Product Improvement, Acceptance Test and Evaluation, and Contractor Engineering and Technical Services (CETS). Due to the phasing of the requirement over a
 three-year period,
 engineering services will fluctuate depending on the number of ships in each year.

 Description:
 Procures Multi-Mission SIGPRO combat systems that includes equipment procurement, recurring engineering, equipment integration and Integrated Logistics Support (ILS) products for DDG 51 Class ships. This
 upgrade is applicable only to hulls not receiving the SPY-6(V)4 radar upgrade.

 Engineering Services:
 Production Support Services are phased over a three (3) year span starting two (2) years prior to installation on a per hull basis. It includes five (5) elements that are related to the production and delivery of a
 usable end-item: Production Engineering, Quality Assurance, Product Improvement, Acceptance Test and Evaluation, and Contractor Engineering and Technical Services (CETS). Due to the three-year phasing
 to support these requirements, engineering services will fluctuate based on the procurements and installations planned.

 Description:
 Procures improved AN/SQQ-89(V)15 with Multi-Function Towed Array (MFTA) to replace the installed MIL-STD AN/SQQ-89(V) including equipment procurement, recurring engineering, equipment integration,
 installation and Integrated Logistics Support (ILS) products for DDG 51 Class ships.SQQ-89 will complete its last install on DDG 78 in FY25.

 Engineering Services:

 Production Support Services are phased over a three (3) year span starting two (2) years prior to installation on a per hull basis. It includes five (5) elements that are related to the production and delivery of a
 usable end-item:
 Production Engineering, Quality Assurance, Product Improvement, Acceptance Test and Evaluation, and Contractor Engineering and Technical Services (CETS). Due to the phasing of the requirement over a
 three year period,
 engineering services will fluctuate depending on the number of ships in each year.

 Description:
 Procures the Dynalec Integrated Announcing System (DIAS) that replaces the ship's existing obsolete and unreliable general announcing system, enabling controlling watch stations to announce ship critical
 information to the crew.
 Integrated Logistics Support includes development of ILS documentation, review and certification. Environmental Quality Testing includes Shock, Vibration and Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) certification to
 ensure DIAS meets survivability requirements.

1810N / 01 / 9                                                                              0900 / DDG Mod                                                                      19 / DM014 SOLID STATE RADAR
                                                                                                                                                                                EQUIPMENT

                             Resource Summary                                         Prior Years                 FY 2024                   FY 2025                  FY 2026 Base       FY 2026 OOC       FY 2026 Total

Gross/Weapon System Cost ($ in Millions)                                                           173.520                   180.196                  379.109                 337.068             0.000            337.068
Less PY Advance Procurement ($ in Millions)                                                             -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -
Net Procurement (P-1) ($ in Millions)                                                              173.520                   180.196                  379.109                 337.068             0.000            337.068
Plus CY Advance Procurement ($ in Millions)                                                             -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -
Total Obligation Authority ($ in Millions)                                                         173.520                   180.196                  379.109                 337.068             0.000            337.068
                            (The following Resource Summary rows are for informational purposes only. The corresponding budget requests are documented elsewhere.)
Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Millions)                                                           -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -



 Description:
 SPY-6(V)4 solid-state radar provides the latest capability to the DDG-51 Class for Air Defense and Ballistic Missile Defense. The SPY-6(V)4 upgrade replaces legacy SPY-1 radar arrays with SPY-6 arrays to
 deliver AEGIS Baseline 10 with Integrated Air and Missile Defense (IAMD) capability. SPY-6(V) 4 upgrades are applicable to DDG 51 Flight IIA ships, DDG 91 through DDG 124 & DDG 127. SPY-6(V)4 fielding
 plans are integrated with SEWIP BLK III (BLI 2312) in order to rapidly deliver enhanced capability to the DDG-51 Class required to pace new threats by international competitors.

 Solid State Radar Installations take place over three years requiring two years of advance planning and an installation year.
 Production lead time and delivery dates reflect first shipset delivery within a procurement year.


 Engineering Services:
 Engineering services include the following efforts: Software Modifications and Land Based Testing prior to shipboard installation, and system integration efforts. In addition, FY26 and prior years also support
 Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) requirements for analysis and design efforts associated with first time integration of SPY-6(V)4 with combat systems and distributed services on in-service DDG-51 Class
 ships. This radar upgrade requires significant redesign of existing distributed service systems such as shipboard power, cooling, and dry-air systems to achieve successful integration. NRE includes Modeling &
 Simulation, Technical Data Package formulation, First Article Qualification and Environmental Testing.

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                               -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -             -
Flyaway Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                             -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -             -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -             -


 Description:
 The program procures shipboard firefighting equipment. Equipment is procured based on installation schedules.

 [P40A / HB003 - EMERGENCY ESCAPE BREATHING DEVICE (EEBD)]: Emergency Escape Breathing Device (EEBD) breathing apparatus for firefighters are reaching their service life of 15 years and must
 be replaced. EEBDs address a high priority CNO effort to replace expired breathing equipment aboard ships throughout the Fleet. The requirement for this capability has been validated by the experience of
 numerous shipboard casualties. EEBD replacement is required to ensure that ships will deploy with operational safety equipment on-board to allow for emergency escape from shipboard fires.

 [P40A / HB010 - DAMAGE CONTROL UPGRADES]: Damage Control Upgrades provides rapid assessments and initial outfitting (within a year) for new capabilities or obsolescence.

 [P40A / L2AB FIREFIGHTING PROGRAMS]: Findings of the Major Fires Review and BONHOMME RICHARD fire investigation recommendations as validated by VCNO in the Learning to Action Board (L2AB).
 Following the USS Bonhomme Richard fire, the L2AB was stood up to pursue solutions to damage control and firefighting. This program funds critical efforts in shipboard fire detection/suppression systems, fire
 prevention features, and advanced firefighting equipment; track programs to completion.

 [P40A / HB830 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING (PE)]: PE includes development of technical manuals, Planned Maintenance System (PMS), Provisioning Technical Documentation (PTD), Program Support Data
 (PSD) and Allowance Parts List (APLs), engineering to manage procurement and material support of design reviews, and program management.

 [P3A / HB002 - MAGAZINE SPRINKLING IMPROVEMENT]: Replaces the detection system designed in the 1960s which has performance deficiencies and is difficult to support and maintain. Replacement
 includes the Fire Detection and Actuation System (FDAS) and the Motor-Operated Pilot Valves (MOPV). When the FDAS detects a fire, the MOPV opens to provide the sprinkling for the magazine. The
 FDAS consists of heat detectors, smoke detectors for amphibious ships, and associated controls. MOPV is the same item procured under HB007 - SOPV Replacement. FDAS/MOPV provides increased fire
 performance and reduced lifecycle and logistics costs.

 [P3A - 2 / HB007 - SOPV REPLACEMENT]: The Solenoid Operated Pilot Valve (SOPV) provides a way for remote control of the Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF) and Countermeasure Wash Down (CMWD)
 systems. Existing SOPVs are unreliable, costly to repair, and produce the longest downtime for the AFFF system. Top Management Attention/Top Management Initiative (TMA/TMI) and INSURV interest due to
 impact of this item on AFFF system operation. Replacement of the SOPV with the Motor Operated Pilot Valve (MOPV) is required to ensure these systems are operational for emergencies. MOPV has a 50 year
 service life and will provide a significant reduction in preventative maintenance and cost.

 [P3A - 3 / HB008 - SCBA REPLACEMENT]: Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) is a compressed air breathing device compatible with a firefighter's protective wear and helmet, and other damage
 control equipment. The SCBA are reaching the end of their service life of 15 years and are being replaced with 30 year cylinders. SCBA replacement is required to ensure that ships will deploy with operational
 safety equipment on-board to provide breathable air while fighting shipboard fires.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 funding decrease is due to a cost decrease of $3.535M in HB002 MAGAZINE SPRINKLING IMPROVEMENT (six installations in FY 2025 to seven installations in FY 2026, cost variability with ship
 class and ship port location), a cost decrease of $0.744M in HB003 EEBD REPLACEMENT (four installations in FY 2025 to one installation in FY 2026, cost variability with ship class and ship port location), a
 cost increase of $0.670M in HB007 SOPV REPLACEMENT (six installations in FY 2025 to seven installations in FY 2026, cost variability with ship class and ship port location), a cost decrease of $2.445M in
 HB010 DAMAGE CONTROL UPGRADES, a cost increase of $0.828M in HB011 L2AB FIREFIGHTING PROGRAMS, a cost increase of $0.031M in HB830 PRODUCTION ENGINEERING, and a cost increase
 of $3.162M in HB008 SCBA REPLACEMENT (zero installations in FY 2025 to two installations in FY 2026).

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $1.798 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 Footnotes:
 (1)
    The P40 unit costs are identified as costs per ship for EEBD. Unit costs can vary based on the distribution of ship classes planned during each FY, quantities per ship class, and the ship port location. Requirement is for DDG-51 (Flight I, II,
 IIA), LCC 0019, LHA 0006, LHD 0001, and LPD 0017 ship classes. PROCUREMENT: Funding includes the breathing apparatus hardware, shipping, and distribution to ships. FY 2026 funding request shows a decrease of $0.744 million as
 the quantity of procurements decreased from four in FY 2025 to one in FY 2026. Procurement costs can vary based on the distribution of ship classes planned during each FY, quantities per ship class, and the ship port location.
 (2)
    FY 2026 funding request reflects a cost decrease of $2.445 million from the FY 2025 funding request due to a change in the damage control upgrades requirements. Damage Control Upgrades provides rapid assessments and initial
 outfitting (within a year) for new capabilities or obsolescence.
 (3)
     The increase in FY 2026 funding is due to an increase in L2AB program requirements. Funding provided for the findings of the Major Fires Review and BONHOMME RICHARD fire investigation recommendations as validated by VCNO
 in the Learning to Action Board (L2AB). Following the USS Bonhomme Richard fire, the L2AB was stood up to pursue solutions to damage control and firefighting. This program funds critical efforts in shipboard fire detection/suppression
 systems, fire prevention features, and advanced firefighting equipment; track programs to completion.
 (4)
    PE costs are calculated for each FY based on the fielding plan distribution and installation level of effort for each FY. FY 2026 funding request shows an increase of $0.031 million from FY 2025 to FY 2026 due to cost variability with ship
 class, ship port location, and increasing quantities of installations.

 Description:
 Replaces the detection system designed in the 1960s which has performance deficiencies and is difficult to support and maintain. Replacement includes the Fire Detection and Actuation System (FDAS) and the
 Motor-Operated Pilot Valves (MOPV). When the FDAS detects a fire, the MOPV opens to provide the sprinkling for the magazine. The FDAS consists of heat detectors, smoke detectors for amphibious ships,
 and associated controls. MOPV is the same item procured under HB007 - SOPV Replacement. FDAS/MOPV provides increased fire performance and reduced lifecycle and logistics costs.

 Procurement costs shown are based on new hardware contract award estimates (HB002/HB007) and actual installation costs from prior years . Installation costs shown are weighted and will vary based on ship
 class, ship port location, and installation availability.

 Quantities are number of ships.

 Requirement is for DDG 0051, LHA 0006, LHD 0001, LPD 0017 ship classes.

 PROCUREMENT: Procurement costs include FDAS and MOPV equipment, shipping, and distribution to ship. FDAS/MOPV procurement costs can vary significantly by ship class, flight, and quantity and/or size
 of the magazines. DDG-51 ships already have smoke detectors. PROCUREMENT costs are budgeted in the FY prior to equipment installation. PROCUREMENT costs may be budgeted in the same FY as
 installation based on installation schedule variation.

 INSTALLATION: Funding includes all costs associated with the Fleet Modernization Program Magazine Sprinkling Improvement Design Support Allocation (DSA) and equipment installations onboard ships. DSA
 costs are budgeted in the FY prior to equipment installation starting in FY2021. FDAS/MOPV installation costs can vary significantly by ship class, ship port location, and ship installation availability. DSA costs
 may be budgeted in the same FY as installation based on installation schedule variation.

 FY 2026 procurement costs are in support of seven (7) DDG 0051 Flight I/II/IIA ships (average $0.588 million) for a program total of $4.116 million.

 FY 2026 installation support services costs include are in support of seven (7) DDG 0051 Flight I/II/IIA ships (average $1.342 million) and DSA ($3.876M) for a program total of $13.268 million. Installation costs
 shown are weighted and will vary based on ship class, ship port location, and installation availability.

 Description:
 The Solenoid Operated Pilot Valve (SOPV) provides a way for remote control of the Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF) and Countermeasure Wash Down (CMWD) systems. Existing SOPVs are unreliable,
 costly to repair, and produce the longest downtime for the AFFF system. Top Management Attention/Top Management Initiative (TMA/TMI) and INSURV interest due to impact of this item on AFFF system
 operation. Replacement of the SOPV with the Motor Operated Pilot Valve (MOPV) is required to ensure these systems are operational for emergencies. MOPV has a 50 year service life and will provide a
 significant reduction in preventative maintenance and lifecycle cost.

 Procurement costs shown are based on new hardware contract award estimates (HB002/HB007) and actual installation costs from prior years. Installation costs shown are weighted and will vary based on ship
 class, flight, ship port location, and installation availability.

 Quantities are number of ships.

 Requirement is for DDG 0051, LHA 0006, LHD 0001, LPD 0017 ship classes.

 PROCUREMENT: Costs include MOPV equipment, shipping, and distribution to ship. SOPV procurement costs vary significantly based on ship class and flight. PROCUREMENT costs are budgeted in the FY
 prior to equipment installation, PROCUREMENT costs may be budgeted in the same FY as installation based on installation schedule variation.

 INSTALLATION: Funding includes all costs associated with the Fleet Modernization Program SOPV replacement Design Support Allocation (DSA) and equipment installations onboard ships. DSA costs are
 budgeted in the FY prior to equipment installation. SOPV installation costs vary significantly based on ship class, ship port location, and ship installation availability type. DSA costs may be budgeted in the same
 FY as installation based on installation schedule variation.

 FY 2026 procurement costs are in support of seven (7) DDG 0051 ships (average $0.539 million).

 FY 2026 installation costs are in support of seven (7) DDG 0051 ships (average $0.835 million) and DSA ($1.038 million).

 Description:
 Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) is a compressed air breathing device compatible with a firefighter's protective wear and helmet, and other damage control equipment. The SCBA are reaching the end
 of their service life of 15 years and are being replaced with 30 year cylinders. SCBA replacement is required to ensure that ships will deploy with operational safety equipment on-board to provide breathable air
 while fighting shipboard fires.

 Quantities are number of ships.

 Requirement: AFDL 0001, AFDM 0001, CG 0047, DDG 0051 (Flight I,II,IIA), LCC 0019, LCU, LHD 0001, LPD 0017, LSD 0041, LSD 0049, MCM 0001, and PC 0001 ship classes.

 PROCUREMENT: Funding includes the breathing apparatus, 45-minute air cylinders, and 30-minute air cylinders, shipping, distribution to, and modifying stowage on ships. SCBA procurement costs can vary
 significantly based on ship class.

 INSTALLATION: Funding includes all costs associated with the Fleet Modernization Program SCBA equipment installations onboard ships. SCBA installation costs can vary significantly based on ship class, ship
 port location and ship installation availability.

 SCBA replacement fielding will complete in FY 2026: LPD 26, LPD 27

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -      -              -


 Description:
 The switchboard program provides mission critical switching capability required to link shipboard combat equipment including weapons, launchers, sensors, computers and navigation equipment. In essence,
 switchboards serve as the central connection point for most elements of combat and weapon systems, interior communications, data transfer, and command and control systems. They are designed to
 accommodate either analog, digital interfaces, or a combination of both. In total, this budget item supports approximately 200 ships and 1,000 pieces of equipment throughout the acquisition life cycle.

 Functions include: data routing, action cutout, test and operating mode selection (including casualty back-up modes), power monitoring and control, circuit protection, peripheral equipment isolation, signal
 processing, and frequency conversion amplification and switching. In summary, the primary purpose is to provide systems intra and interface compatibility.

 Changes in other elements of the combat and Interior Communication (IC) systems will frequently mandate either conjunctive modification to switchboards via ship change documents (SCDs), ordnance alteration
 (ORDALT)/field changes (FCs) or partial or complete replacement of existing switchboards. Typical switchboard mods include hardware/field change kits, ORDALT/SCD/FC instructions, technical manual updates
 and revisions to other supporting documentation. Hull unique switchboard configurations require hull unique documentation. Subsequent alterations to these switchboards require hull unique design, hardware,
 installation, and checkout procedures. New Switchboards are normally installed during a regular overhaul by a shipyard.

 Command and control switchboards are currently installed on and are required for almost all surface combatants and amphibious warfare ships. Individual switchboard unit cost varies from ship to ship,
 depending upon size, complexity, and whether analog or digital interfaces or some combination thereof are utilized. Modifications to existing switchboards via Ship Alterations (SHIPALTs), SCDs, ORDALTs
 or FCs are quantified by kits or change packages rather than individual units. The number of kits required for different classes of ships are as follows: DDGs/CGs require three to four (3 - 4) per ship, LSDs
 require two (2) per ship, LPDs require one (1) per ship, MCMs require (3) per ship and LHDs require two (2) per ship. The magnitude of modifications also varies, as Cruiser Modernization is considerably more
 complex than the Amphibious Class hulls. Switchboard hardware is normally procured by the Invitation For Bids (IFB) process, from manufacturers on Qualified Products List (QPL)-17000. There are currently six
 companies listed on QPL-17000. All contracts awarded are competitive, fixed price.

 [P40A / ENG UPGRADES/MODS TO EQUIPMENT & TECH DOCUMENTATION]: Combat Systems & Interior Communication Switchboard Engineering and Modifications Upgrades to Equipment, Drawings,
 Technical Manuals (TMs) Allowance Parts Lists (APLs) and Allowance Equipage Lists (AELs). This line covers the costs to upgrade/modify existing equipment and associated technical documentation to
 implement and validate upgraded switching configurations essential for the ships' switchboards to properly integrate all elements of the Combat System and Interior Communication interfaces. The upgraded
 engineering modification drives the procurement of hardware modification kits (i.e., ORDALTs & Field Changes). These engineering modifications are essential to the functional deployment of Battle Force
 Interoperability.

 [P40A / ORDALT/FIELD CHANGE KITS]: Combat Systems & Interior Communication Switchboard Engineering and Modification Upgrade Procurements. This line covers the costs to implement and validate
 upgraded Procurement of ORDALT/Field Changes kits driven by upgraded engineering modification. These engineering modifications are essential to the functional deployment of Battle Force Interoperability.

 Description:
 The Landing Helicopter Assault (LHA) and Landing Helicopter Dock (LHD) Modernization Program is required to upgrade the in-service LHA 6 and LHD 1 Class ships in order to keep the LHA and LHD
 Class relevant and operational. The LHA and LHD Program is composed of a series of improvements in both the Hull, Mechanical, and Electrical (HM&E), Interior Communication, Cybersecurity and Aviation
 Integration systems. Some major modernization efforts include the procurement and installation of Brushless Generators, Electric Plant and Power Management Platform (PMP), Main Feed Pump replacement,
 and Propulsion and Auxiliary Control System (PACS), Ballast and DC Console, High Efficiency Super Capacity (HES-C) Chiller, and Modular Refrigeration System.

 The Brushless Generator replacement will reduce down time, total ownership costs, and maintenance costs associated with antiquated brush type generators. Main feed pump procurement and install replaces
 obsolete main feed pumps for the main propulsion boiler. Propulsion and Auxiliary Control System (PACS) provides a network infrastructure for HM&E systems.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request for LHA/LHD Midlife includes $86,884 thousand of discretionary and $36,500 thousand of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $123,384 thousand. The mandatory funds support AMPHIB
 modernization and LHD HM&E integration and availability procurements. Further information for this reconciliation request is provided in Section 200002 (Shipbuilding) of the Reconciliation Exhibit.

 FY 2026 funds provided for the modernization of Landing Helicopter Assault (LHA) and Landing Helicopter Dock (LHD) ships to include alteration procurements, installations and Design Services Allocation
 (DSA), continued Non Reoccurring Engineering (NRE) and engineering services. Discretionary funding increased by $5.282M from FY 2025 to FY 2026. Net increase mainly driven by increases in procurement
 quantity of Power Management Platform (Electric Plant Control), HES-C procurements and a decrease in installation of Propulsion and Auxiliary Control System (PACS).

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $13.131 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 Footnotes:
 (1)
    The FY 2026 decrease in funding is due to removing two availabilities in FY 2026 (USS IWO JIMA and USS BOXER) for HME conjunctive integration, modifications and testing. Conjunctive Alteration provides modernization modifications
 and integration to include conjunctive modernization growth Work and prorates of HM&E modifications to accommodate PEO IWS, NAVWAR, NAVAIR and other Program's Modernization during Shipyard Availabilities, equipment
 procurement, non-recurring and recurring engineering equipment integration and Integrated Logistics Support (ILS) products for LHA and LHD Class ships.
 (2)
    The FY 2026 increase supports the USS America Installation in FY 2026 and funds Advance Planning/Procurements for USS MAKIN ISLAND installation planned for FY 2027. The installation will implement a Defense-in-Depth Functional
 Implementation Architecture (DFIA) and meet the requirements of the standards released by the Information Assurance Technical Authority Board (IA TAB) across all the HM&E systems deploying the ability to protect, detect, react, and
 restore in a contested environment. The HM&E Condition System will monitor the signals on the HM&E network, both between HM&E systems and also those coming from other functional domains.
 (3)
    The FY 2026 funding increase supports Joint Strike Fighter Autonomic Logistics Information System (JSF ALIS) installations. JSF ALIS is the tactical aircraft logistics system focused on sustainment, together with economies of scale
 supporting a global supply chain system for aircraft. JSF ALIS is deploying with an Operational Data Integrated Network (ODIN) via On-Board Kit (OBK) rollout. The JSF ALIS ODIN is a form/fit/function install. Installs are to be performed by
 the In-Service Engineering Agent (ISEA). Installs are not limited to CNO availability schedules and may be performed within windows of opportunity (WOO).

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                    -                 -                  -



 Description:
 Machinery Control System (MCS) will replace the existing obsolete Versa Module Europa (VME) server with Windows XP operating system (O/S) as part of the ship construction award for LHD8 and LHA6. Both
 the hardware and software are no longer supported by industry and require a complete replacement. MCS will leverage existing infrastructure replacing internal hardware to the existing system's servers. LHD8,
 LHA 6-7 will receive a Versatile Performance Switching (VPX) server with Windows 10, or future O/S that provides improved bandwidth for control of the ship's 37 vital engineering systems, while maintain margin
 for new capabilities to be introduced. The new system provides room for growth and flexibility to easily incorporate emerging cyber security requirements that will help prevent adversarial threats.

1810N / 01 / 9                                                                              0933 / LHA/LHD Midlife                                                              2 / Power Management Platform (Electric
                                                                                                                                                                                Plant Control)
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready) :                                                                                  MDAP/MAIS Code:
                             Resource Summary                                         Prior Years                 FY 2024                   FY 2025                  FY 2026 Base       FY 2026 OOC       FY 2026 Total
Procurement Quantity (Units in Each)                                                                    -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -
Gross/Weapon System Cost ($ in Millions)                                                            33.073                    24.507                     1.941                 12.394             0.000             12.394
Less PY Advance Procurement ($ in Millions)                                                             -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -
Net Procurement (P-1) ($ in Millions)                                                               33.073                    24.507                     1.941                 12.394             0.000             12.394
Plus CY Advance Procurement ($ in Millions)                                                             -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -
Total Obligation Authority ($ in Millions)                                                          33.073                    24.507                     1.941                 12.394             0.000             12.394
                            (The following Resource Summary rows are for informational purposes only. The corresponding budget requests are documented elsewhere.)
Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -



 Description:
 Description: Power Management Platform (PMP) modernization converts legacy analog controls to a digital automated control system. This system gathers and combines all Electric plant data that can be
 monitored at different control stations throughout the engineering plant ensuring redundancy of the system. PMP simplifies multiple control sequences into single step operations, making possible new control
 capabilities that were too cumbersome for operators to perform. PMP implements a gradual Ship Service Turbine Generator (SSTG) turbine startup/shutdown sequence which allows the turbine to achieve
 stabilized temperatures more consistently, reducing operational stress on components and improves reliability and operational availability of the system. PMP automation will reduce electrical system transients
 and stress on breaker components by employing a Zero Power Transfer (ZPT) strategy, which limits the current flow through an individual breaker to zero during open/close evolutions. During PMP installation
 obsolete switchboard breakers will be replaced with Commercial Off-the-Shelf (COTS) Whipp and Bourne circuit breakers that will improve navy total ownership by increasing commonality across the fleet and
 reducing preventative and corrective maintenance.

 Footnotes:
 (5)
        The FY 2026 funding increase supports an installation in FY 2028.
 (6)
    Installation funding for a ship is divided over a three-year period. Installation execution funding in the year of availability, and Design Service Allocation (DSA) two years prior to availability, and DSA in the
 year prior to the availability. This includes ship checks, ship installation drawing (SID) development, logistic document development and long lead time material procurements (LLTM). Installation costs across the
 FYDP may vary depending on current port rates. Port rates are updated every year by a CNRMC letter based on the forecasted port loading model. Port loading rate forecasts are the rates recommended for all
 budgeted maintenance and organic modernization within a specific port. Individual ship's availability durations vary dependent upon the integration of ship's maintenance and modernization work packages.

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                    -                 -                  -



 Description:
 The Brushless Generator replacement will reduce down time, total ownership costs, and maintenance costs associated with antiquated brush type generators.

 Brushless Generator Upgrade replaces five (5) brush-type Ship's Service Turbine Generators on LHDs that are plagued by severe reliability, maintenance and obsolescence problems. Brushless Generator
 Upgrade will reduce both Ship's Force and depot level maintenance requirements and increase the electric plant Operational Availability (Ao) on LHD class ships.

 Description:
 Main Feed Pump (MFP) replacement addresses system obsolescence by installing an industry supported, steam driven pump with a new electronic automatic boiler control that will prevent over-speed
 conditions, which is a major cause of MFP failures. The new pump will improve reliability, operational availability and bring commonality with other ship classes.
 Engineering Services required in year prior to install.

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                    -                 -                  -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                    -                 -                  -



 Description:
 (P40A) SCS-GOV (LHD7/8, LHA 6/7): The Ship Control System-Government (SCS-GOV) supports the standardization of hardware, software and design of the surface ship Ship Control Systems (SCS) to
 address hardware and software obsolescence issues. The installation will bring commonality across the LHD 1-8 and LHA-6 hulls. SCS-GOV replaces the analog Ship Control System (SCS) with a digital SCS.
 Analog systems are very maintenance intensive constantly requiring alignment of the amplifiers, indicators, and other transmitting devices. The existing system is also largely obsolete requiring cannibalization
 from decommissioned ships.

 Description:
 The High Efficiency Super Capacity (HES-C) Chiller is a new start for FY26.

 The High Efficiency Super Capacity (HES-C) Chiller increases the cooling capacity onboard LHD class ships to regain cooling margin to support critical loads while reducing mechanical complexity making it
 lighter, more efficient and improving operational availability. Increased cooling capacity is required for critical combat system operations.

 Each procurement quantity consists of three HES-C Chillers required for installation in a single space onboard LHDs.

 Engineering Services support development and delivery of logistics support products, training support, etc. for the HES-C system.

 Footnotes:
 (10)
     The FY 2026 funding increase is due to the first year of procurement. It funds the procurement of 3 ship sets and Non-reoccurring Engineering (NRE). Installation funding for a ship is divided over a three-
 year period. Installation execution funding in the year of availability, and Design Service Allocation (DSA) two years prior to availability, and DSA in the year prior to the availability. This includes ship checks, ship
 installation drawing (SID) development, logistic document development and long lead time material procurements (LLTM). Installation costs across the FYDP may vary depending on current port rates. Port rates
 are updated every year by a CNRMC letter based on the forecasted port loading model. Port loading rate forecasts are the rates recommended for all budgeted maintenance and organic modernization within a
 specific port. Individual ship's availability durations vary dependent upon the integration of ship's maintenance and modernization work packages.

 Description:
 Hierarchical Yet Dynamically Reprogrammable Architecture (HYDRA) modernization replaces the unsupported P7100/MRK fixed and portable radios with new XG-75 radios and Radiating Transmission Lines
 (RTL) that incorporates industry standard Association of Public Safety Communications Official (APCO) P-25 signaling protocols. The new system will maintain vital, below deck, wireless communications that
 enable ship's force to coordinate amphibious, aviation, force protection and damage control operations safely. HYDRA modernization will establish commonality across CG, CVN, LPD and LSD Ship Classes.

 Footnotes:
 (12)
      The FY 2026 decrease is due to cancellation of a CNO schedule change and decrease of a shipset procurement. Unit purchased in FY 2024 could not be installed in FY 2026 due to cancellation of the LHD 2
 FY 2026 Selected Restricted Availability (SRA). Prior year cost are sunk cost. Installation funding for a ship is divided over a three-year period. Installation execution funding in the year of availability, and Design
 Service Allocation (DSA) two years prior to availability, and DSA in the year prior to the availability. This includes ship checks, ship installation drawing (SID) development, logistic document development and
 long lead time material procurements (LLTM). Installation costs across the FYDP may vary depending on current port rates. Port rates are updated every year by a CNRMC letter based on the forecasted port
 loading model. Port loading rate forecasts are the rates recommended for all budgeted maintenance and organic modernization within a specific port. Individual ship's availability durations vary dependent upon
 the integration of ship's maintenance and modernization work packages.

 Description:

 Classes during midlife modernization. Existing announcing systems are riddled with obsolescence that degrades performance and presents a safety concern, during shipboard operations and casualties. This is
 a capability required in the LHD Required Operational Capabilities (ROC) document.

 Description:
 Integrated Communication Terminals (ICT) upgrade replaces the antiquated system with a Commercial off-the-Shelf digital system that improves both internal and external communications with the ship
 necessary to meeting USMC requirements for executing operations. The system incorporates hardware installed on the LCS, CVN-78 and CVN-68 classes, reducing supportability and training requirements for
 the fleet. This upgrade is necessary for Digital Conference Switch System (DCSS) and Integrated Voice Network System upgrades to be implemented.

 Description:

 tactical employment of forces. The new system will bring LHDs into alignment with LPD, LSD and LCC modernization.

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                               -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -             -
Flyaway Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                             -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -             -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -             -


 Description:
 The FY 2026 budget provides funding for Extended Service Life Program (ESLP) targeted modernization of various Hull, Mechanical and Electrical (HM&E) systems onboard the Amphibious Command Ship
 (LCC) 19 hull. The major modernization efforts include Ship Service/Emergency Diesel Generator Upgrades (SS/EDG).

 [P40A / SS/EDG Genset Upgrade]: The Ship Service Generator upgrades replace the #1SS/EDG and #3SSDG with a 2000KW unit, similar to the existing #3SSDG but shock qualified, and accomplishes
 necessary distribution modifications. This replacement adds an additional 1425KW to the Diesel generating plant and provides the additional capacity to provide ample power to support full ship's functions/C4I
 capabilities on a continuous basis. The design also includes the installation of dual redundant voltage regulators.

 [P40A / IVN Upgrade]: Integrated Voice Network (IVN) upgrade replaces the legacy Ship Service Telephone System with ruggedized communications system that provides command and control communications
 for tactical employment of forces. The new system will bring LCC 19 into alignment with LPD, LSD, and LHD modernization.

 [P40A / General Announcing System Upgrade]: The General Announcing System upgrade replaces currently installed 1/3/6 MC Announcing Systems with the AN/SIA-129 Digital Integrated Announcing System
 to provide a technology refresh and enhancement for the 1/3/6 MC one-way announcing systems.

 [P40A / 21/24MC CAPT/FLAG CMD Intercom Replacement]: The 21/24 MC CAPT/FLAG CMD Intercom upgrade removes and replaces the existing obsolete and unreliable 23MC and 26MC intercom units with
 a Commercial Off The Shelf (COTS) solution.

 [P40A / Ship Control System (SCS) Modernization]: The Ship Control System upgrade will replace the existing, analog ship control equipment in the Pilot House, Steering Gear Room and Engine Order Station
 with digital equivalents. This change is being driven primarily to address obsolescence issues but will also provide additional redundancy, accuracy, capability, alarm management and will reduce maintenance
 workload for ship's force.

 [P40A / Pilot House Redesign]: The Pilothouse Redesign upgrade replaces existing Bridge layout with improved navigational operations and watchstander capability.

 [P40A / LCC 19 ESLP]: The ESLP modernization package upgrades or replaces existing Degaussing, Automation Controllers, Automatic and Manual Bus Transfer Switches/Breakers, Water Heater,
 Desalinators, Boiler Water Level Indicators, Main Feed Pumps, Programmatic Logic Console, Lube Oil Control and Steam Plant Control for safety, elimination of obsolescence challenges, enhanced operational
 reliability, improved watchstander capability, and improvement of systems and equipment to meet end of service life.

 Justification:
 The increase in funding from FY 2025 to FY 2026 supports installation of the Ship Service/Emergency Diesel Generator Upgrade alteration planned for the LCC 19 Docking Selected Restricted Availability.

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $2.809 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 Footnotes:
 (1)
    Engineering services costs prior to first install are due to non-recurring engineering costs, which include first article development and environmental quality testing. Also included are software development and system engineering milestone
 reviews and cyber security hardening under the Risk Management Framework. Installation funding for a ship is divided over a three-year period. Installation execution funding in the year of availability, Design Service Allocation (DSA) two
 years prior to availability, and DSA in the year prior to the availability. This includes ship checks, ship installation drawing (SID) development, logistic documentation development, and long lead time material (LLTM) procurements. Installation
 costs across the FYDP vary depending on current port rates. Port rates are updated every year by the Commander, Navy Regional Maintenance Center (CNRMC) based on the forecasted port loading model. Port loading rate forecasts are
 the rates recommended for all budgeted maintenance and organic modernization within a specific port. Individual ship's availability durations vary dependent upon the integration of ship's maintenance and modernization work packages.
 Engineering services are higher due to labor requirements for system assembly prior to installation.

 Description:
 [Integrated Bridge Display System (IBDS)] The Integrated Bridge Display System (IBDS) upgrade is a Government-developed replacement for existing digital and analog ship control/navigation information. It
 consolidates variety of information provided to the pilothouse to common displays in various spaces on U.S. Navy ships. The IBDS also provides opportunities to improve configuration management, logistic
 supportability, and obsolescence issues endemic to the fleet.

 [SS/EDG Genset Upgrade] The Ship Service Generator upgrades replace the #1SS/EDG and #3SSDG with a 2000KW unit, similar to the existing #3SSDG but shock qualified, and accomplishes necessary
 distribution modifications. This replacement will add an additional 1425KW to the Diesel generating plant and will provide the additional capacity to ensure there is ample power to support full ship's functions/C4I
 capabilities on a continuous basis. The design will also include the installation of dual redundant voltage regulators.

 Description:
 POLLUTION CONTROL SYSTEMS/EQUIPMENT (PCE) PROGRAM
 Funds for the PCE Program are used to procure, install, and modernize Navy shipboard environmental equipment, systems, and technologies. The PCE Program ensures that Navy ships comply with applicable
 legal and statutory requirements (i.e., Federal laws and statutes, international regulations, and Department of Defense (DoD) and Navy instructions) governing environmental protection and shipboard discharge
 requirements. These requirements collectively restrict overboard discharge of oily wastewater, sewage, solid waste, plastic waste, and hazardous waste. Failure to comply risks potential personal, civil, and
 criminal liability; and can significantly impose constraints on the operational capabilities of Navy ships. Funding specifically supports procurements and installations for the Oil Pollution Abatement (OPA)
 Improvements program, the Dry Waste Processing Suites (DWPS) Improvements program, and the Hazardous Material Stowage Modernization program.

 SHORE BASED POLLUTION EQUIPMENT - (Logistics)
 The shore-based funds provide for equipment required to clean up Navy oil spills in the near shore to open sea environment and in areas distant from fixed bases as required by the Federal Water Pollution
 Control Act - Public Law 92-500, 33CFR154-155. The law created a National Oil and Hazardous Substance Pollution Contingency Plan and designates the Department of Defense as one of the primary agencies
 responsible for promotion of effective operation of the plan. The Supervisor of Salvage is assigned the responsibility to provide technical expertise, resources, and equipment for response to Navy-originated
 spills of oil and other hazardous material in remote areas, coastal waters and/or the open sea. Major items of procurement include the following:

 [P40A / HF067 VESSEL SKIMMER SYSTEMS]: These systems are self-propelled skimmers designed for near-shore and low sea-state open ocean environments and are used in conjunction with oil containment
 boom for mechanical recovery of oil on the water. They are designed to be rapidly transportable to the scene of an oil spill.

 [P40A / HF068 UNDERWATER OIL RECOVERY SYSTEM]: A rapidly deployable system that provides grinders, sampling tools, sensors, cutters, connections, pumps, hoses, and hydraulic tools to locate,
 sample, penetrate, and offload tanks below the water.

 [P40A / HF069 42" BOOM REPLACEMENT SECTIONS]: Ocean Booming Systems (2000FT each) and Marco self-propelled skimming systems (600FT each) use standardized high buoyancy, high flexibility
 NAVY 42HB inflatable boom purchased in 55FT sections to provide phased replacement of rubber boom damaged or worn beyond economical repair. The other components of these systems have a longer
 useful life and need replacement at much longer intervals.




LI 0935 - Pollution Control Equipment                                                                   UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                    Volume 1 - 285
Navy                                                                                                           Page 1 of 20                                  P-1 Line #10
                                                                                                 UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-40, Budget Line Item Justification: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                            Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                         P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 01: Ships Support Equipment / BSA 9: Other 0935 / Pollution Control Equipment
Shipboard Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                         Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                    Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 [P40A / HF077 RUBBER BOAT REPLACEMENT]: Various spill response systems for ship salvage in harbor and near shore environments contain rubber boats to place and maintain skimmers, boom and
 anchors. These boats and motors are reaching the end of service life and require replacement.

 [P40A / HF078 SALVAGE SKIMMER SYSTEMS SERVICE LIFE EXTENSION]: These systems are a collection of small, special-purpose skimmers, containment boom, transfer pumps, storage tanks, sorbents,
 and ancillary equipment intended as a stand-alone response package for small, salvage-related spills inside and adjacent to ships. This replaces worn out and obsolete components.

 [P40A / HF079 ARTIC OIL/HEAVY DEBRIS RECOVERY SYSTEM SERVICE LIFE EXTENSION]: This system is designed to recover oil in an arctic and/or heavy debris environment where specific weather
 conditions render normal skimmer recovery methods useless. This replaces worn out and obsolete components.

 [P40A / HF081 COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS]: Provide Command Vans, Trailers and Portable Command Centers with improved communications and web access essential for coordination of dispersed
 operations.

 [P40A / HF082 VISCOUS OIL PUMPING SYSTEM IMPROVEMENT]: These provide specialized pumps and water injection to pump very viscous fluids. Large pumps are needed to move large quantities of oil
 to lighter a stricken ship. Additionally, oil that weathers, emulsifies, or mixes with other contaminants will become thick and viscous to the point that regular centrifugal pumping systems will not move the oil. This
 upgrades capabilities of existing systems.

 [P40A / HF084 - HYDRAULIC SUBMERSIBLE PUMPS]: Hydraulic submersible pumps for lightering oil or dewatering. These cylindrically shaped pumps are capable of fitting down a butterworth fitting to remove
 oil from tanks on damaged or grounded ships. A "Butterworth" fitting is a trade name for a fitting that connects to high pressure tank cleaning hoses. Butterworth is a manufacturer (butterworth.com) and their
 fittings are an industry standard for this application.

 [P40A / HF086 - FLEET EXERCISE HOT TAP TRAINING KITS]: The hot tap training kit provides a complete kit for training, including communications, test plates, hull mock-ups, fasteners, rigging, and multiple
 cutting systems, flange systems, and pumping systems.

 [P40A / HF087 - LIGHT OIL RECOVERY CLASS 5 SKIMMER INSERTS]: Disk skimmer inserts and ancillary support and installation equipment for upgrading modular and standard skimmer systems. Includes,
 skimmer, disks, hoses, flanges, valves, handling hardware, and safety hardware.

 [P40A / HF091 - VESSEL OF OPPORTUNITY (VOSS) SHIP ADAPTER MOUNTING KITS]: Vessel of Opportunity (VOSS) ship adapter mounting kits.

 [P40A / HF092 - VAN REFURBISHMENT]: For the new purchase or refurbishment of shipping containers to be used as equipment system vans. Includes vans, transportation, flooring, rollers, electrical
 components, doors, structural elements, gaskets, paint, engineering, and design.

 [P40A / HF093 - LOGISTICS SUPPORT EQUIPMENT]: Logistics Support Equipment to include weight handling support systems (forks, rigging, extensions, stabilizers, etc).

 [P40A / HF094 - SKIMMER SUPPORT EQUIPMENT]: Skimmer Support Equipment to include equipment boxes and containers, engineering, and materials for the procurement, integration, and maintenance of
 skimmer support systems, including boat racks, rollers, handling equipment, pumps, hoses, valves, piping, hydraulics, and electrical controls.

 [P40A / HF095 - FIELD LIGHTING]: Field lighting equipment to include tower structures, stands, electrical wiring and controls, bulbs, reflectors, shields, switches, boxes, skids, carts, rigging, and tools.

 [P40A / HF096 TRANSFER BLADDERS 10K GALLON]: Towable transfer 10,000 gallon bladder system requires replacement to nose hard piping and hose connection to improve handling at speeds above 3
 knots and to accommodate 4 inch hose from skimmer design improvements.

 [P40A / HF097 MINI VESSEL OF OPPORTUNITY (VOSS) SYSTEM]: Containerized smaller skimming system that can be deployed rapidly and operated from a smaller Vessel of Opportunity.
