Shipboard Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                  Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A

 [P40A / HF098 - NOFI VESSEL OF OPPORTUNITY (VOSS) SYSTEM]: Vessel of Opportunity (VOSS) offshore skimming system soft rubber and fabric components are reaching the end of their service life.
 These are provided by only one vendor, NOFI, and are the Navy's only offshore systems. The hard components (reels, vans, tooling etc) will be retained and reused.

 [P40A / HF830 PRODUCTION ENGINEERING]: Air conditioning plant kit and software quality control, technical manual and complete conversion instruction revisions, technical reports based on investigative
 efforts of Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) quality assurance or obsolescence issues, and support equipment removal from inactive ships or other Navy assets. Development of technical manuals,
 Planned Maintenance System (PMS), Provisioning Technical Documentation (PTD), Program Support Data (PSD) and allowance Parts List (APLs), engineering to manage procurement and material, and in
 support of design reviews.

 [P3A / HF024 CFC-114 (R-114) AC CONVERSION]: The production of chlorofluorocarbon (CFC)-based refrigerants (including CFC-12 and CFC R-114) was prohibited after 31 DEC 95 by the Clean Air Act of
 1990. Presidential Executive Order 13693 calls for federal agencies to "promote sustainable acquisition and procurement by ensuring that each of the following environmental performance and sustainability
 factors are included [...] alternatives to ozone-depleting substances". The Navy is currently dependent on CFC-based refrigerants for the mission-critical cooling of (1) vital electronics and weapon systems, (2)
 food and medical stowage, and (3) inhabited spaces aboard surface ships and submarines. To counter the immediate threat of production cessation on uninterrupted Fleet operations, DoD directed the Defense
 Logistics Agency to establish a stockpile of CFC-based refrigerants. The stockpile was sized to support Fleet operations until the last CFC-based systems are retired or converted to ozone-friendly refrigerants.
 This program procures and installs conversion kits on existing CFC R-114 Air-Conditioning (A/C) plants onboard surface ships. The inventory objective for CFC R-114 is 402 converted units.

 [P3A - 2 / HF031 OPA IMPROVEMENTS]: Existing OPA systems have demonstrated poor operational availability, maintainability, and reliability. This comprehensive effort addresses the entire OPA system,
 from holding tanks to overboard discharge, leveraging recommended improvements new to the Fleet and approved, partially completed alterations. The alterations needed to correct design deficiencies are ship
 specific and require ship checks to confirm the current configuration as prior alterations may have completed one or several of the recommended improvements. The alterations may include but are not limited
 to: installation of new tank level sensors; upgrades to the Oily Water Separator (OWS) and the Oil Content Monitor (OCM), correction of OWS suction problems, correction of OCM inlet & outlet piping including
 related of flush water piping configuration problems, and upgrade of oily waste transfer pump relief valve. Extensive work will occur with LHD-1 Class upgrades to convert the existing OWS to the VS-50 OWS
 and DDG-51 through DDG-88 ships will receive the Oily Waste Membrane System to produce an effluent that meets the 15 ppm maximum oil discharge requirement.

 [P3A - 2 / HF031 Oil Pollution Abatement (OPA) Improvements]: HF5IN - INSTALLATION OF EQUIPMENT - EXPEDITIONARY WARFARE Funding is for installation of equipment for the Fleet Modernization
 Program installations.
 HF6IN - INSTALLATION OF EQUIPMENT - SURFACE WARFARE Funding is for installation of equipment for the Fleet Modernization Program installations

 [P3A - 3 / HF032 DRY WASTE PROCESSING SUITE IMPROVEMENTS]: The Fleet is challenged to keep solid waste equipment operating and sanitary while maintaining compliance with the Act to Prevent
 Pollution from Ships (APPS). Legacy shipboard solid waste equipment systems are experiencing decreasing reliability, maintainability, and availability (RMA) due to the following issues:

 - Equipment service life issues and risks: Legacy equipment have been in service for more than 10 to 20 years and are at risk of reaching the end of their service life, reduced RMA, and experiencing parts
 availability and obsolescence issues.
 - Increasing quantity of shipboard plastic waste: The quantity of shipboard plastic waste has significantly increased by more than 50%, which risks overwhelming the processing capacity of legacy equipment.
 - Increasing Fleet population at sea: The increased population of men and women at sea has increased sanitary and hygiene-related wastes that cause unique treatment challenges for legacy shipboard solid
 waste systems equipment.
 - Challenges managing increasing hazardous material (HM) inventory at sea: The Fleet is experiencing issues with storing, controlling, and managing HM inventory due to increased depth and range of HM,
 longer missions, greater array of equipment, and increased operational tempo. Improperly stored HM presents personnel, safety, environmental, and equipment risks to the Fleet at sea.

 The Dry Waste Processing Suites (DWPS) Improvements Program will improve RMA and reduce obsolescence, sanitation, and safety issues by modernizing legacy shipboard solid waste systems. The DWPS
 Improvements Program will decrease labor-hours by improving operation and maintenance, increase system availability, address current and projected obsolescence issues, and decrease component failures by
 redesign and equipment optimization. The improvement of legacy solid waste equipment, and transition and implementation of new solid waste processing tools will ensure the safe and efficient management of
 solid waste and HM.

 impacting variations in costs due to improvements on different ship classes, location of availability and the pollution prevention equipment installed within suite that is modernized.

 Footnotes:
 (1)
   These systems are a collection of small, special-purpose skimmers, containment boom, transfer pumps, storage tanks, sorbents, and ancillary equipment intended as a stand-alone response package for small, salvage-related spills inside
 and adjacent to ships. This replaces worn out and obsolete components.
 (2)
       Higher viscosity fluids require more energy to pump due to increased resistance to flow. As a result, the power requirements for the pump will increase, leading to higher energy consumption and operational costs.
 (3)
   A "Butterworth" fitting is a trade name for a fitting that connects to high pressure tank cleaning hoses. Butterworth is a manufacturer (butterworth.com) and their fittings are an industry standard for this application. The program specifically
 uses these for Hot Tap missions, where it is drill into sunken ships and empty their tanks of any existing oil or fuel.
 (4)
    The NOFI Current Buster is a high-speed open ocean skimmer that is a Vessel of Opportunity Skimming System which is made to be attached to the side of a suitable vessel and then driven along to collect oil. The program currently does
 this with the Coast Guard Juniper Class Buoy Tenders, where the program uses adapter mounting kits to connect to the existing bulkhead hole patterns in those ships for their previous SORS skimming systems. Each USCG Buoy Handler
 has a different hole pattern, and it has to be redesigned with mounting plates over time to capture these. Increase is due to moving towards making mounting plates for the smaller USCG Keeper Class tenders as well as the new Navy T-ATS
 class ships coming online.
 (5)
       This is for the upkeep, replacement, and annual certification of weight handling equipment necessary to move, load, and position pollution systems (forks, rigging, extensions, stabilizers, etc).

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -



 Description:
 The Navy is currently dependent on CFC-based refrigerants for the mission-critical cooling of (1) vital electronics and weapon systems, (2) food and medical stowage, and (3) inhabited spaces aboard surface
 ships and submarines. To counter the immediate threat of production cessation on uninterrupted Fleet operations, DoD directed the Defense Logistics Agency to establish a stockpile of CFC-based refrigerants.
 The stockpile was sized to support Fleet operations until the last CFC-based systems are retired or converted to ozone-friendly refrigerants. This program procures and installs conversion kits on existing CFC
 R-114 Air-Conditioning (A/C) plants onboard surface ships. The inventory objective for CFC R-114 is 402 converted units.

                            (The following Resource Summary rows are for informational purposes only. The corresponding budget requests are documented elsewhere.)
Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                    -                 -                   -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                    -                 -                   -



 Description:
 OPA Systems Improvements - Expeditionary Warfare: These installations resolve issues with existing OPA systems including poor operational availability, maintainability, and reliability. Variations in costs are
 subject to specific ship needs and are confirmed based upon ship checks. Shipset install/hardware buys may include: VS-50 oily water separators, secondary membrane treatment system, oil content monitor
 datalogger(s), multiple pumps, valves, valve actuators, sensors, switches, placards, and hoses.

 OPA Systems Improvements - Surface Warfare: These installations resolve issues with existing OPA systems including poor operational availability, maintainability, and reliability. Variations in costs are
 subject to specific ship needs and are confirmed based upon ship checks. Shipset install/hardware buys may include: oily water transfer systems, secondary membrane treatment system, oil content monitor
 datalogger(s), multiple pumps, valves, valve actuators, sensors, switches, placards, and hoses.

 [HF031 Oil Pollution Abatement (OPA) Improvements] Unit cost fluctuations for OPA improvements are attributed to the following key factors:

 1. Scope of Hardware Suite
 Costs vary depending on whether the ship receives a full OPA system suite or select components only (e.g., Oil Content Monitors (OCMs) or Oil Water Separators (OWSs)).

 2. Platform Type
 Installations are conducted across different ship classes, including both Expeditionary and Surface Warfare vessels, contributing to variability in integration complexity and associated costs.

 3. Installation Location
 Costs are influenced by the geographical location of the ship's availability period. Installations within the Continental United States (CONUS) differ from those conducted Outside CONUS (OCONUS). Among
 CONUS shipyards, further cost variations exist between East Coast and West Coast facilities due to labor rates, logistics, and local infrastructure.

 4. Technology Configuration by Hull Class
 Beginning in FY26, OPA upgrades for Arleigh Burke-class Destroyers (DDG Flight I, II, and IIA) incorporate centrifugal OWS technology for selected ships, while others retain legacy membrane OWS systems,
 resulting in cost differentials.

 Description:
 Dry Waste Processing Suite Improvements - Expeditionary Warfare: Funding provided to support dry waste handling equipment improvements on LHA, LHD, LPD and LSD platforms including compressed melt
 units, pulpers, shredders, and compactors.

 Dry Waste Processing Suite Improvements - Surface Warfare: Funding provided to support dry waste handling equipment improvements on CG, LCS and DDG platforms including compressed melt units,
 pulpers, shredders, and compactors.

 Dry Waste Processing Suite Improvements - Air Warfare: Funding provided to support dry waste handling equipment improvements on CVN platforms including compressed melt units, pulpers, shredders,
 incinerators, and compactors.

 [Dry Waste Processing Suite Improvements] 1. Hardware Configuration and Platform Diversity
 Unit cost and quantity fluctuations beginning in FY25 are driven by differing combinations of Solid Waste system modifications namely incinerators and/or Compressed Melt Unit (CMU) upgrades across a range
 of ship classes, including destroyers, amphibious ships, and aircraft carriers. Additional variation arises from partial or staggered system installations.

 Description:
 This line supports modernization and obsolete equipment replacement for SSNs, recapitalization of militarily critical silencing ranges, modifications and upgrades to weapons launch equipment, upgrades &
 refurbishment of Submarine Special Operating Forces (SOF) equipment and Dry Deck Shelters (DDS), and the VIRGINIA class Acoustic Superiority Backfit program.

 The increase in funding from FY 2025 to FY 2026 is due to findings from the SIB-25 study that resulted in the addition of funding South Tongue of the Ocean Acoustic Facility (STAFAC) Acoustic Measurement
 Facilities Improvement Program (AMFIP) Revised Estimate at Completion, Southeast Alaska Facility (SEAFAC) Life Extension efforts, Pacific Deep Electromagnetic Research Measurement Array (PACDERMA)
 facility construction, the SSN CL - Spectroscopic Total Air Monitor, and the VIRGINIA Class Fire Detection System Backfit. The increase in funding for the VIRGINIA Class Fire Detection System Backfit is
 transitioning from design and development and first-time procurement in FY25 to second procurement and first-time installation in FY26. The increase in funding from FY 2025 to FY 2026 is due to the AUKUS
 Submarine Rotational Forces West (SRF-W) funding to support Ashore C4I Integration, design and installation of C4I systems which will directly contribute to achieving SRF-W IOC and FOC milestones.

 This line funds procurement and installation of VACL Acoustic Superiority machinery modifications. The second component of the AS program, the LVA sonar, is funded under Line Item 2150.

 The SSN Class Component Upgrades sub-line provides for equipment technical refresh and upgrades that consist of hardware, software, system engineering, integrated logistics support, system test and
 evaluation, training, data, alteration installation teams and program management. The majority of these upgrades address obsolesces issues that result in unsupportable hardware or provide for improved
 reliability of systems that have proven to have inadequate reliability in service.

 The Facilities/Lab Upgrades sub-line procures equipment and material used to implement, sustain and modernize the military high priority Submarine Signature Programs. These spares procurements, technical
 refreshes and equipment upgrades are key to measuring submarine signatures and sustaining Fleet stealth. These ranges and measurement systems are not funded by other NAVSEA program offices. The
 program supports all classes of nuclear submarine (SSN/SSBN/SSGN), and associated land-based laboratories/facilities.

 The Weapons Handling Systems sub-line fund modifications and improvements to SSN 688, SSN 774 and SSN 21 class submarine weapons launching systems, stowage and shipping systems, torpedo tube
 system components and torpedo tube test equipment. These modifications are required to address reliability, maintainability, functionality and safety modifications and/or tactical improvements resulting from
 operational use and experience. This funding also provides management services required for installations.




LI 0941 - Submarine Support Equipment                                                                   UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                    Volume 1 - 305
Navy                                                                                                           Page 1 of 18                                  P-1 Line #11
                                                                                               UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-40, Budget Line Item Justification: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                        Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                         P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 01: Ships Support Equipment / BSA 9: Other 0941 / Submarine Support Equipment
Shipboard Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                  Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 The VA Class SOF sub-line procures and installs unique system components required for SOF operations and support on designated SOF VIRGINIA class submarines. Starting in FY24, this PB009 sub-line
 includes funding for Dry Deck Shelters Modernization that was previously included under the SSN Class Component Upgrades (PB002) sub-line in FY22 and FY23.

 AUKUS Submarine Rotational Forces West (SRF-W) sub-line procures equipment necessary for C4I Support of units and organizations in Australia. Equipment covers all necessary IT services (NIPR, SIPR,
 NNPI, SYLAN, SECNET, TRANET, Telephony, etc.). These systems are all essential to establishing normal operations for US units in Australia. Costs are expected to be one time procurement costs and totals
 are based on similar recent installations.

 [P40A / SSTG Digital Voltage]: SSTG Digital Voltage Regulator upgrade (SHIPALT 4884K) - Upgrades SSN-774 - 779 SSTG voltage regulators. Existing SSTGs on early VIRGINIA Class submarines use analog
 voltage regulators, which are obsolete and unsustainable. S/A 4884K upgrades these ships to the later style Digital Voltage Regulator which is supportable. SSN 780 and later ships were delivered with the new
 design Digital Voltage Regulator.

 [P40A / Integrated Communications System (ICS) Block V ShipAlt]: Upgrades Block I/II VIRGINIA class ICS systems to the Block V system (SHIPALT 5061K). The ICS system provides integrated interior
 communication and alarms functions throughout the submarine. This system is required for safe operation of the ship. The ICS system on these ships is a COTS based system designed in the mid-1990s and
 first installed in 2002. Much of the design is now obsolete and unsupported. ICS must be operational to meet underway requirements. This alteration addresses obsolescence of key components in the legacy
 systems by upgrading the entire system to the current generation Block V system.

 [P40A / DGIOTS Shock Modification]: Diesel Generator Independent Over Speed Trip System (DGIOTS) Shock Modification (SHIPALT 4778K) - The DGIOTS control panel on VIRGINIA Class Block I/II has
 obsolete components and fails to meet requirements for shock and vibration. SHIPALT 4778K provides a shock/vibration-qualified control panel using current, supportable technology.

 [P40A / Fwd Automatic Bus Transfer (ABT) Power Modification]: Modifies Block I/II VIRGINIA class forward ABTs to improve system capability to support critical electronic systems during a loss of power.
 (SHIPALT 4882K).

 [P40A / Manual Remote Operator (MRO) Shock Mod]: VIRGINIA class MRO Shock Modification (SHIPALT 4620K) - Accomplishes design changes on 20 MROs per ship to meet shock qualification requirement.
 Required on SSN 774-781; later ships received the alteration during new construction.

 [P40A / Circuit D Upgrade]: Funds development, procurement and installation of SHIPALT 5062K to upgrade the Circuit D system on Block I/II VIRGINIA ships to the current design Block V system. The Circuit D
 system supports degaussing underway and is a critical system that is obsolete in Block I/II VIRGINIA class submarines and require modernization to allow continued support. Additionally, this system requires an
 interface with shipboard navigation data available through the SWFTS system. Future upgrade of the VA Class SWFTS sonar/fire control systems to TI-24/APB-23 or greater involve a change of middleware from
 CORBA to AMPQ protocols, which requires SHIPALT development and implementation for these two systems to support continued operation with the new SWFTS communication protocol.

 [P40A / SSTG Reliability Upgrades]: Funds a variety of SHIPALT efforts to improve VACL SSTG reliability.

 [P40A / Portable Ship Control Unit Upgrades]: The portable ship control unit provides navigational information, ship status and a communication systems to bridge watchstanders during surfaced operations.
 Effective and reliable functionality is critical for safe operation of the submarine. Changes in navigation data protocol associated with certain combat system upgrades (SWFTS installations) require updates to the
 portable ship control system for continued operation. This line procures and installs updated systems to allow continued function and improve reliability.

 [P40A / Low Pressure Electrolyzer (LPE) Upgrade]: Procures & installs SHIPALT 5031K on selected SSN-688 class ships to replace legacy Electrolytic Oxygen Generator (EOG) system with a modern Low
 Pressure Electrolyzer (LPE). A reliable oxygen generation system is critical to support crew health and submarine stealth (inoperable oxygen generators are likely to greatly increase the frequency and time the
 ship must operate at periscope depth where she is most vulnerable to detection and collision). The LPE produces oxygen at low pressure, eliminating the need for high pressure oxygen storage which greatly
 improves safety and reliability as well as decreasing maintenance cost. Procurements are driven by the SSN-688 Class maintenance period schedule as replacement of the EOG with the LPE will be done during
 CNO availability due to the time to complete the installation. Production engineering efforts in this line support the development and qualification of modernization efforts to support long term sustainment of in
 service LPE systems.

1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 01: Ships Support Equipment / BSA 9: Other 0941 / Submarine Support Equipment
Shipboard Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                   Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A

 [P40A / Central Air Monitoring System (CAMS) Modernization]: The CAMS system is critical to allow monitoring of atmospheric conditions throughout the submarine while underway, and hence critical to crew
 health & safety. The existing system suffers from multiple parts that are obsolete. This line funds efforts to develop a back fit SHIPALT for all classes of submarines (SSN 774, SSN 688, SSN21, SSBN 726).
 Procurements beginning in FY26 to be installed during Intermediate Level Availabilities (CMAVs/TOAs).

 [P40A / SSN HM&E System Sustainment]: Funds development and implementation of various minor critical alterations to in-service SSN HM&E systems. This funding line differs from the PTROCS line in that it
 supports modernization of hardware items such as lifelines, hatch clips, pumps, etc. whereas PTROCS supports modernization driven by obsolescence of COTS based control systems.

 [P40A / VA Class Machinery Control System Modernization]: VIRGINIA class submarines use COTS based systems for control of the main propulsion machinery and turbine generators. Reliable operation of
 these systems is critical to safe underway operation of the submarine. This line procures and installs a number of alterations to mitigate obsolescence of these control systems.

 [P40A / Emergency Diesel Generator Reliability Upgrades]: The Emergency Diesel Generator is a critical backup system required for safe underway operation. SEAWOLF and LOS ANGLES class submarines
 use a Fairbanks Morse Engineering generator system which remains largely in a 1970's era configuration. This funding line develops and installs modernization solutions to improve reliability and supportability of
 the engines and related control systems in order to support continued operation throughout the life of the ship.

 [P40A / VACL Battery Management System Upgrade]: VIRGINIA Class ships (Block I-IV) use legacy Automatic Battery Management / Battery Current Management Systems (ABMS/BCMS) to monitor battery
 health and operating conditions. This monitoring equipment is required to safely operate the battery and thus the submarine. The existing design is 20 years old, and while a tech refresh was conducted in
 FY20/21, the refreshed systems will be obsolete and unsupportable well prior to the end of service life for these boats. Funding is requested to replace the legacy systems starting with VA class Block III boats
 with the new design Battery Management System (BMS) used on VA class Block V.

 [P40A / VACL Pressure Hull Castings]: Due to significant vendor issues regarding improper heat treatment of large pressure hull castings on VACL hulls, certain castings require replacement to allow continued
 safe operation of the submarine. This program procures replacements castings for known deficient castings in the fleet. SIB-25 study funding has been added to support casting replacement efforts.

 [P40A / VACL Modified Tailcone]: The modified tailcone mitigates an issue that imposes operational restrictions on VA Class SSNs. This funding line modifies existing tailcones for a more economical price than
 procuring new tailcones. Installation will be performed during EDSRA/DMP as part of the existing planned work package.

 [P40A / SWCL San Pump Upgrade]: This line procures and installs modified OHIO class sanitary pumps for SEAWOLF class ships. Original design SEAWOLF class pumps have proven unsupportable and 2 of
 the 3 ships in the class do not have operational pumps and instead must blow tanks with air. Blowing places operation restrictions on the ship and causes excessive wear of HPACs along with detrimental fatigue
 cycles on the hull/tank structure. The use of modified OHIO class pumps saves significant procurement cost and leverages existing support and repair facilities.

 [P40A / Periodic Tech Refresh of COTS Systems (PTROCS)]: VA Class SSNs have several COTS based. HM&E systems requiring planned modernization to allow continued supportability or operation.
 Modernization needs are identified based on system health and predictive modeling or, for SWFTS connected systems, as needed to maintain interface compatibility. PTROCS groups and level loads the
 design and development lines of these HM&E systems into one PTROCS design and development line to support more responsive ship alteration development of these systems. This line will fund proactive
 management of these systems, and systems previously funded under other lines, such as VACL ICS and VACL machinery control will continue to be funded under these lines. Provides funding to support
 design and development of modernization efforts for emerging COTS obsolescence issues. Modernization issues are driven by diminishing manufacturing sources and SWFTS technical insertion schedule.

 [P40A / SWCL Ship Control Systems]: SIB-25 Study funding has been added to support modernization efforts. This line funds modernization efforts for SEAWOLF class unique ship control system/Fly By Wire
 hardware, software and certification test systems. SEAWOLF class ship control systems/Fly By Wire have unique hardware and software systems that have multiple obsolescence issues that require mitigation
 to allow continued supportability of these ships. Additionally, unique test equipment required for certification of these components requires modernization to ensure continued functionality. Additional funding was
 added to FY25 and out to continue modernization efforts.

Shipboard Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                        Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                   Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 [P40A / VA Class ICCP Upgrade Procurement]: Virginia class submarines use a COTS based Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) system to protect against corrosion of exposed metallic components.
 Reliable operation of this system is critical to prevent excessive corrosion. Failure of the system to perform results in damage that requires expensive structural repairs in overhauls and has impact on the ability of
 the ship to meet required service life. The existing system on Block I/II ships is obsolete and unsupportable. This line procures and installs an upgrade to the current generation Block V VACL system.

 [P40A / MPU/SSTG Servo Valve Upgrade]: Upgrades hydraulic servo valves on Block I-IV VIRGINIA CLASS MPU and SSTG throttle valve systems to mitigate hazards documented in HAR 392-1931. Funds are
 to procure valves in FY25 and they will be installed via A&I when schedule supports.

 [P40A / VIRGINIA Class Fire Detection System Backfit]: Investigations and reviews following the USS BONHOMME RICHARD (LHD-6) fire identified a need for improvement in fire detection and notification
 capability onboard US Navy ships. No attack submarines currently have this capability outside of temporary systems installed during availabilities. A requirement for this capability has been added to the
 VIRGINIA CLASS Block VI baseline. This funding will be used in the development of a backfit ShipAlt based on the VIRGINIA CLASS Block VI system and installation on all in-service VIRGINIA CLASS
 submarines in the first available CNO availability. Funding profile assumes funding of S/A development in FY24, installs starting in FY26, and 2-year lead time for material. Cost is based on VIRGINIA CLASS
 Block VI cost estimates.

 [P40A / VA CL - Payload Tube Expeditionary Reload Time Improvements]: Current Multiple AUR Canister (MAC)/MAC Support Service Module (MSSM) piping configuration does not allow at sea flushing.
 This results in a lengthy in-port cleaning process being required to reload VIRGINIA Payload Tubes (VPTs) after launching weapons. Funding will be used to implement system modifications to support at-
 sea HAZMAT flushing thereby increasing the speed of reloading the VPTs in port. PMS392 has life-cycle sustainment responsibly for Weapons Launch and Handling Systems, hence this modification requires
 PMS392 funding.

 [P40A / SEAWOLF Class - Bow Dome]: The SEAWOLF Class submarines sustainment strategy did not include the provisioning of replacement bow domes; therefore, no replacement bow domes for these in-
 service submarines are within the Navy's inventory. There are three SEAWOLF Class submarines in service, including USS JIMMY CARTER. This program would procure one SEAWOLF Class bow dome for
 use in the event replacement is required. Funds in FY25 are to purchase a Bow Dome which has a three plus year lead time.

 [P40A / VCS Obsolescence]: These lines provide additional funding for VCS Block I/II and III/IV modernization to address existing and future systems which will require modernization to address various
 obsolescence related issues. This provides funding to address additional modernization needs similar to those covered by Periodic Tech Refresh of COTS Systems and various other ShipAlts developed to
 backfit Block V/VI designs on Block I-IV submarines.

 [P40A / NPES Systems]: The average age of Non-Propulsion Electronic Systems (NPES) on Block I/II are more than 18 years old. Due to Obsolescence and lack of replacement parts CANNABs are having to
 be taken from boats in Availabilities to keep boats outside of Availabilities operational. There are multiple Sonar Systems, Combat Systems, Electronic Warfare (EW), Imaging, and Radar Systems, Ship Defense
 and Unmanned Systems, and Training Systems that have significant Obsolescence issue that must be addressed. This funding supports various aspects of NPES alteration development.

 [P40A / Mat Sup Phase out 688]: Due recent assessment of LA CL overhaul schedules and probable service life extensions has shown a need to install some systems like the Low Pressure electrolyzers (LPEs)
 on additional boats. This funding will support this installations.

 [P40A / Sup Fut VCS Blocks]: Funding will be used to upgrade systems like the Electro-Mechanical Power Conditioner and Controller (EPCC) on older classes based on New Constructions designs.

 [P40A / VCS Bowdome]: The initial VIRGINIA class sustainment strategy did not include the provisioning of replacement bow domes, therefore no replacement domes for in-service submarines exist in the Navy's
 inventory. To date, the fleet has replaced two VCS bow domes by taking them from new construction, which is no longer possible due to 2/year construction. This line provides funding to procure two VIRGINIA
 class bow domes in support of in-service SSN maintenance and sustainment.

 [P40A / VA CL NRFI Shaft Refurbishment]: During each shaft change-out availability, a recently refurbished Ready For Issue (RFI) propulsion shaft is mated up to various propulsor components in preparation for
 installation. Some of this work can be performed off hull and in parallel to the EDSRA timeline, while other work is being done. While RFI VA Class shafts have historically been delivered to yards just in time for
 installation, they have not been available in sufficient time to execute required schedules. Having RFI shafts available at the start of EDSRA would allow some schedule savings during the availability. To support

LI 0941 - Submarine Support Equipment                                                           UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                       Volume 1 - 308

Shipboard Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                        Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                    Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 this effort, spare RFI shafts are needed in inventory. As these are extremely long lead time items (more than 4 years), funding for additional new manufacture shafts would not help in the short term. There are
 three (3) existing offloaded Not Ready For Issue (NRFI) shafts available for refurbishment. Funding for the refurbishment of these shafts to become RFI shafts in storage would help reduce time and schedule risk
 for shaft change-out availabilities. This funding would be utilized by NAVSUP to award repair contracts to NAVSEA certified submarine shaft repair vendors. Schedule saving would return the Ship back to the
 fleet sooner increasing the Ao for the ship. Reducing man-days is a NSS-SY effort and outsourcing the refurbishment reduces the time a ship is off line.

 [P40A / SWF SCS Test Equipment]: The Performance Monitoring Program (PMP) provides direct fleet support in their areas of expertise to correct CASREPS, prevent Fail to Sails, and provide 24 hour, 7 days
 a week support to subs and squadrons to ensure subs stay on schedule. The PMP consists of 8 PMT sites including Guam and Diego Garcia/Souda Bay detachment. Funding is required to maintain the $15.7M
 of test equipment operational from normal wear/repairs, and obsolescence issues. Currently, we spend $400K/year (2.55% of total inventory value) and falling behind on planned tech refresh and obsolescence.
 With the stand up of the Reliability (RAM) IPT, ISEAs have recommended a few new test sets to improve Ao and reduce maintenance costs. The PMP Sustainment mangers have taken the point in finding
 solutions to reliability issues, this funding will facilitate the reliability working group and sustainment managers in providing support to the fleet. The PMP maintains roughly 4,215 general and special purpose test
 equipment items at all sites.

 [P40A / VA CL - Drawing Updates & HME Modernization]: VA CL fast attack submarines drawings include excessive amounts of Technical Variance Documents (TVD) or Engineering Reports (ER) hanging
 against the drawings. They are not being revised to 'clean' drawings. A cost savings measure in New Construction resulted in continued growth in TVD hanging against drawings. This measure did not anticipate
 that shipyards would be adversely impacted. This has resulted in significant increase in planning costs for EDSRA and DMP work. This will incur at least $2-4B (AWP planning costs) over the life of the VA CL
 program. Planners and sailors are at risk and can miss critical data when reviewing drawings and TVD. This could result in a number of different issues including tagging out the wrong component, use of the
 wrong material or use of an incorrect maintenance procedure. $6M of HME modernization efforts had to be deferred in FY22. Funding will allow the planning yard to start updating the drawings and remove
 hanging paperwork against the drawings. This will reduce the existing backlog and keep it from continuing to grow.

 [P40A / Acoustic Range Equipment]: Procures equipment and materials required to implement, sustain and modernize the infrastructure associated with the Submarine Acoustic Silencing Program. TYCOMs
 have consistently rated the conduct of noise trials as a high priority. The requirements for this program are identified in Chief of Naval Operations (CNO) Specific Operational Requirements 46-28 (assessment
 of ship's acoustic posture, etc.) and Naval Sea Systems Instruction (NAVSEAINST) C9073.2B (Acoustic Surveys Policy). This is the only program in place to procure equipment for the purpose of measuring,
 monitoring, assessing and improving the stealth and security of deployed submarines. The equipment is used for fixed/mobile range facilities, on test vessels, and at supporting laboratories. This program
 replaces/upgrades broken or obsolete hardware and software. This modernization ensures the readiness and capabilities of the technology/infrastructure supporting the Acoustic Measurement Facilities
 Improvement Program, to include the South Tongue of the Ocean Acoustic Facility (STAFAC) and Southeast Alaska Facility (SEAFAC), remain aligned with platform requirements and evolution of the threat.
 Specific items include hydrophones, towed arrays, ranging and tracking systems, electronics, calibrated noise sources, shore power cables, buoys, fiber optic cables, data analysis systems, workstations, data
 storage and retrieval, communications systems, analyzers, tape recorders, accelerometers, monitors, etc.

 [P40A / Underwater Electro-Magnetic (UEM) Range Equipment]: Procures equipment and materials to support the Electro-Magnetic Silencing Program for operating nuclear submarines. The requirements for
 magnetic silencing and surveillance are identified in Chief of Naval Operations (CNO) Instruction OPNAVINST 8950.2H. Underwater Electromagnetic Measurement System (UEMMS) equipment and supporting
 facilities acquire/analyze magnetic and electric field signatures to monitor, assess and reduce the detectability/susceptibility of deployed submarines and surface ships to improve stealth and ensure mission
 security. This program replaces broken or obsolete hardware and software used to monitor ship's UEM signatures. Modernizations ensure system readiness and provide technology insertions as necessary to
 pace the threat and changes to platform signature requirements. The systems enable the technical community to assess the electromagnetic health of the force and improve the performance of individual ship
 systems. Specific items include conductivity-time-depth (CTD) sensors, acoustic doppler current profilers (ADCPs), magnetic /electric field sensors, sensor stanchions/tubes, data acquisition equipment, buoys,
 cabling and ancillary materials, data analysis hardware/software, data storage/retrieval systems, calibration sources, workstations, communications systems, analyzers, and supporting laboratory equipment.

 [P40A / Pacific Fleet Submerged Electro-Magnetic Range]: Funds a submerged Underwater Electromagnetic (UEM) measurement capability (range) in the PACFLT AOR, located at the Pacific Missile Range
 Facility (PMRF) in Hawaii. This submerged measurement capability will fill a current SUBPAC capability gap and provide signature monitoring capability similar to the system at the South Florida Ocean
 Measurement Facility (SFOMF) in Fort Lauderdale, FL (that supports SUBLANT and the baseline assessment of all new construction submarines). The PAC UEM range will support VIRGINIA Class stealth, and
 future submarine classes.

Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 [P40A / Weapon Launching Handling System]: The Submarine Torpedo Tube Support category funds in-service support and alteration procurements for all submarine Torpedo Tubes (TT), Torpedo Ejection
 Pumps (TEP), Internal Countermeasure Launchers (ICL), Weapons Launch Console (WLC) and Weapons Stowage and Handling Systems (WSHS). Development efforts under this item number include
 Engineering Change Proposals (ECP), ORDALTs, and Test Equipment and alteration material procurement to correct significant deficiencies in equipment affecting personnel safety, ship safety and system
 performance. Efforts also include modernization required to ensure continue supportability of COTS based systems and comparability with SWTFS system upgrades. Production engineering includes resolving
 Liaison Action Requests (LARs) configure/test assembly in lab prior to ship installation and in-shop engineering support of vendor testing.

 [P40A / Weapon Launch Console (WLC) Tech Refresh]: VIRGINIA Block I-IV weapons launch consoles face a variety of obsolescence and supportability issues. Additionally, modernization in Block III & IV
 ships is required to ensure continued operability with future SWFTS upgrades. The weapons launch console interfaces with SWTFS systems, and thus must support correct interface protocols and general
 requirements such as cybersecurity compliance but is not modernized as part of SWFTS installs. This funding line supports design, procurement, and installation of required modifications to these systems on
 Block I-IV ships.

 [P40A / Torpedo Tube Fiber Optic Interface Upgrade]: Future weapons systems and other payloads such as the MK 48 Mod 8 / Mod 9 HWT are planned to transition to a fiber optic post-launch communication
 system. This provides an 1,800,000x increase in bandwidth over legacy copper systems, however a new tube penetrator is required to support this interface. The new penetrator will be installed in an existing
 unused port in the tube to minimize installation costs, however design and procurement costs are significant due to the need to meet SUBSAFE and Level 1 requirements. This funding line will upgrade all in
 service SSN platforms.

 [P40A / Weapon Handling Systems - EPCC Tech Refresh]: VIRGINIA Block I-IV class submarine weapon handling systems use electromechanical actuators to actuate weapons cradle movement controlled by
 Electromechanical Power Conditioner and Controllers (EPCCs). These units are no longer produced and unsupportable. This line funds design, procurement and install of new design EPCC systems that are
 supportable and maximize commonality with VIRGINIA Block V submarines.

 [P40A / VCS Torpedo Room Fixes]: Early VCS torpedo rooms have multiple documented hazards. This line provides funding to procure and install SHIPALT 4561K on the 9 remaining Block I/II submarines that
 do not already have it, and accelerates procurement and installation of the Block V EPCC on Block I-IV submarines beyond what item 3.10 allowed in order to address critical obsolescence issues.

 [P40A / VA Class SOF Modifications]: Starting in FY22, all funding for FY22-25 SSN SOF support transferred from BLI 0950 to BLI 0941. Provides for procurement of VIRGINIA Class unique system components
 that will be installed during planned modernization periods. Also provides funding to perform integrated testing of these unique systems to ensure satisfactory operation with other HM&E and Combat Systems.
 Funds are provided for procurement of Submarine Special Operations Forces (SOF) unique system components that will be installed during planned modernization periods. Funds are also provided for
 installation of SOF unique system components. All funding for FY21 and prior resides in BLI 0950.

 [P40A / Diver Oxygen Treatment System (DOTS)/Lockout Trunk]: Funds installation of Diver Oxygen Treatment System (DOTS) and certification of Lockout Trunk (LOT) on Special Operations Forces (SOF)
 designated submarines SSN 777 and 778 to support NAVY SOCOM requirements. Supports the planning and procurement of long-lead time materials for installation during scheduled Depot Modernization
 Periods (DMPs) due to length of time required to install DOTS.

 [P40A / Dry Deck Shelter (DDS) Modernization]: The DDS is a certified diving system that attaches to modified host submarines and provides for insertion of SOF forces and platforms. The Department of the
 Navy has MOA cost share agreements with the United States Special Operations Command (USSOCOM) to support the modernization of DDS units in order to accommodate current and future Naval Special
 Warfare payloads, as well as large U.S. Navy payloads. Funding continues engineering design, fabrication, assembly, testing, and sparing. Changes include extending the shelter 50 inches, replacing the Track
 and Cradle assembly with a higher capacity and remotely operated Payload-Launch and Recovery System (P-LARS), incorporation of remote controls for all DDS functions, and relocation of equipment inside the
 DDS hangar to accommodate Navy and USSOCOM payloads such as SWCS/MK-11 SDV. DDS modernization is currently a 68/32 cost share between SOCOM and the Department of the Navy.

 [P40A / A SOCOM Project Integration into VACL Block I-III]: Support for in service VA Class SOF capabilities. Starting in FY25, this PB009 sub-line includes funding to modify (2) additional VACL Block I-III
 submarines in order to integrate and host a SOCOM Project.

Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 [P40A / Submarine Rotational Forces - West]: AUKUS Submarine Rotational Forces West (SRF-W) sub-line procures equipment necessary for C4I Support of units and organizations in Australia. Equipment
 covers all necessary IT services (NIPR, SIPR, NNPI, SYLAN, SECNET, TRANET, Telephony, etc.). These systems are all essential to establishing normal operations for US units in Australia. Costs are expected
 to be one time procurement costs and totals are based on similar recent installations.

 Justification:
 The increase in funding from FY 2025 to FY 2026 is due to findings from the SIB-25 study that resulted in the addition of funding South Tongue of the Ocean Acoustic Facility (STAFAC) Acoustic Measurement
 Facilities Improvement Program (AMFIP) Revised Estimate at Completion, Southeast Alaska Facility (SEAFAC) Life Extension efforts, Pacific Deep Electromagnetic Research Measurement Array (PACDERMA)
 facility construction, the SSN CL - Spectroscopic Total Air Monitor, and the VIRGINIA Class Fire Detection System Backfit. The increase in funding for the VIRGINIA Class Fire Detection System Backfit is
 transitioning from design and development and first-time procurement in FY25 to second procurement and first-time installation in FY26. The increase in funding from FY 2025 to FY 2026 is due to the AUKUS
 Submarine Rotational Forces West (SRF-W) funding to support Ashore C4I Integration, design and installation of C4I systems which will directly contribute to achieving SRF-W IOC and FOC milestones.

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $.393 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 Footnotes:
 (1)
       1.1 & 1.2 SSTG Digital Voltage Regulator program upgrades a total of 6 ships.
 (2)
       1.3 & 1.4 ICS Block 5 Upgrade: Procures and installs S/A 5061K on all VACL block I/II hulls. Funding profile varies to align with CNO availability schedules. 2 year procurement lead time.
 (3)
       1.5 & 1.6 DGIOTS Shock Modification ShipAlt 4776K: Procurement and installation occurs in the same year (less than 12-month lead time).
 (4)
       1.7 & 1.8 Forward Automatic Bus Transfer Power Mod: Procurement and installation occurs in the same year (less than 12 months lead time).
 (5)
       1.9 & 1.10 MRO Shock Mod ShipAlt 4620K funding plan is to procure and install a total of 8 units.
 (6)
       1.11 & 1.12 ShipAlt 5062K Circuit D/Cathodic Protection Sys OER: Funds procurement and installation of alteration to upgrade legacy VACL systems to supportable blk V system.


LI 0941 - Submarine Support Equipment                                                                                                   UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   Volume 1 - 317
Navy                                                                                                                                        Page 13 of 18                                           P-1 Line #11
                                                                                                               UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-40a, Budget Item Justification For Aggregated Items: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                                   Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                         P-1 Line Item Number / Title:                                                                  Aggregated Items Title:
1810N / 01 / 9                                                                                 0941 / Submarine Support Equipment                                                             Submarine Support Equipment
 (7)
   1.13, 1.14 & 1.15 SSTG Reliability Upgrades: Funds modifications required to improve reliability of VACL SSTGs. Install and procurement funding varies from year to year based on differences in quantities as needed to align with
 maintenance availabilities.
 (8)
       1.16 Portable Ship Control Units: Installs upgrades portable ship control system to allow continued operation with updated combat systems suite.
 (9)
       1.21 & 1.22 Low-pressure Electrolyzer (LPE) funding transferred from BLI 0990. Procurements have an 18 month lead time. Program funding procures and installs a total of 7 units scheduled to coincide with 688 class refueling overhauls.
 (10)
        1.23 LPE Production Engineering - Funds efforts that support sustainability of LPE units on in-service SEAWOLF & VIRGINIA class submarines.
 (11)
        1.24 Central Atmosphere Monitoring System (CAMS) Modernization - Funds efforts to develop, qualify and implement modernization solutions that allow sustainment of the CAMS system until a replacement system is available.
 (12)
     1.25 SSN HM&E System Sustainment - Funds development and implementation of various minor critical alterations to in-service SSN HM&E systems. This funding line differs from the PTROCS line in that is support modernization of
 hardware items such as lifelines, hatch clips, pumps, etc. whereas PTROCS support modernization driven by obsolescence of COTS based control systems.
 (13)
        1.26 VA Class Machinery Control System Modernization - Procures and installs various ShipAlts to update obsolete COTS based machinery control systems to supportable modern systems.
 (14)
        1.27 Emergency Diesel Generator Reliability Upgrades - Funds procurement and installation of various alterations required to support sustainment and reliable operation of SSN Emergency Diesel Generators.
 (15)
        1.28 VACL Battery Management System Upgrade - Funding is requested to replace the legacy systems starting with VA class block III boats with the new design Battery Management System (BMS) used on VA class block V.
 (16)
        1.29 VACL Pressure Hull Castings - Funds replacement of hull castings identified to have significant defects resulting from improper vendor heat treatment of these large castings.
 (17)
        1.30 VACL Modified Tailcone - Modified tailcone, mitigates vulnerability that imposes operational restriction on VA Class SSNs.
 (18)
        1.31 SWCL San Pump Upgrade - Procures and installs modified OHIO class sanitary pumps on SEAWOLF class submarines to eliminate the requirement to blow tanks overboard with high pressure air.
 (19)
        1.32 Periodic Tech Refresh of COTS Systems (PTROCS) - Provides funding to support design and development of modernization efforts for emerging COTS obsolescence issues.
 (20)
        1.34 SWCL Ship Control System Sustainment - Funds modernization efforts for SEAWOLF class unique ship control system hardware, software and test systems.
 (21)
    1.35 & 1.36 VACL ICCP Upgrades - Procures and installs alteration to replace obsolete VACL Blk I/II ICCP system to Blk V baseline. Procurements start in FY25 with procurement and installation quantity varying as needed to align with
 maintenance availabilities.
 (22)
     1.37 VCS MPU/SSTG Servo Valve Upgrade (SIB-25) - Procures and installs alteration to replace VACL servo valve upgrades as part of a reliability concern in the legacy design. Procurements start in FY25 with procurement and
 installation quantity varying as needed to align with maintenance availabilities.
 (23)
     1.38 SSN Fire Detection System (SIB-25) - Provides funding to support design and development of the BLK V efforts to ensure safety of the Payload that is installed on VACL submarines. The increase in funding for the VIRGINIA Class
 Fire Detection System Backfit is transitioning from design and development and first-time procurement in FY25 to second procurement and first-time installation in FY26.
 (24)
        1.39 Payload Tube Expeditionary Reload Time Improvements (SIB-25) - Funding will be used to implement system modifications to support at-sea HAZMAT flushing thereby increasing the speed of reloading the VPTs in port.
 (25)
        1.40 SWCL Bowdome (SIB-25) - Provides funding to procure the replacement Dome for SSN 21 Class which is showing signs of wear and deterioration.
 (26)
     1.41 BLK I/II Obs (SIB-25) - Procures and installs alteration to replace obsolete VACL Blk I/II systems to Blk V baseline. Procurements start in FY25 with procurement and installation quantity varying as needed to align with maintenance
 availabilities.
 (27)
    1.42 BLK III/IV Obs (SIB-25) - Procures and installs alteration to replace obsolete VACL Blk III/IV systems to Blk V baseline. Procurements start in FY25 with procurement and installation quantity varying as needed to align with
 maintenance availabilities.
 (28)
        1.43 NPES (SIB-25) - This funding supports various aspects of NPES alteration development for Blk I/II systems.
 (29)
        1.44 Mat. Sup. Phase out 688 - Provides funding to harvest parts from Deactivation ships to ensure that the refuelers are fully supported with the material needed to reach the new service life.
 (30)
    1.45 Sup. Fut. VCS Blocks - Provides funding to support the in-service VACL BLK of ships that are under design changes reviews in future BLK designs to allow for development of back-fits to ensure sustainment through strategic
 upgrades is achieved.
 (31)
      1.47 SSN CL - Spectroscopic Total Air Monitor (STAM) - Funds development, qualification, procurement, and installation of the STAM system. This system replaces the legacy CAMS system on multiple SSN classes, which suffers
 reliability, maintainability, and procurement issues due to system age.
 (32)
        1.48 - VA CL - NRFI Shaft Refurbishment - Funds will refurbish 3 NRFI shafts.
 (33)
        1.49 - SWF SCS Test Equipment - The program supports the procurement of new test equipment in support of the Performance Monitoring Program (PMP).
 (34)
        1.50 - VA CL - Drawing Updates & HME Modernization - The program will start updating the drawings and remove hanging paperwork against the drawings.
 (35)
     2.1 Acoustic Range - In order to meet the acoustic requirements identified in CNO Specific Operational Requirements 46-28 (assessment of ship's acoustic posture, etc.) and NAVSEAINST C9073.2B (Acoustic Surveys Policy), this
 budget line increased from FY25 to FY26 to support STAFAC Recapitalization. The STAFAC facility reached end of design life in 2023 and must be upgraded with additional sensor and processing capability in order to validate COLUMBIA
 (CLB) Class CDD stealth requirements. FY26 increase reflects STAFAC Recapitalization project Cost to Complete and accounts for increased complexity in final sensor suite and mooring design, significant increases in component
 procurement costs due to inflation and limited supply chain, expanded at-sea testing requirements, and increased government/contract labor costs associated with deep-water cable-laying operations/support ships/at-sea system validation
 operations. Additionally, the Southeast Alaska Facility (SEAFAC) acoustic measurement range requires investment in the modernization of major components in order to extend the design life of the range into the mid-2030s. In FY26 the
 program will begin the modernization of submerged electronics and tracking systems within the SEAFAC range.


LI 0941 - Submarine Support Equipment                                                                          UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                                              Volume 1 - 318
Navy                                                                                                               Page 14 of 18                                       P-1 Line #11
                                                                                                            UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-40a, Budget Item Justification For Aggregated Items: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                          Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                       P-1 Line Item Number / Title:                                                           Aggregated Items Title:
1810N / 01 / 9                                                                               0941 / Submarine Support Equipment                                                      Submarine Support Equipment
 (36)
     2.2 Underwater Electromagnetic Range Equipment - In order to meet the requirements for magnetic silencing and surveillance, as identified in CNO Instruction OPNAVINST 8950.2H, and pace the rate of introduction of VA class
 submarines with advanced degaussing and impressed current cathodic protection systems, this budget line establishes dedicated funding (in FY25 and out) to sustain and modernize underwater electro-magnetic range surveillance systems
 to support the measurement submarine UEM signatures and assessment of platform health/stealth.
 (37)
    2.3 Pacific Fleet Submerged Electro-Magnetic Range - This effort, which started in FY24, funds the installation of the Pacific Fleet Submerged Electro-Magnetic Range, a submerged Underwater Electromagnetic (UEM) measurement
 capability in the PACFLT AOR, located at the Pacific Missile Range Facility (PMRF) in Hawaii. This facility fills a current SUBPAC capability gap and will be similar to the system currently at the South Florida Ocean Measurement Facility
 (SFOMF) in Fort Lauderdale, FL and would support VIRGINIA Class and future submarine classes. Increase in PAC Fleet Submerged Electro Magnetic Range from FY25 to FY26 as project moves from the range component design phase to
 component installation and facility construction.
 (38)
        3.1 - 3.7 Sub Weapons Launch/ Handling Support funds several projects that support the modernization of the Submarine Weapons Launch/ Handling Support equipment and systems.
 (39)
   3.8 Weapons Launch Console (WLC) Tech Refresh - Funds design, procurement and installation of modification to Virginia block I-IV weapons launch consoles to mitigate obsolete components and ensure interoperability with future
 SWFTS upgrades. Installation is planned to align with TI-24 SFWTS upgrades.
 (40)
        3.9 Torpedo tube fiber optic interface upgrade - Procures and installs new torpedo tube penetrators to support new weapons and other payloads using optical communication systems.
 (41)
    3.10 Weapons Handling Systems-EPCC Tech Refresh - Funds design, procurement and install of new design weapons handling electromechanical power conditioner and control (EPCC) systems for VIRGINIA block I-IV ships that are
 supportable and maximize commonality with VIRGINIA block V submarines.
 (42)
        3.11 Torpedo Tube OPROD Upgrade - This line procures and installs upgraded OPRODs that increase fatigue life to exceed the service life of the ship.
 (43)
        3.13 VCS Torpedo Room Fixes (SIB-25) - Fund will implement Shipalts to address multiple documented hazards on VIRGINIA Class.
 (44)
     4.1 SOF Upgrades and Alterations - Funding is for SOF designated VACL submarine investments for modernization and product improvements required to achieve and maintain the SOF operational availability and presence
 requirements. Additional funding addresses component or equipment performance deficiencies, obsolescence issues, and DMSMS for the SOF VACL HOSUBs. Requirement per OPNAV N97 Ltr. SerN87/10U160461 (Subj: Submarine
 Requirements to Support SOF Delivery from SSGN and SSN).
 (45)
    4.2 Install Diver Oxygen Treatment System/Lockout Trunk on SSN 777 & 778 - Funds installation of Diver Oxygen Treatment System (DOTS) and certification of Lockout Trunk (LOT) on Special Operations Forces (SOF) designated
 submarines SSN777 and 778 to support NAVY-SOCOM requirements. Requirement per OPNAV N97 Letter Ser N87/10U160461, Submarine Requirements to Support SOF Delivery from SSGN and SSN.
 (46)
    4.3 Dry Deck Shelters Modernization - Funding to continue modernization and sparing of the Dry Deck Shelters based on MOA between OPNAV N97, USSOCOM, COMNAVSPECWARCOM, and NAVSEA dated 4 June 2019 which
 promotes an agreement to continue the cost share agreement between the Undersea Enterprise and SOCOM.
 (47)
        4.4 A SOCOM Project integration into VACL BLK I-III - Starting in FY25, modify (2) additional VACL BLK I-III submarines in order to integrate and host a SOCOM Project.
 (48)
        5.1 Submarine Rotational Forces West - AUKUS SRF-W Ashore C4I Integration, design and installation of C4I systems directly contributes to achieving SRF-W IOC and FOC milestones.

 Description:
 The VIRGINIA Class BLI 0942 provides a wide range of materials required to operate, test, support and maintain the viability of VIRGINIA SSN774 Class Submarines. Additional details are provided below for
 each effort.

 The Major Shore Spares component includes rotatable pool and insurance spares. Propulsor rotatable pool assets support planned maintenance during scheduled availabilities by decreasing equipment turn-
 around time/availability duration. Rotatable pool program equipment includes the high pressure air compressor, various pump/motor assemblies, radar mast, valves/actuators, ventilation fans and thin line towed
 array components and other components.

 The Test and Evaluation (T&E) component includes upgrading the afloat acoustic system required to conduct TECHEVAL/OPEVAL satisfactorily, efficiently, and with minimal risk of equipment failure. Conduct
 hardware and software development, procurement, maintenance, and operations as required to achieve and maintain the submarine acoustic signatures trials program. The resultant capabilities support the
 Submarine Acoustic Trials Enterprise, which includes the VIRGINIA Class Submarine test and evaluation program.

 VPT Platforms and Intermediate and Depot (I&D) Support Equipment funding includes Vertical Launch System (VLS) Peculiar Support Equipment (PSE) for All-Up Round Simulators (AURS)/All-Up Round (AUR)
 Ballast Cans necessary to conduct TECHEVAL/OPEVAL and provide ballast for ship operation. VIRGINIA Class Submarines must maintain a required Platform to Submarine ratio of 0.6 through the FYDP. The
 variable procurement quantities associated with these items are based on inventory required to maintain this ratio.

 Non-Tactical Data Processing System (NTDPS)-Nosis funding includes shipboard wireless LAN that provides a ship wide (forward of the reactor compartment) intranet (Non-classified Internet Protocol (IP)
 Router Network (Unclassified Enclave) (NIPRNET)) that significantly enhances the quality of the work by facilitating electronic correspondence, personnel data management, collaborative services, interactive
 whiteboard, multi-user chat, and access to these sites: Fleet Training Management & Planning System/Navy Training Management & Planning System (FTMPS/NTMPS), Composite Health Care System (CHCS),
 prescriptions, MYPAY-Defense Finance and Accounting Service (DFAS), Navy Personnel Command (NPC), Bureau of Naval Personnel (BUPERS), email, Fleet Technical Support Center, Atlantic (FTSCLANT),
 Submarine Maintenance Engineering, Planning and Procurement (SUBMEPP), and Navy Knowledge Online (NKO).

 Maintenance Planning System funds will be used to help ship programs identify, plan, and execute maintenance activities as well as improve efficiently at all levels (performed by ship's force as well as
 organizational/depot level) by creating a set of tools to provide a robust, distributed and comprehensive training and maintenance solution delivering dynamic content. This set of tools and the associated ship-

 Submarines.

 Ship Control System (SCS) Technology Modernization funding supports obsolescence management of the Fly-By-Wire (FBW) SCS installed and used to maneuver in-service VIRGINIA Class fast attack
 submarines. This funding provides for upgrading software and hardware to incorporate necessary safety, reliability, and performance improvements.

 Additionally, the continuous ship upgrades necessary to maintain class viability of the earlier ships are included in this funding line. This is particularly important for Commercial Off-the-Shelf (COTS) Technology
 Refreshment and Technology Upgrades for Non-Propulsion Electronic Systems (NPES). The class level of modernization and capability rests on available resources.

 [P40A / Auxiliary Equipment]: Auxiliary equipment component includes funding for ladders and ladder storage containers for Sail Racetracks, Logistics Escape Trunk/Logistics Plug Trunk (LET/LPT) and other

 [P40A / Non-Tactical Data Processing System - Nosis]: Provide software and hardware updates, software licenses and technical and logistics support to the Non-Tactical Data Processing System (NTDPS).

 [P40A / Ship Control System Technology Modernization]: Provides for the procurement of materials necessary to modernize VIRGINIA Class Fly-By-Wire (FBW) Ship Control System (SCS) to sustain interfaces
 and technology baseline with the connecting Government Furnished sub-systems contained in the Non-Propulsion Electronics System suite.

Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                               -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -      -              -
Flyaway Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                             -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -      -              -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -      -              -


 Description:
 The Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) Class Support Equipment line procures long-lead major end items and the connective equipment necessary to ensure fit and function required for repairs, and associated
 engineering, technical, and transportation support services for both LCS variants, Freedom and Independence. The items procured increase availability of vital Fleet assets by reducing repair part procurement
 and delivery times.

 LN001 - LCS Battle Spares
 This line procures shore-based LCS Class Support Equipment (Battle Spares), ensuring their availability in the event of a failure or major casualty to key ship systems. Battle Spares are major end items
 available on an as need basis to meet Fleet requirements. Inventory objectives are determined by Reliability, Maintainability, and Availability (RM&A) data, the critical application of equipment, and input from the
 LCS technical community.

 LN002 - LCS Water Jet Spares
 This line procures LCS Water Jets and associated propulsion components for both LCS variants. The configurations vary between ships; there are five Water Jet configurations. Water Jet assemblies are
 designated as Battle Spares and support a rotatable pool for life cycle sustainment.

 LN003 - Engineering Services
 This line funds engineering, technical and transportation support for LCS Class Support Equipment. Services include the establishment of Technical Data Packages (TDPs), the creation of associated depot level
 repair parts lists/installation requirements, and transportation services.

 LN006 - LCS Ships Class Ancillary Equipment
 This funding procures the connective equipment necessary to ensure fit and function of procured LCS Class Support Equipment (Battle Spares) when installed.

 Justification:

 (Freedom Variant) and Engineering Services.

 Mitigation of long lead times for repairs through procurements of adequate battle spares using failure data allows for continued progress toward mainstreaming and normalizing LCS platform sustainment.

 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 Description:
 This program procures replacement of Valve Regulated Lead Acid (VRLA) batteries for all classes of submarines (LOS ANGELES, SEAWOLF, OHIO SSBN/SSGNs and VIRGINIA) as well as funds and manages
 the production engineering efforts for the In-Service Engineering Agent (ISEA) at NSWC Crane.

 FY 2026 funding supports the procurement of replacement Valve Regulated Lead Acid (VRLA) batteries for LOS ANGELES (5); OHIO (4); SEAWOLF (1); VIRGINIA (11); VA VRLA installation tray (1) and
 associated production engineering.

 Submarines utilize a Main Storage Battery that provides an immediately available source of backup power that is vital to maintaining ship and reactor plant safety. Submarines with insufficient battery capacity
 cannot deploy, use the reactor, or operate at-sea. Batteries have a finite life and are consumables; periodic replacements of the battery are incorporated into the maintenance plan for all submarines and are
 scheduled to minimize the impact to submarine force operational availability.
 The present geopolitical environment has necessitated Combatant Commanders placing high demands on the submarine force for strategic deterrence, force protection and especially sensitive ISR (Intelligence,
 Surveillance, and Reconnaissance) missions. The procurement and maintaining of submarine batteries is a pillar that keeps the fleet ready to meet operational commitments.
 Yearly battery procurement costs are based on fixed, per-cell costs defined by a multi-year Firm Fixed Price contract in effect from FY23-FY28. The quantity of cells required and mix of cell types varies yearly as
 a function of fleet requirements; consequently, overall battery procurement cost varies despite fixed baseline cell pricing.

 HM002, HM008, HM009, and HM010 reflect the battery procurement quantities and costs for LOS ANGELES, OHIO, SEAWOLF, and VIRGINIA class submarines respectively. Yearly procurement quantities are
 projected from the expected average service life of historical batteries. Funding supports procurement the average replacement requirement of 20 battery ship sets each year, though individual yearly quantity
 varies based on fleet operational requirements.

 The budget lines also procure battery installation tray sets that are required for all installed batteries. Tray sets are procured during conversion and new construction but must be removed, inspected, and
 reinstalled during shipboard battery replacement. Procurement of additional shipsets greatly improves battery replacement logistics, enabling schedule flexibility and quick adaption to fleet timelines.

 HM830 Production Engineering funds Naval Surface Warfare Center (NSWC) Crane as the Acquisition Engineering Agent (AEA) and In-Service Engineering Agent (ISEA). NSWC Crane is responsible for leading
 a Government & Industry Root Cause Analysis Team to identify and address battery performance issues, provide corrective actions to operational submarines regarding batteries, and conducting qualification
 testing of delivered VRLA batteries. NSWC Crane's analyses supports improvements to design and operating procedures aimed at increased battery life, reliability, and predictability.

 Justification:
 FY 2026 funding supports the procurement of replacement Valve Regulated Lead Acid (VRLA) batteries for LOS ANGELES (5); OHIO (4); SEAWOLF (1); VIRGINIA (11); VA VRLA installation tray (1) and

 Description:
 This budget provides funding for the in service LPD 17 class program support equipment. Previous funding for LPD 17 class support equipment (FY 2012 and earlier) was provided under line item 0981,
 Items Less Than $5 Million. Funding is required to upgrade mission critical electronics and Hull Mechanical & Engineering (HM&E) systems including the Engineering Control Systems (ECS), Ship Control
 Systems (SCS), Degaussing Systems, Shipboard Wide-Area Network (SWAN), commercial software products for ECS, SCS, Command, Control, Communications, Computers, Intelligence, Surveillance and
 Reconnaissance (C4ISR) and Administrative Communications. Ship Installation Drawings (SIDs) funding and tasking are now required at A-2 years of CNO availability start date, vice A-1 years.

 [P40A / CJ010 - Schoolhouse TTE]: CJ010 - SCHOOLHOUSE TTE
 Funding for Schoolhouse Technical Training Equipment (TTE) is for the infrastructure and equipment to set up the schoolhouses for multiple systems such as ECS and SCS 60Hz and Ship Service Diesel
 Generators (SSDG). The schoolhouses will provide critical training to sailors in operation and maintenance of those systems.

 [P40A / CJ020 - Shore Based Spares]: CJ020 SHORE BASED SPARES
 Shore-Based Spares are critical long lead time items that are staged shore-side in the event of a catastrophic failure on an operational ship. These spares must be periodically replenished as assets are
 utilized. Furthermore, as the LPD 17 Class continues to introduce new ships (additional) to the fleet, the number of spare requirements increases as additional items are added to the spares list to support newer
 shipboard configurations.

 [P40A / CJ080 Conjunctive HM&E AIT]: CJ080 CONJUNCTIVE HM&E ALTERATIONS
 Conjunctive HM&E Alterations includes design integration, equipment procurement, recurring equipment engineering, equipment integration and Integrated Logistics Services (ILS).

 [P40A / CJ081 - Navy Common Actuators]: CJ081 Navy Common Actuator Development Program
 Congressionally enacted requirement for Navy Common Actuator solution and supportability for the Class.

 [P40A / CJ035 - HM&E CONDITION SYSTEM]: CJ035 HULL, MECHANICAL AND ELECTRICAL CONDITION SYSTEM
 This solution proposed would implement HM&E principles, it would implement a Defense-in-Depth Functional Implementation Architecture (DFIA) and meet the requirements of the standards released by the
 Information Assurance Technical Authority Board (IA TAB) across all the H,M&E systems deploying the ability to protect, detect, react, and restore in a contested environment. The HM&E Condition System will
 monitor the signals on the HM&E network, both between HM&E systems and also those coming from other functional domains.
