LI 0977 - Underwater EOD Equipment                                                                                           UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          Volume 1 - 419
Navy                                                                                                                             Page 5 of 21                                           P-1 Line #22
                                                                                                UNCLASSIFIED
Exhibit P-5, Cost Analysis: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                                           Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                             P-1 Line Item Number / Title:                                                   Item Number / Title [DODIC]:
1810N / 01 / 9                                                                     0977 / Underwater EOD Equipment                                                 1 / Underwater EOD Programs
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready) :                                                                     MDAP/MAIS Code:
 (†)
       indicates the presence of a P-5a

 Footnotes:
 (1)
    CE 1.1.1 MIW-VIP: MIW-VIP measures magnetic and acoustic signatures of equipment that will be exposed to influence (magnetic and acoustic) ordnance in combat. This effort utilizes existing ranges and
 portable ranges (Forward Area Combined Degaussing and Acoustic Range (FACDAR)). Beginning in FY 2021 this cost element was eliminated.
 (2)
       CE 2.1.1 ExMCM EOD Response Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV): Provides procurement funding for fleet outfitting.
 (3)
     CE 2.1.2 ADVANCED DIVER INTEGRATION SENSORS (e.g. STRIDENT): Provides for the procurement of a specialized EOD Diver underwater handheld sonar that is developed to operate in an influence
 fired ordnance environment.
 (4)
    CE 2.1.3 COMMAND DETONATION SYSTEMS (RUFIS Receiver): Provides for the procurement of improved underwater acoustic receivers to provide safe stand-off command detonation capabilities to
 Explosive Ordnance Disposal (EOD) divers. The improved receiver will address the current capability shortfalls of the legacy MK 58 receiver by improving the signal fidelity, receiving range, and interoperability
 with delivery systems.
 (5)
    CE 2.1.6 MESR PAYLOAD KITS - Sensors: Provides for the procurement of sensor-based payloads (e.g., acoustic, electro-optic, tactile, etc) that will be integrated onto MESR platforms to provide improved
 detection, localization, and identification capabilities when deployed in EOD Response missions.
 (6)
    CE 2.1.7 MESR PAYLOAD KITS - Kinetic Effects: Provides for the procurement of kinetic payloads (e.g., explosive disruptors, bulk charges, etc) that will be integrated onto MESR platforms to provide
 improved neutralization capabilities when deployed in EOD Response missions.
 (7)
     CE 2.1.8 MESR PAYLOAD KITS - C3 Modules: Provides for the procurement of command, control, and communication payloads (e.g., surface relays, acoustic modems, AI/ML algorithms, etc.) that will be
 integrated onto MESR platforms to provide improved safety stand-off and autonomous capabilities when deployed in EOD Response missions.
 (8)
       CE 2.1.9 Provides procurement funding for Fleet outfitting. CE 2.1.9 MESR Platforms quantity and funding was added to increase program wholeness.
 (9)
       CE 2.1.10 Provides procurement funding for fleet outfitting. Procures same vehicle as CE 2.1.1.
 (10)
        CE 2.1.11 MDSU PRODUCTION ENGINEERING & TEST: Provides for production support, first article tests, and factory acceptance tests for MDSU Response ROVs.
 (11)
        CE 2.1.12 NCF RESPONSE REMOTELY OPERATED VEHICLE (ROV): Provides procurement funding for fleet outfitting. Procures same vehicle as CE 2.1.1.
 (12)
        CE 2.1.13 MDSU PRODUCTION ENGINEERING & TEST: Provides for production support, first article tests, and factory acceptance tests for NCF Response ROVs.
 (13)
     CE 2.1.14 PRODUCTION ENGINEERING: Provides for production support, first article tests, and factory acceptance tests for EOD Response ROVs, MK16 PIP, STRIDENT, and other EOD equipment
 requiring additional production support. FY 2026 increase to support additional production engineering & test to the MESR, MK16 PIP, and STRIDENT programs. FY 2026 increase to support additional
 production engineering & test to the MESR, MK16 PIP, and STRIDENT programs.
 (14)
        CE 2.1.15 TRAINING: Funding provides for training of MK16 PIP, MESR, and STRIDENT efforts.
 (15)
        CE 2.1.16 PRODUCT IMPROVEMENT: Provides for engineering services to improve fielded EOD Diver systems and ROVs.
 (16)
     CE 4.1.1 MMUBA SUPPORT EQUIPMENT: Provides the procurement of technologies to provide safer tools and life support systems for EOD UMCM and MDSU operations. CE 4.1.1 funding reduced in
 FY25 due to ongoing contract delays. Funding was reallocated to RDTE (RDTE PRJ 1317) to fund MMUBA unmanned and manned testing; additional funding was shifted to CE 4.1.3 to retrofit the fleet's current
 inventory of MK 16 Mod 1 Underwater Breathing Apparatuses.
 (17)
     CE 4.1.2 MMUBA EQUIPMENT: These resources will procure the Multi-Mission Underwater Breathing Apparatus (MMUBA), which replaces the aging MK-16 UBA. This procurement will substantially
 improve a diver's ability to quickly and effectively counter enemy influence ordnance, which will decrease the detect to engage timeline and open sea lanes more quickly to Fleet maneuver and commercial
 shipping.
 (18)
      CE 4.1.3 MK 16 MOD 1 PRODUCT IMPROVEMENT PROGRAM (PIP): The MK 16 Mod 1 Product Improvement Plan (PIP) provides technological advances to MK 16 MOD 1 hardware and software. The
 PIP will initiate in FY 2025 through a lifetime buy of conversion kits to retrofit the current MK16 MOD 1 inventory. The change from previously planned spending reflects the Navy's updated procurement strategy
 for EOD and expeditionary diving UBAs. The Navy is pursuing the MK 16 PIP over the MMUBA to accelerate the delivery of UBAs to meet Fleet capability and capacity shortfalls earlier and at lower costs.
 (19)
        CE 4.1.4 Next Gen UBA Production Engineering and Test. Provides for production and testing support for Next Gen UBA LRIP units and other Diving Life Support (DLS) equipment.

 Remarks:
 [Hardware/Recurring] [Hardware/Recurring] ExMCM Outfitting: This funding resources the procurement of Expeditionary systems and support equipment. Procurement of this equipment will allow the ExMCM
 forces to be deployed globally and employed from a wide variety of shipboard platforms at sea as well as small craft in performing EOD and EXMCM missions in support of global requirements. The various
 ExMCM outfitting kits comprise the ancillary and support equipment necessary to outfit an ExMCM Company with equipment based on approved Tables of Allowances (TOAs) and are procured from a variety of
 OEMs and sources.

 UUV funding previously provided under Cost Elements 1.1.1. - 1.1.5 has been re-programmed to newly established BLI 1611 Small and Medium Class UUVs.

 (†)
       indicates the presence of a P-5a

 Footnotes:
 (20)
     CE 1.1.6 ExMCM EOD RESPONSE REMOTELY OPERATED VEHICLE (ROV): Procurement of EOD Response vehicles and associated sub-systems to leverage advancements in robotic manipulators,
 target recognition software, user interface, and explosive recognition and neutralization that have been achieved in land robots and which can now be integrated into undersea remotely operated vehicles. These
 EOD response systems will enable more rapid reacquisition, identification and neutralization of mine like targets found by other search assets.
 (21)
        C.E. 1.1.7: Production Engineering Test: Funding supportS Government Acceptance Testing (GAT).
 (22)
        CE 1.1.7 ExMCM TOA EQUIPMENT: Procurement of EXMCM Company Table of Allowance (TOA) equipment for the sixteen ExMCM companies supporting global operations.

 Description:
 This budget provides for "S" cognizance (Shipboard, Hull, Mechanical & Electrical (HM&E) equipment for surface ships and aircraft carriers) which are not in any specific category. This equipment accomplishes
 program alterations for installation during CNO and Fleet availabilities, fills Fleet requisitions from casualties and attrition, provide tech refresh upgrades, and replaces obsolete equipment. Primary objectives are
 to maintain or improve readiness, safety, reliability, reduce workload, lower maintenance costs, improve sailor quality of life, and/or sustain ship classes through their notional life or beyond. The budget purchases
 and installs various equipment including machinery pumps, generators, ship propellers and shafts, air compressors, davits, A/C Plants, and steam propulsion items. It also procures allowance items as required
 by the Coordinated Shipboard Allowance List (COSAL). Major programs are the CVN Class Machinery Plant Upgrades, MACHALTs, CVN Cybersecurity, and CVN 78 class upgrades.

 [P40A / LT060 - MACHALTs]: The Machinery Alteration (MACHALT) Program supports identification, procurement, and installation of Hull, Mechanical and Electrical (HM&E) internal equipment and system
 improvements on in-service surface ships. The MACHALT Program is a single source for system level Ship Change Document (SCD) deployment outside of CNO availabilities, and is a turn-key modification and
 alteration vehicle that supplies all required engineering, equipment, logistics, installation, management, tracking, reporting, scheduling, special tools and/or procedures, and Ship's Force training. MACHALTs
 are installed via the Navy Modernization Process (NMP) in the following priority order: (1) Mandatory Safety/Statutory, (2) Component-level installations targeting risk to TYCOM operational redlines, (3) Safety,
 and (4) Troubled Systems/Obsolescence Alts. The program seamlessly integrates small modernization upgrades into ship maintenance Window(s) of Opportunity (WOO) or Continuous Maintenance Availability
 (CMAV) periods. Beginning in FY 2025, Low Pressure Air Compressor (LPAC) Programmable Logistic Controller (PLC) Navy Common Core Controller (NC3) shipset installations will be accelerated on
 DDG-51 class ships to improve reliability and performance of the compressor air systems, meet Operational Redlines, and improve Fleet Readiness to critical ship systems. Due to Fleet usage, requirements,
 and alignment of availability periods in a given year, adjustments are made to quantities and items throughout the Future Years Defense Program (FYDP). Program execution improves system reliability/
 maintainability/availability, reduces Diminishing Manufacturing Sources Material Shortages (DMSMS)/obsolescence risk, drives increased commonality, and reduces life cycle cost.

 [P40A / LT115 Additive Manufacturing]: Additive Manufacturing (AM) program to procure and install AM Equipment on Navy Platforms to increase Self-Sufficiency.

 [P40A / LT120 - Carrier Propulsion Plant Inspection]: Funds are used to procure the latest technology inspection system tooling, i.e., laser-optic, ultrasonic, fiber-optic, electro-optic and computed radiography
 inspection systems.

 [P40A / LT330 - CVN Cybersecurity]: CVN Cyber Security enables the development and installation of Ship Change Documents (SCDs) for Boundary Defense Capability (BDC), Situational Awareness, and
 Physical Security. It increases the Defense in Depth and improves the Cyber Security aspects of the CVN Nimitz Class HM&E Networks. Funding is used to update hardware and software applications and
 system configurations required for compatibility with the Department of Defense (DoD) CYBER requirements, Security Technical Implementation Guide, and other CYBER requirements. Funding is also used

Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 to update system cybersecurity Certification and Accreditation; add capability to protect, detect, and respond/recover to internal system events; and add additional defense in depth and monitoring to external
 interfaces.

 [P40A / LT331 CVN 78 Class Modernization]: CVN 78 Class funding will cover Contractor Furnished Equipment (CFE) modernizations in future availabilities to include: Dual Band Radar Capacitor Bank, Plastic
 Waste Compressed Melt Units, Waste Pulpers, Waste disinfection equipment, Plasma Arc Waste Destruction System (PAWDS) Exhaust piping, Vacuum Collection Holding and Transfer (VCHT) pumps, Hangar
 Bay and Flight Deck Speakers, Machinery Control and Monitoring System upgrades, Steering Control System upgrades, Jet Blast Deflector (JBD) electro mechanical actuators (EMA) and emergency lowering
 capability, Aviation support efforts (to include: Mission Bay and Ready rooms, MH-60 equipment stowage, Flight equipment stowage/maintenance work center) and Passive Countermeasures System material.

 [P40A / LT335 CVN Training and Equipment]: This funding procures spare equipment, propulsion shafts, and propellers with long repair turn-around times to support a change in maintenance philosophy from
 inspection and repair to remove and replace in order to significantly reduce in-schedule risk to CNO maintenance periods. Funding will be used to replace antiquated equipment and provide a technology refresh
 in the machine shops on the CVN-68 class carriers. Funding will also be used to replace outmoded and obsolete Training Test Equipment in established Navy schools, as identified in approved or draft Navy
 Training System Plans (NTSPs) as well as procure state-of-the-art, mobile and reconfigurable instructional facility, positioned at the point of need to support Sailor 2025 and Ready Relevant Learning (RRL).

 [P40A / LT830 - Carrier Production Engineering]: The review and approval of any production contract technical documentation, or the separate development of this documentation, to include Technical Manuals,
 Planned Maintenance System (PMS), Level III Production Drawings, Provisioning Technical Documentation (PTD), Program Support Data (PSD), Allowance Parts List (APL); and engineering support for final
 design reviews.

 [P3A / LT160 - Machinery Plant Upgrades]: The Machinery Plant Upgrade project within the CVN Fleet Modernization Program (FMP) encompasses CVN Machinery Control Systems, Ship Control (Navigation/
 Steering), Integrated Voice Control Networks, Announcing Systems and Shipboard Air Traffic Control Communication Systems. The machinery plant upgrade modernization alterations currently consist of
 greater than 40 different Ship Change Documents (SCD) with a cost value of approximately $42M for all the alterations combined. Individual SCDs can range in cost from $5K (for software only changes) to ~
 $14M for major system upgrade procurements. The range of SCD installs can cost as low as $100K and as high as $4M for individual SCDs. During each CNO availability, we schedule and complete a subset
 of these 40+ alterations. Each hull is different receiving varying subsets of the 40+ SCDs depending on individual ship configurations. These alts are scheduled for installation over 3-4 availabilities (which may
 be 9-12 years) with many installations occurring in later years of the FYDP. Additionally, the machinery plant upgrade program is technology driven and many alterations are still being finalized in the Navy
 modernization Program (i.e. Ship Change Document (SCD) development) process for installation in later years. New SCDs continue to be developed to proactively address obsolescence issues associated with
 ever changing technology.

 [P3A - 2 / LT314 - Carrier Oil Water Separator]: Enhanced Oil Water Separator (OWS) systems are being installed on the CVN68 Class carriers to meet the new environmental discharge law of 15 parts per
 million (ppm) worldwide. The new OWS systems include an oil content monitor and a bilge stripping structure. The new system is expected to reduce costly maintenance and reliability issues.

 [P3A - 3 / LT327 - Modular Refrigeration System.]: Modular Refrigeration System (MRS) replaces the existing legacy Ship's Stores Refrigeration System (SSRS) onboard CVN68 Class carriers. The legacy
 system is energy inefficient, space inefficient, and maintenance intensive, requiring highly skilled personnel to operate and maintain acceptable box temperatures. Most hardware associated with the legacy
 systems is specialized mil-spec and is either becoming unsupportable due to obsolescence or is grossly expensive to maintain. Each MRS is comprised of a Modular Refrigeration Unit (MRU), an operating/
 diagnostic control panel, and small chilled water cooled flat-plate condenser. There are 18 MRUs required per hull. MRS's are self-contained hatchable MRUs with a dedicated control panel that eliminate 90% of
 current maintenance requirements and require minimal operator support/training.

 Justification:
 FY 2026 adds funding for Machinery Alteration (MACHALT) (LT060) for accelerating the procurement and installation of Automatic Lube Oil Pump (ALOP) / Automatic Hydraulic Oil Pump (AHOP) Flexible Shaft
 MACHALT Pump Coupling Shipsets.

 The FY 2026 request for PEO Carriers' Fleet Modernization Program includes $0 thousand of discretionary and $75,203 thousand of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $75,203 thousand. The mandatory
 funds partially fund the planning and execution of modernization for six In-Service Carriers to include CVN 71, CVN 72, CVN 73, CVN 76, CVN 77 and CVN 78 CNO Availabilities. The modernization (Ship
 Change Documents) being executed will be for Machinery Control System (MCS), Steering Control System (SCS), Modular Refrigeration System (MRS), cybersecurity, voice and interior communications
 capability upgrades. Further information for this reconciliation request is provided in Section 200010 (Readiness) of the Reconciliation Exhibit.

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $2.594 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 Footnotes:
 (1)
   Increase from FY 2025 to FY 2026 is due to funds provided to support the acceleration of procuring and installing 42 Attached Lubricating Oil / Hydraulic Oil Pump (ALOP/AHOP) Flexible Coupling shipsets. Operating the Main Reduction
 Gear (MRG) with the existing non-functioning ALOP increases the risk of gear and bearing damage. By replacing these couplings, adequate lubrication will be provided to the MRG bearings and gear-teeth during a "loss of electrical power"
 casualty, preventing immediate and severe coast-down damage, increases readiness, and provides material support for a critical item.
 (2)
       Additive Manufacturing (AM) program to procure and install AM Equipment on Navy Platforms to increase Self-Sufficiency.
 (3)
   Effort provides cybersecurity for carrier assets and infrastructure with emphasis to prevent network intrusion on CVNs. Efforts consists of (1) boundary defense mods, (2) situational awareness mods, and (3) physical security mods.
 Quantities vary per hull and are fundamentally different. Hulls will receive different modifications in varying combinations; each hull cost is mutually exclusive of other costs.
 (4)
    The FY 2026 request for PEO Carriers' Fleet Modernization Program includes $0 thousand of discretionary and $75,203 thousand of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $75,203 thousand. $75,203 thousand of mandatory
 (reconciliation) split between Procurement of $57,154 thousand and Install $18,049 thousand.
 (5)
       The decrease from FY 2025 to FY 2026 is due to the addition in FY 2025 funding provided for material procurement to support the Naval Sustainment Plan - Shipyard (NSS-SY) Planning pillar in support of the CNO Navigation Plan 2022.

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                    -                 -                  -



 Description:
 LT160 - MACHINERY PLANT UPGRADES - The Machinery Plant Upgrade project within the CVN Fleet Modernization Program (FMP) encompasses CVN Machinery Control Systems, Ship Control (Navigation/
 Steering), Integrated Voice Control Networks, Announcing Systems and Shipboard Air Traffic Control Communication Systems. The machinery plant upgrade modernization alterations currently consist of
 greater than 40 different Ship Change Documents (SCD) with a cost value of approximately $42M for all the alterations combined. Individual SCDs can range in cost from $5K (for software only changes) to ~
 $14M for major system upgrade procurements. The range of SCD installs can cost as low as $100K and as high as $4M for individual SCDs. During each CNO availability, we schedule and complete a subset
 of these 40+ alterations. Each hull is different receiving varying subsets of the 40+ SCDs depending on individual ship configurations. These alts are scheduled for installation over 3-4 availabilities (which may
 be 9-12 years) with many installations occurring in later years of the FYDP. Additionally, the machinery plant upgrade program is technology driven and many alterations are still being finalized in the Navy
 modernization Program (i.e. Ship Change Document (SCD) development) process for installation in later years. New SCDs continue to be developed to proactively address obsolescence issues associated with
 ever changing technology.

 Description:
 Enhanced Oil Water Separator (OWS) systems are being installed on the CVN68 Class carriers to meet the new environmental discharge law of 15 parts per million (ppm) worldwide. The new OWS systems
 include an oil content monitor and a bilge stripping structure. The new system is expected to reduce costly maintenance and reliability issues.

                            (The following Resource Summary rows are for informational purposes only. The corresponding budget requests are documented elsewhere.)
Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -



 Description:
 Modular Refrigeration System (MRS) replaces the existing legacy Ship's Stores Refrigeration System (SSRS) onboard CVN68 Class carriers. The legacy system is energy inefficient, space inefficient, and
 maintenance intensive, requiring highly skilled personnel to operate and maintain acceptable box temperatures. Most hardware associated with the legacy systems is specialized mil-spec and is either becoming
 unsupportable due to obsolescence or is grossly expensive to maintain. Each MRS is comprised of a Modular Refrigeration Unit (MRU), an operating/diagnostic control panel, and small chilled water cooled flat-
 plate condenser. There are 18 MRUs required per hull. MRS's are self-contained hatchable MRUs with a dedicated control panel that eliminate 90% of current maintenance requirements and require minimal
 operator support/training.

 [Modular Reefer System] Modular Refrigeration System (MRS) replaces the existing legacy ship's stores refrigeration system (SSRS) onboard CVN68 class carriers. The legacy system is energy inefficient,
 space inefficient, and maintenance intensive, requiring highly skilled personnel to operate and maintain acceptable box temperatures. Most hardware associated with the legacy systems is specialized mil-spec
 and is either becoming unsupportable due to obsolescence, or is grossly expensive to maintain. Each Modular Refrigeration System (MRS) is comprised of a Modular Refrigeration Unit (MRU), an operating/
 diagnostic control panel, and small chilled water cooled flat-plate condenser. There are 18 MRUs required per hull. Modular Refrigeration Systems (MRS's) are self-contained hatchable modular refrigeration units
 (MRU) with a dedicated control panel that eliminate 90% of current maintenance requirements and require minimal operator support/training.

 Description:
 The Joint Chemical and Biological Defense Program (CBDP) funds the development and procurement of Chemical and Biological Defense (CBD) Equipment to enhance the warfighter's ability to survive and
 complete their mission in a chemical biological contaminated environment. The Navy is responsible for the associated installation/integration and sustainment funds only. The Navy's requirement for Joint
 Biological Agent Identification and Diagnostic System (JBAIDS), Aerosol Vapor Chemical Agent Detector (AVCAD), and Enhanced Maritime Biological Detection (EMBD) system has been validated by the Chief
 of Naval Operations in associated Joint Operational Requirements Documents and Capability Production Documents. The inventory objective for EMBD upgrades was revised rom one-hundred ten (110) to one-
 hundred fourteen (114) due to adding four LCS class ships because planned decommissioning has been delayed which allowed time to install the system upgrade.

 [P3A - 3 / CW001 CHEMICAL WARFARE PROGRAM EMBD UPGRADE KIT]: CHEMICAL WARFARE PROGRAM EMBD UPGRADE KIT: The Enhanced Maritime Biological Detection (EMBD) kit is intended as
 a lifecycle replacement for the Joint Biological Point Detection System (JBPDS), and will include improved detection and identification capabilities. The equipment procurement is funded out of the Joint Service
 Chemical Biological Defense Program Budget. The inventory objective for shipboard installations is 114.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request for CBD includes $2,607 thousand of discretionary and $12 thousand of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $2,619 thousand. The mandatory funds [brief description of content funded
 by mandatory funds]. Further information for this reconciliation request is provided in Section 200010 (Readiness) of the Reconciliation Exhibit.

 FY 2026 discretionary funding is for installation of equipment including Fleet Modernization Program installations, installation of training equipment, installation of equipment in other shore facilities, and
 associated required new equipment training. Procurement of equipment is funded by the Joint Service CBDP. Costs can vary significantly, even within ship class, depending on installation location (East coast,
 West coast, overseas, etc.), if installation accomplished pier side or in a shipyard (i.e., availability type during which installation actually occurs), ship as built and as found conditions, and installation growth work
 (i.e., unplanned/unexpected work) driven by as found conditions.

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $.661 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 Description:
 OPNAVINST 3400.10G articulates U.S. Navy Chemical, Biological and Radiological Defense (CBRD) policy and establishes functional responsibilities to ensure the highest level of the Fleet readiness and
 warfighting sustainability in a CBR environment. JBAIDS systems will identify and quantify biological organisms of operational concern and other pathogens of clinical significance for confirmatory and prognostic
 purposes. The CENTCOM HR urgent need (dated Aug 2009) modifies the JBAIDS ORD (dated May 2003) with the addition of detection capability for the 2009/A/H1N1 influenza. The equipment procurement is
 funded out of the Joint Service CBDP Budget P-1 Item Nomenclature: JBAIDS. Beginning in FY13, system will install with refrigeration unit.

 Description:

 warfighting sustainability in a CBR environment. The Aerosol Vapor Chemical Agent Detector (AVCAD), formerly Next Generation Chemical Detector (NGCD), provides shipboard point detection system for

                            (The following Resource Summary rows are for informational purposes only. The corresponding budget requests are documented elsewhere.)
Initial Spares ($ in Millions)                                                                          -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -
Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                                                            -                         -                         -                     -                 -                  -



 Description:
 OPNAVINST 3400.10G articulates U.S. Navy Chemical, Biological and Radiological Defense (CBRD) policy and establishes functional responsibilities to ensure the highest level of the Fleet readiness and
 warfighting sustainability in a CBR environment. The Enhanced Maritime Biological Detection (EMBD) kit is intended as a lifecycle replacement for the Joint Biological Point Detection System (JBPDS) and
 includes improved detection and identification capabilities. The equipment procurement is funded out of the Joint Service Chemical Biological Defense Program Budget. The inventory objective for shipboard
 installations is one-hundred fourteen (114). Planned installations increased by four to incorporate LCS-13 thru LCS-19 because ships are not decommissioning as previously expected.

 [CW001 CHEMICAL WARFARE PROGRAM EMBD UPGRADE KIT] Remarks: A total of seventy-five (75) complete and two started of one-hundred fourteen (114) planned. EMBD is an acquisition program
 developed and procured thru JPEO-CBRND which will provide lifecycle replacement kits for existing JBPDS. The EMBD kit installs within the existing JBPDS frame as specific line replaceable units (LRUs)
 addressing certain parts obsolescence issues and provides hardware and software upgrades to the system. EMBD kit installation timeline takes approximately six days to account for LRU removal, perform
 LRU operability assessment for LRUs remaining in the system, and perform legacy LRU refurbishment in line with each EMBD kit installation to ensure proper operation upon completion of the upgrade. Without
 performing these additional steps, the EMBD kit upgrade will operate in a less-than-optimal state, resulting in a reduced automated biological point detection and identification state.

 Footnotes:
 (1)
    FY 2026 funding supports installations on eighteen (18) ships at approximately $115K each, for a total of $2,070K, plus $228K in DSAs. The procurement costs are specifically referring to Installation and
 Design Service Allocation (DSA) costs as there are no equipment procurement costs with any detector system installed. DSA costs are for government engineering oversight/support in preparation for ship
 installation and ship class planning yards for engineering services which include ship check, development and approval of ship installation drawings (SIDs), Ship Selected Record changes, Bill of Material (BOM),
 onsite planning yard engineering support during installation to answer liaison action record (LARs), and other engineering services depending on the planning yard. DSA costs are generally funded one year prior
 to planned installation due to drawing development lead time and installation preparation efforts.

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -                -


 Description:
 Division A of the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2020, Public Law 116-93 appropriated funding in Other Procurement, Navy (OPN) to initiate a program funding private contracted ship maintenance in the U.S.
 Pacific Fleet (CPF) in Fiscal Year (FY) 2020. Division C of the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021 continued this program in the U.S. Pacific Fleet, and Division C of the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2022
 expanded the program to include both U.S. Pacific Fleet and U.S. Fleet Forces (FFC).

 FY 2026 funds $2.4B for Continental United Status (CONUS) private contracted surface and submarine maintenance in both U.S. Pacific Fleet (CPF) and U.S. Fleet Forces Command (FFC).

 Funding private contracted ship maintenance planned for CPF and FFC with OPN allows the Navy to implement commercial best practices for ship maintenance and achieve Navy's A-120 contracting policy. It
 provides more flexibility to extend contract lead times, enables the Navy to address emergent repair requirements, and strengthens industry partnerships. The program continues to provide stability and financial
 efficiency to its industry partners, and continues the more stringent fiscal discipline demonstrated in the execution of the program. The Navy remains committed and motivated to utilizing this program to improve
 both the execution of depot-level maintenance and the overall readiness posture of the U.S. Navy's maritime forces.

 AVAILABILITIES DEFINITIONS:
 CIA (Carrier Incremental Availability): An availability for continuous accomplishment of depot maintenance and selected modernization on CVN 68 class aircraft carriers.

 DMP (Depot Modernization Period): An availability scheduled primarily for the installation of major high priority warfare improvement alterations.

 DPIA (Docking Planned Incremental Availability): A labor-intensive availability, of less than one year duration, for aircraft carriers in an Incremental Maintenance Plan. Maintenance and modernization are
 accomplished. Aircraft carriers assigned to Incremental Maintenance Plans are maintained through CIA's, PIAs and DPIAs in lieu of overhauls.

 DPMA (Docking Phased Maintenance Availability): A PMA expanded to include maintenance and modernization that require dry-docking.

 DSRA(Docking Selected Restricted Availabilities): An SRA expanded to include maintenance and modernization that require dry-docking.

 EDPMA (Extended Docking Phased Maintenance Availability): A DPMA expanded to include maintenance and modernization that cannot be accomplished in a DPMA.

Reactor Plant Equipment
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                         Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                     Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A

 EDSRA (Extended Docking Selected Restricted Availability): A DSRA expanded to include maintenance and modernization that cannot be accomplished in a DSRA.

 ERP (Extended Refit Period): Selected Restricted Availability with short docking inspection period

 EOH (Engineered Overhaul): Overhaul availabilities of nuclear powered submarines during which the reactor is not refueled.

 ERO (Engineered Refueling Overhaul): Overhaul major maintenance availabilities of different nuclear powered submarine classes during which the reactor is also refueled.

 MMP (Major Maintenance Period): An onsite non-CNO availability for SSGNs for the accomplishment of maintenance and modernization.

 OH (Overhaul): A major availability normally exceeding 6-months duration for the accomplishment of maintenance and modernization.

 PIA (Planned Incremental Availability): A labor-intensive availability, of less than 6 months duration, for aircraft carriers in an Incremental Maintenance Plan. Maintenance and modernization are accomplished.
 Aircraft carriers assigned to Incremental Maintenance Plans are maintained through CIA's, PIAs and DPIAs in lieu of overhauls.

 PIRA (Pre-Inactivation Restricted Availability): A hull specific availability assigned to establish a final, abbreviated OPCYCLE prior to inactivation.

 P-MA (Phased Maintenance Availability): A short labor- intensive availability for ships in a Phased Maintenance Program for the accomplishment of maintenance and modernization. Ships assigned to Phased
 Maintenance Programs are maintained through PMAs in lieu of overhauls.

 RA/TA (Restricted/Technical Availability): RA/TA is used to provide depot level support and maintenance (ie. Combat Systems, Habitability, Calibration, Ship Engineering material assessment, Nuclear Regional
 Maintenance Facilities)

 SCO (Service Craft Overhaul): A major industrial availability for the accomplishment of maintenance and modernization on service craft.

 SRA (Selected Restricted Availability): A short labor- intensive industrial period assigned to ships in Progressive or Engineered Operating Cycle Maintenance Programs for the accomplishment of maintenance
 and selected modernization. Ships assigned to Progressive Maintenance Programs are maintained through SRAs in lieu of overhauls.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $163.538 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 FY 2026 requests $2.4 Billion for U.S. Pacific Fleet and U.S. Fleet Forces to continue private contracted ship maintenance. Funding for ship maintenance Overhauls (OH) to Restricted/Technical Availabilities
 (RA/TA) performed at private shipyards. Ship overhauls restore the ship, including all operating systems that affect safety or combat capability, to established performance standards.

 Description:
 The Other Procurement, Navy, Reactor Power Units budget provides for the procurement of reactor refueling cores including naval nuclear fuel work (and the completion of core assembly to support select SSN
 and CVN refueling). Reactor refueling cores are the assemblies of nuclear fuel and associated structural and reactivity control equipment required for the refueling of nuclear powered ships. The procurement
 of these units is accomplished by the Department of Energy (DOE) using the Economy Act of 1932, as amended and codified in 31 USC 1535. The DOE has developed production lines to fabricate these units
 at the sole remaining core manufacturer. Reactor refueling core procurements are required to support scheduled refueling of operational ships and are budgeted on a lead-time basis while preserving workload
 stability and efficiency.

 carriers (CVN), moored training ships (MTS). and fleet training equipment.

 Description:
 DIVING AND SALVAGE EQUIPMENT:
 The equipment procured from this Diving and Salvage OPN line item provides critical diving and salvage equipment that enables planned/emergent waterborne submarine/ship repairs, salvage and battle
 damage repair operations, search and recovery to water depths up to 20,000 feet of seawater and Undersea Clandestine Insertion of Special Operations Forces (UCI-SOF) (service common equipment) to keep
 our Navy fleet operational and enable national priority missions. Program objectives are to: (1) provide increased safety for divers, (2) increase underwater ship maintenance capabilities, (3) provide reliable,
 rapid response salvage capability, and (4) reduce diving equipment maintenance costs by standardizing configuration of diving systems in the Fleet.

 FY-26 investment in Diving capability continues to emphasize phased replacement of aging life support equipment including surface-supplied diving helmets (20), HP composite flask replacements (70), surface
 supplied augmented SCUBA sets (1), compact diver life support system air supplies (15) and an HP Air Compressor (1). Navy dive computers (185), required by each diver to calculate decompression penalties,
 will also be procured. This equipment is essential to maintaining diving readiness throughout the Fleet. Additionally, we will make investments in new technology equipment that offer capability gains such as
 chamber van upgrades (5) and transportable recompression chamber system chamber upgrade kits (3) and equipment which will significantly improve divers' visual acuity in dark, limited visibility operations.
 This improved visual acuity equipment includes diver augmented visual displays (DAVD) (8 Gen3). When integrated together these systems will produce a virtual environment in which a diver can operate and
 be directed by topside supervisors to execute salvage, search and recovery and other missions. Lastly, we continue to invest in upgrades to the Navy Experimental Diving Unit, which houses national assets
 such as the Ocean Simulation Facility (OSF). The OSF is a 40+ year old system that permits advanced research and testing of diving technology, diving procedures, human performance, decompression and
 physiology essential to advancing diving capabilities.

 FY-26 investment in Salvage includes a hydraulic power units (2 NEXGEN 6B), deck systems hydraulic power unit upgrade (2) all used for ship salvage. In Underwater Ship Husbandry (UWSH) we continue
 investments in cofferdam systems (1 standard), a surface ship support system (1), video/camera equipment (1), UWSH Storage and Shipping Containers (1) and a pier-side support van (1). Underwater Ship
 Husbandry investments continue to produce significant return on investment by eliminating the need for emergent dry dockings, thereby returning ships to service faster and less expensive than traditional
 shipyard availabilities.

 FY-26 investment in Naval Special Warfare Service-Common Equipment provides diving support equipment to include SCUBA diving systems (917) and ROV systems (2). 228 SCUBA diving systems and 2
 ROVs excluded from Exhibit P40a as mandated by the FY25 Reconciliation Bill.

                                                                                                                                                                                                        Volume 1 - 489

1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 01: Ships Support Equipment / BSA 11:                                    1130 / Diving and Salvage Equipment
Ocean Engineering
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                   Other Related Program Elements: 0204561N, 0204454N,
                                                                                                                                                 0204423N, 0203424N, 0603713N
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 [P40A / DIVING (N97)]: The DIVING EQUIPMENT to be procured in FY26 in the amount of $3.798M includes modernization of the Navy Experimental Diving Unit's Ocean Simulation Facility, surface supplied
 diving helmet upgrades, air storage flasks, SCUBA equipment, an HP air compressor, diver life support systems, dive computers (wrist mounted for calculating decompression requirements), transportable
 recompression chamber system upgrades, and Diver Augmented Visual Display (DAVD) equipment. DAVD investment will provide Navy divers the ability to see and navigate in zero visibility water. More
 detailed descriptions are provided below:

 [P40A / HY176 - HP AIR COMPRESSORS]: (FY26 = 1 @ $81,380) This item replaces high pressure air compressors in existing divers' life support systems (DLSS) which have reached the end of their service
 life. Required I/O is 300.

 [P40A / HY177 - AIR PURIFICATION UNITS]: The Portable Air Purification Unit is used when charging diver's life support system (DLSS) breathing air flasks. The Portable Air Monitor Unit can monitor the diver's
 breathing air in accordance with the requirements of the U.S. Navy Dive Manual and can be inserted inline of the DLSS to monitor the purity of the diver's breathing air in accordance with the requirements of the
 U.S. Navy Dive Manual. . Required I/O is 50 units.

 [P40A / HY179 - NAVY EXPERIMENTAL DIVING UNIT]: (FY26 = 1 @ $717,000) NEDU's mission is to support the Fleet diver through test and evaluation of diving equipment and procedures as well as
 hyperbaric systems for NAVSEA, Navy, and DoD activities. Funding is to procure equipment for test, facilities atmospheric control, life support, and physiological systems. These systems not only ensure the
 safety and lives of NEDU sailors performing experimental dives, but ultimately support the combat readiness and mission success of the Fleet sailors who use the equipment tested at NEDU. Funding is included
 to support the periodic upgrade of Ocean Simulation Facility (OSF) components. The OSF is the world's largest man-rated hyperbaric chamber affording space for 12 divers in 5 hyperbaric dry chambers, man-
 rated for dives to 2,250 feet of sea water (1000 psi) with a 50' x 15', 55,000-gallon wet-pot capacity, temperatures from 28 to 104 degrees Fahrenheit, an associated 1.3 million-cubic foot (37 km3) bottle field and
 uses a fully computerized data instrumentation and collection system. Unit prices vary depending on the specific equipment mix planned for upgrade in any single year.

 [P40A / HY193 - SURFACE SUPPLIED DIVING HELMET]: Kirby Morgan KM-37 NS Underwater Breathing Apparatus. This is a diving helmet with a fiberglass shell and second stage regulator that works in
 conjunction with a surface supply control console assembly (CCA) and an umbilical. Required I/O is 250.

 [P40A / HY199 - NAVY DIVE COMPUTER]: (FY26 = 185 @ $1,762 each) The Navy Dive Computer is a diver worn decompression computer which provides primarily Self Contained Underwater Breathing
 Apparatus (SCUBA) divers with real time calculation of decompression limits/obligations. I/O is 1400.

 [P40A / HY210 - COMPACT DIVER LIFE SUPPORT SYSTEM AIR SUPPLY]: (FY26 = 15 @ $22,820 each) This is a portable Air Supply that has designated primary and secondary air cylinders with a simple,
 unregulated control panel. The air supply will provide 3000-5000 psi diver-breathing air to diving life support systems (DLSS)including but not limited to the Standard Navy Double-Lock Recompression Chamber
 (SNDL), Transportable Recompression Chamber System (TRCS), Flyaway Diving System (FADS) III, and Lightweight Diving Systems (LWDS). This is for use in surface supplied diving and hyperbaric treatment
 operations. Required Inventory Objective I/O is 25.

 [P40A / HY213 - HP COMPOSITE FLASK REPLACEMENT]: (FY26 = 70 @ $5,542 each) The flasks used in the Chamber Air and Oxygen System (CAOS), Air Supply Rack Assembly (ASRA), Oxygen
 Regulating Console Assembly (OSCA), Helium-Oxygen Supply Rack Assemblies (HOSRA), Compact Air Supply Assembly (CASRA), and Compact Oxygen Supply Rack Assembly (COSRA) all require
 replacement after 15 years. NAVSEA 00C will provide the cylinders to fleet diving commands. Required Inventory Objective I/O is 2500.

 [P40A / HY224 - SCUBA REGULATORS]: This is 1st and 2nd stage Self Contained Underwater Breathing Apparatus (SCUBA) regulator fitted with an additional octopus 2nd stage and a console with both a
 cylinder pressure gauge and diver's depth gauge included. I/O is 5,000.

 [P40A / HY233 - SURFACE SUPPLY AUGMENTED SCUBA SYSTEM]: (FY26 = 1 @ $119,870) The Surface Supply Augmented SCUBA System consists of the Interspiro DIVIATOR Dive Panel (DP) Surface
 Supply System and the Interspiro DIVIATOR Self Contained Underwater Breathing Apparatus (SCUBA) System. This is a lightweight, highly portable system which makes is ideal for rapid deployment to remote
 locations. The apparatus combines the capability of surface supplied diving with the portability of SCUBA. Required Inventory Objective I/O is 50.

                                                                                                                                                 0204423N, 0203424N, 0603713N
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 [P40A / HY249 - DIVER LAUNCH AND RECOVERY SYSTEM (LARS)]: A Frame launch and recovery system for divers that includes a diver's stage. I/O = 6

 [P40A / HY254 - DIVER AUGMENTED VISUAL DISPLAY COMPUTER BASED TRAINING SYS]: Computer based training system that will provide training to USN divers in the operation and maintenance of
 the DAVD system. HY-251 and HY-254: This equipment significantly improve divers' visual acuity in dark, limited visibility operations. This improved visual acuity equipment includes sector scan SONAR (3),
 augmented visual displays (3), 3D SONAR (1) and associated computer based training hardware (1). When integrated together these systems will produce a virtual environment in which a diver can operate and
 be directed by topside supervisors to execute salvage, search and recovery and other missions. I/O = 10

 [P40A / HY271 - SURFACE SUPPLIED DIVING HELMET UPGRADE]: (FY26 = 20 @ $11,675 each) The Surface Supplied Diving Helmet Upgrade is a phase replacement for Kirby Morgan KM-37 Underwater
 Breathing Apparatus that have reached the end of their service life. This is a rugged diving helmet with a second stage regulator that works in conjunction with a surface supply control console assembly (CCA)
 and an umbilical. Required I/O is 400

 [P40A / HY272 - MULTI-OCCUPANT FLEXIBLE RECOMPRESSION CHAMBER SYSTEM]: The Multi-Occupancy Flexible Recompression Chamber System is a collapsible, extreme lightweight double-lock
 chamber for expeditionary operations. It includes a recompression chamber for up to three occupants, control console, interconnecting hoses, and high pressure gas supplies for air and oxygen. It is used to
 treat diving related injuries, including Arterial Gas Embolism and Decompression Sickness in accordance with the U.S. Navy Dive Manual. I/O is 36.

 [P40A / HY273 - CHAMBER VAN UPGRADE]: (FY26 = 5 @ $185,700 each) The Chamber Van Upgrade is a phase replacement for ISO Containers that house portable recompression chambers, which
 have reached the end of their service life due to deterioration from salt-water exposure. In order to ensure the correct level of environmental protection during all missions for the hyperbaric chamber and their
 occupants, the upgraded Vans with improved protective coatings has become a necessity and requirement. I/O is 30

 [P40A / HY274 - DIVER AUGMENTED VISUAL DISPLAY (DAVD) GEN 3]: (FY26 = 8 @ $79,500 each) The GEN 3 DAVD system will be an upgraded version of the GEN 2 DAVD, it will allow for greater
 accuracy of diver tracking and head positioning and will augment the divers underwater vision with AR/VR capability using helmet mounted high resolution cameras. I/O = 20

 [P40A / HY278 - DIVER AUGMENTED VISUAL DISPLAY (DAVD) GEN3 UPGRADE KIT]: This kit will modify a DAVD - GEN 2 system to improve it to a GEN3 system for US Navy Fleet divers.

 [P40A / HY289 - TRANSPORTABLE RECOMPRESSION CHAMBER SYSTEM CHAMBER UPGRADE KIT]: (FY26 = 3 @ $8,248 each) Transportable Recompression Chamber System Upgrade Kits for 5000psi
 service to increase the TRCS operational capability, reduce footprint and provide interface with other U.S. Navy Certified Dive Systems rated to 5000psi.

 [P40A / HY290 - DIVER AUGMENTED VISUAL DISPLAY (DAVD) 3D SONAR WITH GPC UPGRADE]: The Diver Augmented Visual Display (DAVD) 3D sonar imaging system with global positioning system
 (GPS) upgrade. This sonar produces real time high-resolution 3D imagery that can be used to create a 3D model of a large undersea area and real time sonar imagery that can be used for diver navigation.

 [P40A / NAVAL SPECIAL WARFARE (NSW) SERVICE-COMMON EQUIPMENT (N95)]: The NSW SERVICE COMMON EQUIPMENT to be procured in FY26 in the amount of $2.732M (includes $1.353M
 RECON RED funds excluded from the P40a exhibit) includes SCUBA diving systems and ROV systems. More detailed descriptions are provided below:

 [P40A / HY236 - (NSW) SERVICE COMMON STANDARD DOUBLE LOCK RECOMPRESSION CHAMBER]: The NSW Service Common Standard Navy Double Lock Recompression Chamber System
 (SNDLRCS) is a conventional chamber designed and built using standard commercial specifications and standards. The SNDLRCS provides for surface decompression, training, and for the full range of
 recompression treatment for two patients and two attendants. These chambers are containerized to allow for transport and installation at a destination for long term operations. The SNDLRCS will replace aging
 legacy recompression chambers that are difficult to maintain and will be retired due to fatigue and material flaws. Required I/O is 4

 [P40A / HY291 -(NSW) HP COMPRESSOR]: This item replaces high pressure air compressors in existing divers' life support systems which have reached the end of their service life.

 [P40A / HY293 - (NSW) HASKEL PUMPS]: This item replaces oxygen booster pumps in existing divers' life support systems which have reached the end of their service life.

 [P40A / HY294 - (NSW) NAVY DIVE COMPUTER]: The NSW Navy Dive Computer (NDC) is a decompression computer that monitors a divers depth and dive time to determine a decompression profile in
 accordance with US Navy decompression algorithms. These are to be used by Naval Special Warfare (NSW) divers and shall only be the make and model listed on the US Navy Authorized for Navy Use (ANU)
 list.

 [P40A / HY296 - (NSW) DIVER LIFE SUPPORT SYSTEM (DLSS) CHARGING VAN]: Modified ISO 8 ft x 8 ft x 10 ft ISO containers equipped to store and ship portable compressors and ancillary equipment in
 support of NSW combat diving operations. Each van is complete with a humidity controlling device for prolonging equipment life during storage. The system includes all necessary rigging and handling equipment.

 [P40A / HY297 - (NSW) STANDARD DOUBLE LOCK RECOMPRESSION CHAMBER VAN UPGRADE]: These MILVANS are modified ISO 8 ft x 8 ft x 20 ft ISO containers equipped to store and ship portable
 recompression chambers and ancillary equipment in support of Naval Special Warfare (NSW) combat diving operations. Each van is complete with a humidity controlling device for prolonging equipment life
 during storage.

 [P40A / HY307 - (NSW) DIVING SUPPORT EQUIPMENT SCUBA DIVING SYSTEMS]: (FY26 = 917 @ $2,001 each) (includes 228 systems excluded from P40a mandated by the FY25 Reconciliation Bill). This
 supports the SCUBA systems Table of Allowance (TOA) requirements for life-cycle replacement and/or upgrades to existing systems within the SCUBA TOA assembly.

 [P40A / HY308 - (NSW) DIVING SUPPORT EQUIPMENT ROV SYSTEM]: (FY26 = 2 @ $450,000 each) (excluded from P40a mandated by the FY25 Reconciliation Bill). This supports the Table of Allowance
 (TOA) and other non-TOA diving requirements for the remotely operated vehicle (ROV) system to include ROV systems, tools, initial spares and testing as required to support fielding. This line also supports life-
 cycle upgrade of existing and procurement of new systems as required.

 [P40A / HY309 - (NSW) MK25 REPLACEMENT]: This supports the Table of Allowance (TOA) for the MK25 Closed Circuit Rebreather life-cycle replacement to include MK25 systems, tools, initial spares and
 testing as required to support fielding.

 [P40A / MOBILE DIVING SALVAGE UNIT (MDSU) OUTFITTING EQUIPMENT (N95)]: The MDSU EQUIPMENT to be procured in FY26 in the amount of $0.339M provides prioritized outfitting for Mobile Diving
 Salvage Unit (MDSU), Explosive Ordinance Disposal (EOD) Commands and Underwater Construction Team (UCT) Table of Allowance (TOA) requirements of centrally managed diving systems and equipment.
 This equipment includes diving, salvage and combat support equipment to meet Required Operational Capabilities / Projected Operational Environment (ROC/POE) and Navy Mission Essential Tasks (NMET) to
 include underwater visual augmentation systems, dive computers and Self-Contained Underwater Breathing Apparatus (SCUBA) regulators. More detailed descriptions are provided below:

 [P40A / HY262 - (MDSU) DIVER AUGMENTED VISUAL DISPLAY (DAVD) GEN 3 UPGRADE KIT]: This kit will modify a DAVD - GEN 2 system to improve it to a GEN 3 system for Navy Expeditionary Combat
 Command divers.

 [P40A / HY263 - (MDSU) SELF CONTAINED UNDERWATER BREATHING APPARATUS REGULATORS]: (FY26 = 7 @ $1,000 each) These a self contained underwater breathing apparatus (SCUBA)
 regulators that are used to reduce air pressure in a SCUBA cylinder and deliver that air at a useable pressure to a Navy Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC) diver. The make and model of the SCUBA
 regulator shall be approved on the US Navy Authorized for Navy Use (ANU) list.

 [P40A / HY264 - (MDSU) NAVY DIVE COMPUTER]: (FY26 = 8 @ $1,740 each) This is a diver decompression computer that monitors a divers depth and dive time to determine a decompression profile in
 accordance with US Navy decompression algorithms. These are to be used by Navy Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC) divers and shall only be the make and model listed on the US Navy Authorized for
 Navy Use (ANU) list.

 [P40A / HY277 - (MDSU) DIVER AUGMENTED VISUAL DISPLAY (DAVD) GEN 3]: (FY26 = 4 @ $79,572 each) The Gen-3 DAVD system will be an upgraded version of the GEN-2 DAVD, it will allow for greater
 accuracy of diver tracking and head positioning and will augment the divers underwater vision with AR/VR capability using helmet mounted high resolution cameras. I/O = 20

 [P40A / HY298 - (MDSU) DAVD 3D SONAR WITH GPS UPGRADE]: The 3D sonar imaging system with global positioning system (GPS) upgrade. Sonar produces real time high-resolution 3D imagery that can
 be used to create a 3D model of a large undersea area. The system is also capable of producing real time sonar imagery that can be over-laid onto a previously produced 3D model and used for diver locating
 and navigation controlled by a topside control system. These are to be used by Navy Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC) divers.

 [P40A / HY306 - (MDSU) NAVY DIVE COMPUTER LO MU]: This is a diver decompression computer that monitors a divers depth and dive time to determine a decompression profile in accordance with US
 Navy decompression algorithms and have a low magnetic signature. These are to be used by Navy Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC) divers and shall only be the make and model listed on the US Navy
 Authorized for Navy Use (ANU) list.

 [P40A / EXPLOSIVE ORDNANCE DISPOSAL (EOD) DIVING & SALVAGE EQUIPMENT (N95)]: The EOD EQUIPMENT to be procured in FY26 in the amount of $5.367M (includes $4.034M RECON RED funds
 excluded from the P40a exhibit) provides prioritized outfitting for Explosive Ordinance Disposal (EOD) Commands. The outfitting provides diving, salvage and combat support equipment to meet Navy Mission
 Essential Tasks (NMET) to include a standard navy double-lock recompression chamber, SCUBA equipment, dive computers, diver augmented visual display 3D sonar, and a diver launch and recovery system.

 [P40A / HY279 - (EOD) DIVER TELEMTRY SYSTEM]: Diver HUD System (EOD) - The system provides the diver with a heads up display (HUD) which incorporates various data for the diver to include inputs
 such as status, telemetry, terrain mapping, navigation and tracking. The system is provided for use in existing EOD underwater breathing apparatus (UBA) or face masks. I/O = 200.

 [P40A / HY300 - (EOD) TRANSPORTABLE RECOMPRESSION CHAMBER SYSTEM (TRCS)]: The Transportable Recompression Chamber System (TRCS) provides an effective two-man evacuation, transport,
 treatment, and transfer under pressure capability in order to benefit a diver suffering a pressure related ailment requiring urgent hyperbaric treatment. This is the lightest, most transportable hyperbaric system
 available to the U. S. Navy. These are to be used by Navy Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC) divers. I/O = 7

 [P40A / HY301 - (EOD) STANDARD NAVY DOUBLE-LOCK RECOMPRESSION CHAMBER (SNDL)]: (FY26 = 1 @ $1,958,000) (excluded from P40a mandated by the FY25 Reconciliation Bill) The Standard
 Navy Double Lock Recompression Chamber System (SNDLRCS) is a conventional chamber designed and built using standard commercial specifications and standards. The SNDLRCS provides for surface
 decompression, training, and for the full range of recompression treatment for two patients and two attendants. These chambers are containerized to allow for transport and installation at a destination for long
 term operations. The SNDLRCS will replace aging legacy recompression chambers that are difficult to maintain and will be retired due to fatigue and material flaws. These are to be used by Navy Expeditionary
 Combat Command (NECC) divers. I/O = 3

 [P40A / HY305 - (EOD) DIVING SUPPORT EQUIPMENT SCUBA DIVING SYSTEMS (NECC)]: (FY26 = 2 @ $1,088,000 each) (excluded from P40a exhibit mandated by the FY25 Reconciliation Bill) This
 equipment package provides for auxiliary and supporting dive equipment to outfit Underwater Construction Team detachments tables of allowance (TOA) requirements.

 [P40A / HY310 - (EOD) NAVY DIVE COMPUTER LO MU]: (FY26 = 218 @ $3,663 each) This is a diver decompression computer that monitors a divers depth and dive time to determine a decompression profile
 in accordance with US Navy decompression algorithms and have a low magnetic signature. These are to be used by Navy Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC) divers and shall only be the make and model
 listed on the US Navy Authorized for Navy Use (ANU) list.

 [P40A / HY311 - (EOD) DAVD 3D SONAR WITH GPC UPGRADE]: (FY26 = 1 @ $143,780) The 3D sonar imaging system with global positioning system (GPS) upgrade. Sonar produces real time high-
 resolution 3D imagery that can be used to create a 3D model of a large undersea area. The system is also capable of producing real time sonar imagery that can be over-laid onto a previously produced 3D
 model and used for diver locating and navigation controlled by a topside control system.

 [P40A / HY312 - (EOD) NAVY DIVE COMPUTER]: (FY26 = 1 @ $45,000) This is a diver decompression computer that monitors a diver's depth and dive time to determine a decompression profile in accordance
 with US Navy decompression algorithms. These are to be used by Navy Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC) divers and shall only be the make and model listed on the US Navy Authorized for Navy Use
 (ANU) list.

 [P40A / HY313 - (EOD) DIVER LAUNCH AND RECOVERY SYSTEM (LARS)]: (FY26 = 1 @ $344,860) Frame launch and recovery system for divers that includes a diver's stage.

 [P40A / SALVAGE (N97)]: The SALVAGE EQUIPMENT to be procured in FY26 in the amount of $.993M includes salvage tow systems, pumps, hydraulic equipment, deep ocean salvage systems, a crane,
 salvage equipment transport and landing craft, a forklift, and fire-fighting equipment. Also included are underwater ship husbandry equipment (cofferdam systems, a submarine support system, surface ship
 support system, propeller repair kit, power tools and rigging equipment,) to permit divers to make underwater repairs to ships and submarines afloat. More detailed descriptions are provided below:

 [P40A / HY131 - REMOTE OPERATED VEHICLES (ROV) HANDLING SYSTEM]: These systems are used to launch and recover remotely operated vehicles and to tend the deployed cable, compensate for ship
 motion, monitor cable tension, and store cable. Required I/O is 10 (5 operational and 5 spares).

 [P40A / HY145 - COFFERDAM SYSTEM]: (FY26 = 1 @ $129,000 each) This system will contain a variety of cofferdams necessary to accomplish underwater repair tasks to hull plating, shafts, stern tubes and
 sea chests on several ship classes. The cofferdams are engineered structural habitats which provide a safe underwater dry environment for divers to work and require very little maintenance. Required I/O is 50.

 [P40A / HY190 - VIDEO/CAMERA EQUIPMENT]: (FY26 = 1 @ $125,000) Underwater video/camera equipment used by divers to perform detailed inspections and provide objective quality evidence (OQE) of
 ship hulls and appendages condition before, during and after repair. Equipment is used extensively throughout the Fleet. This equipment will replace aging systems currently in use throughout the Fleet. I/O is 30.

 [P40A / HY197 - UNDERWATER SHIP HUSBANDRY (UWSH) PIERSIDE SUPPORT VANS]: (FY26 = 1 @ $245,000) Portable milvans and shippable containers outfitted with general and special purpose tools
 to support various underwater ship husbandry operations. I/O is 12.

 [P40A / HY198 - UNDERWATER SHIP HUSBANDRY (UWSH) SURFACE SHIP SUPPORT SYSTEMS]: (FY26 = 1 @ $153,000) Special purpose underwater tools used by divers to perform routine and
 emergent repairs to all Classes of surface ships. I/O is 32

 [P40A / HY200 - DEEP DRONE UPGRADE]: Modernization of the Remote Operated Vehicle (ROV) DEEP DRONE to include power, telemetry, avionics, thrusters, camera systems, and tools.

 [P40A / HY202 - 300/400 AMP PORTABLE ELECTRIC WELDER]: System consists of a portable electric powered welder, welding cables, a stinger and ground clamp, welding safety equipment, and a spare
 parts kit. The system is used for arc welding and oxygen-arc cutting. Required Inventory Objective I/O is 22.

 [P40A / HY204 - 400 AMP WELDER-DIESEL]: System consists of a skid-mounted, portable, diesel powered welding generator, welding cables, a stinger and ground clamp, welding safety equipment, and a
 spare parts kit. The system is used for arc welding and oxygen-arc cutting. Required Inventory Objective I/O is 27.

 [P40A / HY205 - LIGHTING KIT SYSTEM]: System consists of extended able masts, 500-watt halogen floodlights, drop lights, extension cords, spare bulbs, and other miscellaneous items to support general
 lighting for salvage and oil pollution operations. The Lighting Kit System can use any 100-volt, single phase power source found at the salvage or pollution site. Required Inventory Objective I/O is 27.

 [P40A / HY206 - KERRIE CABLE UNDERWATER CUTTING SYSTEM]: System is used for underwater cutting. The kit contains a flexible thermic lance, oxygen hoses, an oxygen regulator, various fittings, and a
 400-amp safety switch enclosed in a watertight box. Required Inventory Objective I/O is 24.

 [P40A / HY215 - LIGHTWEIGHT MOORING SYSTEM]: This is a lightweight and highly portable system for establishing a mooring in shallow to deep water. The system shall include a mooring leg consisting of
 anchors, chain, synthetic line and associated jewelry and fittings. Total I/O is 8.

 [P40A / HY221 - ADVANCED POWER GENERATOR]: This system generates electrical power using solar and wind energy and stores it in super-efficient. Lithium Ion Batteries. The system is capable of
 supplying electrical power to operate a Command Van, Salvage Shop Van and a Salvage Battle Damage Repair (BDR) Rigging Van for up to 5 days with no traditional petroleum fueled electrical power generator
 support. I/O is 8.

 [P40A / HY227 - COMPLEX AND/OR LARGE UWSH REPAIR COFFERDAM SUPPORT SYSTEMS]: Special purpose underwater tools, equipment, cofferdams and habitat with a higher complexity and or size
 used by divers to perform routine and emergent repairs on all classes of surface ship and submarines. I/O is 15.

 [P40A / HY228 - CLYDE WINCH UPGRADE MODIFICATIONS]: Portable Clyde deck winch consists of a dual drum assembly and a horizontal capstan mounted on the side of the deck winch. The deck winch is
 hydraulically driven by a self contained diesel engine and each drum/ capstan can pull up to 8 tons. The drive mechanism and braking system is upgraded to a hydraulic control and operation. Required I/O is
 20.

 [P40A / HY230 - NEXGEN HYDRAULIC POWER UNIT, MOD 6B]: (FY26 = 2 @ $112,000 each) This Hydraulic Power Unit is designed to provide the hydraulic fluids to operate US Navy Salvage equipment
 including the Beach Gear system. The hydraulic power unit is silenced for significant personnel safety and meets the latest environmental standards for clean air. Required I/O is 60.

 [P40A / HY232 - DECK SYSTEMS HYDRAULIC POWER UNIT UPGRADE]: (FY26 = 2 @ $42,000 each) Deck Systems Hydraulic Power Unit (HPU) Upgrade: The Hydraulic Power Units the provide hydraulic
 fluid to operate the Portable Capstan are at the end of their service life and are no longer supported by the manufacturer. New HPU is more reliable, simpler to maintain, and is sound silenced for enhanced
 personnel safety. Required I/O is 22.

 [P40A / HY237 - COMMAND CONFERENCE CENTER]: Provides a large expandable office area that is attached to the Salvage Command Van or can operate independently. Space will be environmentally
 controlled, able to be quickly unpacked with hard sides, and when repacked the shipping foot print will be a 20 ft connex box. Required I/O is 8.

 [P40A / HY241 - DEEP OCEAN SALVAGE SYSTEM]: Heave compensated lift system with power unit and ancillary equipment for executing deep water recovery operations. Required I/O is 2.

 [P40A / HY247 - HYDRAULLIC CHAIN HOIST (25 TON)]: This system provides a means to lift loads utilizing rigging points under water. These Hydraulic Chain Hoists are specially designed for underwater use
 and are sized to safely lift 25 tons. Required I/O is 10.

 [P40A / HY248 - HYDRAULIC CHAIN HOIST (50 TON)]: This system provides a means to lift loads utilizing rigging points under water. These Hydraulic Chain Hoists are specially designed for underwater use
 and are sized to safely lift 50 tons. Required I/O is 15.

 [P40A / HY265 - POWER GENERATOR 200 KW]: Large diesel driven power generation set capable of providing power to operate SUPSALV Remote Operated Vehicle (ROV) systems. Forward deployed at all
 Emergency Ship Salvage Material (ESSM) Facilities to ensure ROV Systems always have access to redundant source of power to support USN contingency operational requirements. I/O is 8

 [P40A / HY266 - 600 CFM SALVAGE AIR COMPRESSOR (TIER 4)]: Air compressor, diesel driven, with volume capable of pressurizing ship compartments for dewatering and able to support other fleet salvage
 operations requiring large volumes of pressurized air. I/O is 8

 [P40A / HY267 - DIAMOND WIRE SAW CUTTING SYSTEM]: Salvage cutting systems designed to cut small to medium ship hulls using a simple diamond impregnated wire. Used for Harbor clearance
 operations or any fleet operation which requires ship hull sections to be cut. I/O is 4

 [P40A / HY268 - SALVAGE HYDRAULIC CUTTER SYSTEM]: Hydraulically operated tools for cutting or moving metal hull pieces, piping, cabling, etc. during salvage operations, harbor clearance ops, or any
 fleet operations in which removing of interference items are needed. I/O is 8

 [P40A / HY269 - SALVAGE FIRE FIGHTING KIT]: Fire-fighting Kit designed to be used with the Salvage Jetting Pump system and provides a Fire Fighting Monitor, auxiliary equipment for foam, and other
 equipment for salvage team to fire a shipboard fire from the pier or Vessel of Opportunity (VOO). I/O is 8

 [P40A / HY282 - SALVAGE ROLLER BAG]: Salvage roller systems designed to roll small to medium ship hulls using a simple diamond impregnated bags. Used for Sarbor clearance operations or any fleet

 [P40A / HY283 - MAGNETIC PATCH KIT UPGRADES]: Patch Kit Upgrade adds the latest technology of patching materials for salvors to utilize in combating flooding casualties due to combat, grounding, or
 collision. Patching material encompass a wide variety of methodologies to ensure salvors have a complete selection of options to respond to a wide variety of scenarios.

 [P40A / HY284 - PIER/VOO FIRE FIGHTING SYSTEMS]: Fire Fighting System is an independant complete system for set up on a Pier or Vessel of Opportunity (VOO) for applying large volumes of water onto a
 ship for shore facility to combat a major casualty involving fire. Systems include energy source, pumps hoses, monitors, and control consoles needed to fight a major casualty.

 [P40A / HY285 - TOWING KIT]: Equipment basket to conduct emergent tows for Naval vessels. Kit would contain certified bridles and tow jewelry required for those tows to allow rapid deployment. I/O is 7.

 [P40A / HY299 - UNDERWATER SHIP HUSBANDRY (UWSH) STORAGE & SHIPPING CONTAINERS]: (FY26 1 @ $33,000) - Special purpose portable storage and shipping containers to support various
 complex underwater ship husbandry equipment and/or rigging operational kits. Each container provides controlled storage and a secure shipping footprint. I/O is 60

 [P40A / HY302 - AUV SPARE VEHICLE]: Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) for conducting search down to 20,000 feet for critical DOD assets recovery and mapping of debris field. 2nd vehicle to support
 critical contingency operation to ensure mission success.

 [P40A / HY314 -SPARE AUV HANDLING SYSTEM]: Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) Handling System to launch, recover, and house AUV. Required for conducting search operations down to 20,000
 feet for critical DOD assets recovery and mapping of debris field. 2nd AUV Handling System to support critical contingency operations to ensure mission success.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request for Diving and Salvage Equipment includes $7,842 thousand of discretionary and $5,387 thousand of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $13,229 thousand. The mandatory funds
 provide 228 scuba diving systems and two (2) diving support remotely operated vehicle systems for Naval Special Warfare and one (1) Standard Navy Double-Lock Recompression Chamber and two (2) scuba
 diving systems for the EOD community. Further information for this reconciliation request is provided in Section 200010 (Readiness) of the Reconciliation Exhibit.

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $.014 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 Footnotes:
 (1)
    HY131 - A deep ocean salvage mission specific ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle) LARS (Launch and Recovery System). This modernized ROV handling system will accommodate capability increases in ROV max depth and vehicle
 horsepower. The LARS will have a smaller logistics footprint [as compared to obsolete legacy program systems] allowing the added option of commercial air shipment which improves global rapid response and cost effectiveness in support of
 a sustained high salvage OPTEMPO, especially in Westpac.

 Description:
 Naval Sea Systems Command (NAVSEA) -- Boats are procured to fill allowances established by CNO and NAVSEA and to replace boats now in service which are beyond economical repair at shore activities
 and aboard ships. Total inventory objectives change based on Fleet requirements.

 H0001 BASE OPERATING FORCES

 Force Protection Boat (Small) - Light gasoline twin outboard engine powered (up to 150 HP each) aluminum boats from 7 to 8.2 meters (24 to 27 ft) in length used primarily for fleet force protection, maritime
 interdiction, law enforcement operations, at Naval activities and adjacent ports and waterways duties. Can operate in areas where the environment (sea states/climatology) does not present a significant
 challenge. Service life is 7 years.

 CNIC Force Protection Boat (Small) - Gasoline twin outboard engine aluminum boats approximately 8.5 meters (27 ft) in length with a narrow beam to aid in transport. Used primarily for fleet security, force
 protection/anti-terrorism, maritime interdiction and law enforcement operations at Naval activities and adjacent ports and waterways. The boats are provided with shouldering capabilities and foundations suitable
 for weapons. The boats operate primarily at Naval activities in sheltered harbors and waterways. Service life is 7 years.

 CNIC Force Protection Boat (Medium) - Gasoline outboard engine powered aluminum boats approximately 10 meters (33 ft) in length used primarily for fleet force protection, maritime interdiction, law
 enforcement operations at Naval activities and adjacent ports and waterways duties. Needed for operations in areas where the environment (sea states/climatology) is significant enough to necessitate the larger
 boat and resultant larger engines to meet the performance/operational requirements. Service life is 7 years.

 CNIC Force Protection Boat (Large) - Gasoline twin outboard engine powered aluminum boats approximately 11 meters (36 ft) in length with a wide beam used primarily for fleet force protection, maritime
 interdiction, law enforcement operations, at Naval activities and adjacent ports and waterways duties. Needed in areas where the environment (sea states/climatology) necessitates a larger boat for
 dependability. Service life is 7 years.

 CNIC Workboat (Medium) - Diesel inboard-engine powered steel boats up to approximately 11 meters (36 ft) in length used primarily for multi-purpose workboat applications at Naval activities and adjacent
 ports and waterways duties, such as towing and pusher boat capabilities, line handling, harbor cleaning, and security barrier tending. Habitability, maneuverability and bollard pull power are commensurate
