 with operational requirements associated with barrier and moderately sized vessel assist (i.e., barges, boats, etc.). The Workboat (Medium) is capable of performing operations in areas where the operating
 environment (sea states/climatology) is not significant enough to necessitate the larger boat, but includes the capabilities necessary to meet operational requirements. Service life is 20 years.

 CNIC Workboat (Large) - Diesel inboard-engine powered steel boats over approximately 11 meters (36 ft) in length used primarily for multi-purpose workboat applications at Naval activities and adjacent ports
 and waterways. Duties include towing and pusher tugboat capabilities, diver support, ship's husbandry, cargo carrier, personnel ferry, harbor cleaning, firefighting and security barrier tending. Habitability,
 maneuverability and bollard pull power are commensurate with operational requirements associated with large sized vessel assist (i.e., ships, derricks, etc.). The Workboat (Large) is needed for operations in
 areas where the operating environment (including sea states/climatology) is significant enough to necessitate the larger boat and include the capabilities necessary to meet operational requirements. Service life
 is 25 years.

 Arizona Memorial Ferry - The primary mission requirement for the Arizona Memorial ferry boats is to provide safe, reliable shuttle service between the Visitors Center and the Arizona Memorial. Secondary
 missions for the ferry boats include an average of one round trip per day for re-enlistment ceremonies, one or two round trips per day for additional Navy tours (special events, holiday ceremonies, harbor lights
 tours, etc.) and normal daily transit to and from stowage. Ferry service is provided 362 days per year.

 Utility Boats - 25 ft boats used for OSR operations, working in conjunction with Boom Platforms (BPs) and Rapid Response Skimmers (RRS). Typical mission requires UB and BP to sprint to the oil spill location
 ahead of the RRS. After spill assessment, 24" response boom (aboard BP) is tied off to UB, which is used to payout/deploy boom to surround spill while the RRS recovers the oil. UB crew tends boom during oil
 skimming operations, accessing boom over low gunwales or via dive door on UB. Typical mission duration is 3-8 hours. Service life is 10 years.

 H0002 AMPHIBIOUS ASSAULT SHIPS

 7M (24 ft) Rigid Inflatable Boat (RIB) - Diesel powered, primarily used as ship's lifeboats, search and rescue boats, liberty boats, and for general transportation on auxiliaries, combatants, carriers, amphibious,
 and shore activities. Also used for Anti-Terrorism/Force Protection (AT/FP) and Maritime Interdiction Operation/Vessel, Boarding, Search and Seizure (MIO/VBSS) operations. Service life is 12 years.

 11M (36 ft) RIB - Carried as a ship's boat or assigned to a shore activity to perform a variety of operations including personnel and light cargo transfer, anchorage administration AT/FP operations and swimmer
 defense, visit/boarding/search and maritime interdiction, Amphibious Assault Vehicle (AAV) safety boat and Advanced Amphibious Assault Vehicle (AAAV) assist boat. Service life is 12 years.

 H0003 EXPLOSIVE ORDNANCE DISPOSAL FORCES

 Explosive Ordnance Disposal (EOD) Support Craft RIB - Used for area search, MK 5 and MK 16 Underwater Breathing Apparatus (UBA)/diving training, mammal operations, ordnance/mine recovery, parachute
 insertion support and command and control. Service life is 10 years.

 11M (36 ft) EOD RIB - The 11M RIBs are required to support the emergent establishment of an enduring MK 18 unmanned underwater vehicle family of systems capability for near term operational missions
 in the shallow water and very shallow water environments in the Fifth Fleet area of responsibility. Operational missions include intelligence preparation of the operational environment/mine countermeasure
 missions. Service life is 7 to 10 years.

 H0004 NAVAL SPECIAL WARFARE (NSW)

 NSW Short Range Support Craft - Used in support of combat swimmer-diver training evolutions and the Special Warfare Combatant Craft (SWCC) basic crewman training curriculum. Service life is 10 years.

 NSW Long Range Support Craft - SEAL combat swimmer/SEAL Delivery Vehicle (SDV)/surface swimmer safety craft for offshore/open ocean training support. Provides transportation to/from training areas, dive
 supervisor/event officer-in-charge/corpsman safety support platform and injured diver/swimmer egress platform for Naval Special Warfare. Service life is 10 years.

 NSW RIB (formerly Enhanced Visit Board Search and Seizure (VBSS)) - Provide operational commander a capability to conduct VBSS and Maritime Interception Operations (MIO) from an Afloat Staging Base in
 conjunction with an overwatch craft and other friendly forces. Service life is 7 years.

 H0005 RIVERINE ACTIVITIES

 40 Patrol Boat (40PB) - The craft will support maritime security operation missions and tasks such as harbor approach defense; high value unit transit escort operations; VBSS operations and overwatch; critical
 infrastructure protection; amphibious operations support; riverine operations; mine warfare support; embarked forces support (e.g., Special Operations Forces (SOF), EOD, United States Coast Guard (USCG)
 Law Enforcement Detachment (LEDET); and, Humanitarian Assistance/Disaster Relief (HA/DR) support. Twin diesel engine powered boat with a nominal length of 40 ft used to patrol green water littoral zones
 such as coastal water approaches, bays, major rivers, ports, and harbors. Service life is 15 to 20 years.

 Coastal Riverine Force (CRF) Boat Simulator - Funding will be used to procure integrated virtual training simulators. These virtual simulators will provide training for MK VI and 40PB coxswains, navigators,
 engineers, and weapons personnel.

 Riverine Multi-Mission Craft - Provides the Navy with, but not limited to, high value unit Escort, maritime infrastructure protection; coastal riverine security; VBSS, VBSS overwatch and/or mine warfare support.
 Service life is 8 years.

 MK VI Patrol Boat - Naval Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC) Riverine Forces require a tactically sized, highly reliable, combatant craft capable of operating from land or maritime platforms. The mission
 of the MK VI is to provide the operational commander a capability to persistently patrol shallow littoral areas beyond sheltered harbors and bays, and into less sheltered open water out to the Departure Sea
 Area (DSA) for the purpose of force protection of friendly and coalition forces and critical infrastructure. Mission profiles may include: patrol of Maritime Pre-positioning Force (MPF) and Joint Logistics Over the
 Shore (JLOTS) anchorage and marshaling areas, offshore economic infrastructure Gulf Oil Platforms (GOPLATs), High-Value Asset (HVA) shipping escort, over watch of MIO/VBSS operations, Theater Security
 Cooperation (TSC), and Security Force Assistance (SFA). Service life is 7 to 10 years.

 H0006 SUPPORT FORCES

 7M (24 ft) RIB - Diesel powered, primarily used as ship's lifeboats, search and rescue boats, liberty boats, and for general transportation on auxiliaries, combatants, carriers, amphibious, and shore activities.
 Also used for AT/FP and MIO/VBSS operations. Service life is 12 years.

 H0007 FLEET LOGISTICS SUPPORT

 7M (24 ft) RIB - Diesel powered, primarily used as ship's lifeboats, search and rescue boats, liberty boats, and for general transportation on auxiliaries, combatants, carriers, amphibious, and shore activities.
 Also used for AT/FP and MIO/VBSS operations. Service life is 12 years.

 H0008 NAVAL COASTAL WARFARE

 Patrol Boat (Small) - Light gasoline twin outboard engine powered (up to 150 HP each) aluminum boats from 7 to 8.2 meters (24 to 27 ft) in length used primarily for fleet force protection, maritime interdiction, law
 enforcement operations, at Naval activities and adjacent ports and waterways duties. Can operate in areas where the environment (sea states/climatology) does not present a significant challenge. Service life is
 7 years.

 Patrol Boat (Large) - Twin diesel engine powered aluminum boats over 9 meters (30 ft) in length used primarily for fleet force protection, maritime interdiction, law enforcement operations, at Naval activities and
 adjacent ports and waterways duties. Needed in areas where the environment (sea states/climatology) necessitates a larger boat for dependability. Too heavy to meet the performance/operational requirements
 with outboard engines. Service life is 7 years.

 H0009 SUBMARINE WARFARE

 Dive Boat Replacement - Aluminum hull from 50 to 60 ft boat used in operations involving diving or the need to deploy support equipment at or near the water. Examples include dive operations focusing on
 underwater ships husbandry of Fleet assets, training, underwater survey and RDT&E, as well as, general ports and waterways operations, routine harbor maintenance and cleanup duties, and to assist in patrol,
 rescue, firefighting and picket operations. Service life: 15 to 20 years.

 Range Support Craft - Workboat type vessel to serve US Navy Weapons Systems Training and Validation, assisting fleet operations conducting acoustical, thermal and cross-section measurements and
 testing. Hulls to be steel or aluminum with aluminum superstructure and corrosion resistant systems, components and hardware to operate in industrial conditions with minimal maintenance. Sea kindliness
 underway and while loitering for extended periods are among the primary operational requirements. A large open workspace on the after deck with a retrieval ramp providing access to the water and appropriate
 weapons handling machinery. Weapons systems equipment to be handled includes missiles, torpedoes, surface and air launched Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) and targets. Minimal freeboard height
 aft is necessary for crew safety while accessing and operating the weapons retrieval mechanism without compromising stability. The boat must be able to accommodate extreme loading conditions (i.e., from
 a full weapons load topside to light load). The boat must be as stable and as wide as possible to provide the inherent stability characteristics necessary to perform the operations and necessary to provide
 ergonomics for the crew and passengers. Requirements include communications, navigation and other electronics systems necessary to support the sophisticated training, validation and recording of specialized
 measurements to support a wide variety of operations. A Commercial Off The Shelf (COTS) boat built to recognize commercial standards can accomplish specific operational requirements for the mission.
 Service Life is 25 years.

 H0010 POLLUTION CONTROL EQUIPMENT

 Oil Boom Platform - 30 ft Boom Platform (BP) Boat used for Oil Spill Response (OSR) operations, working in conjunction with 25 ft Utility Boats (UBs) and Rapid Response Skimmers (RRS). Typical mission
 requires UB and BP to sprint to the oil spill location ahead of the RRS. After spill assessment, 24" response boom (aboard BP) is tied off to UB, which is used to payout/deploy boom to surround spill while the
 RRS recovers the oil. BP crew delivers and unravels the boom during oil skimming operations. Typical mission duration is 3-8 hours. Service life is 10 years.

 Utility Boats - 25 ft boats used for OSR operations, working in conjunction with Boom Platforms (BPs) and Rapid Response Skimmers (RRS). Typical mission requires UB and BP to sprint to the oil spill location
 ahead of the RRS. After spill assessment, 24" response boom (aboard BP) is tied off to UB, which is used to payout/deploy boom to surround spill while the RRS recovers the oil. UB crew tends boom during oil
 skimming operations, accessing boom over low gunwales or via dive door on UB. Typical mission duration is 3-8 hours. Service life is 10 years.

 Rapid Response Skimmers - 30 ft boats used for OSR operations, working in conjunction with Boom Platforms (BPs) and Utility Boats (UBs). Typical mission requires UB and BP to sprint to the oil spill location
 ahead of the RRS. After spill assessment, 24" response boom (aboard BP) is tied off to UB, which is used to payout/deploy boom to surround spill while the RRS recovers the oil. The RRS "skims" the oil off of
 the top of the water and holds it in an onboard storage container for proper disposal and processing. Typical mission duration is 3-24 hours. Service life is 10 years.

 H0011 WARFARE INTEGRATION

 Workboat (Medium) - Diesel inboard-engine powered steel boats up to approximately 11 meters (36 ft) in length used primarily for multi-purpose workboat applications at Naval activities and adjacent ports and
 waterways duties, such as towing and pusher boat capabilities, line handling, harbor cleaning, and security barrier tending. Habitability, maneuverability and bollard pull power are commensurate with operational
 requirements associated with barrier and moderately sized vessel assist (i.e., barges, boats, etc.). The Workboat (Medium) is capable of performing operations in areas where the operating environment (sea
 states/climatology) is not significant enough to necessitate the larger boat, but includes the capabilities necessary to meet operational requirements. Service life is 20 years.

 H0012 TRAINING ACTIVITIES

 11M (36 ft) RIB - Carried as a ship's boat or assigned to a shore activity to perform a variety of operations including personnel and light cargo transfer, anchorage administration AT/FP operations and swimmer
 defense, visit/boarding/search and maritime interdiction, Amphibious Assault Vehicle (AAV) safety boat and Advanced Amphibious Assault Vehicle (AAAV) assist boat. Service life is 12 years.

 CNIC Force Protection Boat (Medium) - Gasoline outboard engine powered aluminum boats approximately 10 meters (33 ft) in length used primarily for fleet force protection, maritime interdiction, law
 enforcement operations at Naval activities and adjacent ports and waterways duties. Needed for operations in areas where the environment (sea states/climatology) is significant enough to necessitate the larger
 boat and resultant larger engines to meet the performance/operational requirements. Service life is 7 years.

 Naval Diving Salvage Training Command (NDSTC) Utility Boat - Used for training EOD technicians, Joint Service and Fleet Operators in support of area search, MK 5 and MK 16 Underwater Breathing
 Apparatus (UBA)/diving training, mammal operations, ordnance/mine recovery, parachute insertion support and command and control. Service life is 10 years.

 H0830 PRODUCTION ENGINEERING

 Production Engineering funds are used for engineering and technical support of boat and combatant craft acquisition and the development of integrated logistics support documentation to include technical
 data packages, provisioning, technical manuals, preventative maintenance documents and naval training systems plans. Technical support shall include acceptance trials, test and evaluation, inspections of
 production facilities, methods and quality assurance procedures and processes during and after construction. Additionally, official Navy boat inventory management, pre-delivery and post-delivery inspections
 and preparing documentation regarding boat and combatant craft procurement contract closeout and may be accomplished. Boats will not be safe or suitable to perform Navy boat and combatant craft missions
 if acquisition engineering technical support and integrated logistics support are not funded and completed. Boats will not be properly accounted for in accordance with the Department of Navy's instruction
 4780.6G if boat inventory management is not provided.

 H0CA5 DIVE BOATS

 Aluminum hull from 50 to 60 ft boat used in operations involving diving or the need to deploy support equipment at or near the water. Examples include dive operations focusing on underwater ships husbandry
 of Fleet assets, training, underwater survey and RDT&E, as well as, general ports and waterways operations, routine harbor maintenance and cleanup duties, and to assist in patrol, rescue, firefighting and picket
 operations. Service life: 15 to 20 years.

 H0CA6 RANGE SUPPORT CRAFT

 Workboat type vessel to serve US Navy Weapons Systems Training and Validation, assisting fleet operations conducting acoustical, thermal and cross-section measurements and testing. Hulls to be steel or
 aluminum with aluminum superstructure and corrosion resistant systems, components and hardware to operate in industrial conditions with minimal maintenance. Sea kindliness underway and while loitering for
 extended periods are among the primary operational requirements. A large open workspace on the after deck with a retrieval ramp providing access to the water and appropriate weapons handling machinery.
 Weapons systems equipment to be handled includes missiles, torpedoes, surface and air launched Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) and targets. Minimal freeboard height aft is necessary for crew safety
 while accessing and operating the weapons retrieval mechanism without compromising stability. The boat must be able to accommodate extreme loading conditions (i.e., from a full weapons load topside to light
 load). The boat must be as stable and as wide as possible to provide the inherent stability characteristics necessary to perform the operations and necessary to provide ergonomics for the crew and passengers.
 Requirements include communications, navigation and other electronics systems necessary to support the sophisticated training, validation and recording of specialized measurements to support a wide variety
 of operations. A Commercial Off The Shelf (COTS) boat built to recognize commercial standards can accomplish specific operational requirements for the mission. Service Life is 25 years.

 H0CA7 40PB (formerly Patrol Boat (X))

 The craft will support maritime security operation missions and tasks such as harbor approach defense; high value unit transit escort operations; VBSS operations and overwatch; critical infrastructure protection;
 amphibious operations support; riverine operations; mine warfare support; embarked forces support (e.g., Special Operations Forces (SOF), EOD, United States Coast Guard (USCG) Law Enforcement
 Detachment (LEDET); and, Humanitarian Assistance/Disaster Relief (HA/DR) support. Twin diesel engine powered boat with a nominal length of 40 ft used to patrol green water littoral zones such as coastal
 water approaches, bays, major rivers, ports, and harbors. Service life is 15 to 20 years.

 H0CA8 DIESEL FUEL OUTBOARDS

 The Diesel Fuel Outboard Motor project supports the transition of the FY 2015 Rapid Innovation Fund "Affordable Multi-fuel Multi-engine Advanced Combatant Craft" program to the Explosive Ordnance Disposal
 Force. This technology minimizes the types of fuel required to increase standardization, flexibility, and interoperability during deployment while at the same time reduces total ownership cost. This technology is
 being transitioned to the Fleet by being installed aboard Explosive Ordnance Disposal Force Craft currently operating as well as for new procurements in the future.

 H0CA9 MK VI Patrol Boat

 MK VI Patrol Boat - Naval Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC) Riverine Forces require a tactically sized, highly reliable, combatant craft capable of operating from land or maritime platforms. The mission
 of the MK VI is to provide the operational commander a capability to persistently patrol shallow littoral areas beyond sheltered harbors and bays, and into less sheltered open water out to the Departure Sea
 Area (DSA) for the purpose of force protection of friendly and coalition forces and critical infrastructure. Mission profiles may include: patrol of Maritime Pre-positioning Force (MPF) and Joint Logistics Over the
 Shore (JLOTS) anchorage and marshaling areas, offshore economic infrastructure Gulf Oil Platforms (GOPLATs), High-Value Asset (HVA) shipping escort, over watch of MIO/VBSS operations, Theater Security
 Cooperation (TSC), and Security Force Assistance (SFA). Service life is 7 to 10 years.

 H0CAB FORCE PROTECTION BOAT (SMALL)

 Light gasoline twin outboard engine powered (up to 150 HP each) aluminum boats from 7 to 8.2 meters (24 to 27 ft) in length used primarily for fleet force protection, maritime interdiction, law enforcement
 operations, at Naval activities and adjacent ports and waterways duties. Can operate in areas where the environment (sea states/climatology) does not present a significant challenge. Service life is 7 years.

 H0G86 OCO SUPPLEMENTAL

 Current existing systems require replacements for boats due to excessive wear and tear. The boats are being maintained; however, they are not designed for the operational tempo (OPTEMPO) and harsh
 operating conditions in which they will be employed. The Force Protection Coastal (FPC) boats will provide capability called for in Visit, Board, Search and Seizure (VBSS) overwatch Urgent Operational Needs
 Statement (UONS) that cannot be met with existing boats. The Riverine Patrol boats, 7M EOD RIBS, and Force Protection (Large) are for boats already in theater.

 Coastal Riverine Force (CRF) 40PB - The craft will support maritime security operation missions and tasks such as Harbor Approach Defense; high value unit transit escort operations; VBSS operations and
 overwatch; critical infrastructure protection; amphibious operations support; riverine operations; mine warfare support; Embarked Forces Support (e.g., SOF, EOD, USCG LEDET); and, Humanitarian Assistance/
 Disaster Relief (HA/DR) Support. Twin diesel engine powered boat with a nominal length of 40 ft used to patrol green water littoral zones such as coastal water approaches, bays, major rivers, ports, and harbors.
 Service life is 15 to 20 years. This begins the reset of twenty-four (24) over age and deteriorating patrol boats continuously deployed to CENTCOM in support of Operation Freedom's Sentinel - Theater (OFS-T).
 The craft are needed to maintain operational capability and readiness to support force protection and Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance (ISR) missions in CENTCOM for OFS-T.

 Rigid Inflatable Boats (RIB) ISO Global Mine Warfare (MIW) Operations - OCO funding to support the purchase of one (1) 8.5M Rigid Inflatable Boat (RIB) to replace the Combat Rubber Rating Crafts (CRRCs)
 currently being utilized for global Unmanned Underwater Vehicle (UUV) inshore surveys. The larger RIBs are required due to the CRRCs not being designed for offshore use thus unsafe/unreliable in offshore
 maritime conditions, and severely limit maneuverability through most sea-states which is unsuitable for the majority of UUV surveys. RIBs are designed for offshore UUV survey requests. The RHIBs will greatly
 improve the ability to provide mission critical information aimed at protecting the Navy from underwater threats and support the increased naval demand in offshore operations. The project is in geographic area
 for MIW operations in support of a USCENTCOM requirement ("SPARTAN SHIELD"/FTN # 1190C066396)

 UKRAINE PRESIDENTIAL DRAWDOWN AUTHORITY (PDA)

 HSMST (HIGH SPEED MANEUVERABLE SURFACE TARGET) - Ukraine Presidential Drawdown Authority (PDA) funds to procure Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW) range support equipment that includes self-
 propelled surface targets, towed surface targets, and associated target augmentation to mimic threats and/or provide feedback. Self-propelled surface targets include the High Speed Maneuverable Surface
 Target (HSMST). Provides the user with a medium to high speed remote controlled surface target with a high degree of maneuverability. It has a form fitted collar surrounding the deck area of the aluminum hull.
 This target can exceed 45 knots in a calm sea and approaches 40 knots in a sea state 3.

 40PB - Ukraine PDA funds. The craft will support maritime security operation missions and tasks such as harbor approach defense; high value unit transit escort operations; VBSS operations and overwatch;
 critical infrastructure protection; amphibious operations support; riverine operations; mine warfare support; embarked forces support (e.g., Special Operations Forces (SOF), EOD, United States Coast Guard
 (USCG) Law Enforcement Detachment (LEDET); and, Humanitarian Assistance/Disaster Relief (HA/DR) support. Twin diesel engine powered boat with a nominal length of 40 ft used to patrol green water littoral
 zones such as coastal water approaches, bays, major rivers, ports, and harbors. Service life is 15 to 20 years.

 Unmanned Vessels - Ukraine PDA funds. Unmanned Surface Vessel Medium and Large - Used in support of Unmanned Surface Vessel testing and demonstration at the Naval Surface Warfare Center,
 Carderock Division - Combatant Craft Division's Autonomy Lab & Integration Center (ALICe) to support Research and Development of Autonomous Capability for Unmanned Surface Vessels. Provides space for
 payloads for experimentation of autonomy for both harbor and offshore/open ocean environments. Service life is 10 years.

 7M (24 ft) RIB - Ukraine PDA funds. Diesel powered, primarily used as ship's lifeboats, search and rescue boats, liberty boats, and for general transportation on auxiliaries, combatants, carriers, amphibious, and
 shore activities. Also used for AT/FP and MIO/VBSS operations. Service life is 12 years.

 11M (36 ft) RIB - Ukraine PDA funds. Carried as a ship's boat or assigned to a shore activity to perform a variety of operations including personnel and light cargo transfer, anchorage administration AT/FP
 operations and swimmer defense, visit/boarding/search and maritime interdiction, Amphibious Assault Vehicle (AAV) safety boat and Advanced Amphibious Assault Vehicle (AAAV) assist boat. Service life is 12
 years.

 CNIC Force Protection Boat (Small) - Ukraine PDA funds. Gasoline twin outboard engine aluminum boats approximately 8.5 meters (27 ft) in length with a narrow beam to aid in transport. Used primarily for
 fleet security, force protection/anti-terrorism, maritime interdiction and law enforcement operations at Naval activities and adjacent ports and waterways. The boats are provided with shouldering capabilities and
 foundations suitable for weapons. The boats operate primarily at Naval activities in sheltered harbors and waterways. Service life is 7 years.

 SSPBV BLOCKING VESSELS

 Blocking Vessels - 250 ft Off Shore Vessels (OSVs) also referred to as Blocking Vessels (BVs) operating at each Strategic Weapons Facility (Bangor, Washington and Kings Bay, Georgia). The Blocking Vessels
 are a key security component in the transit of SSBNs to and from the dive point. Service Life is 25 years.

 H0014 - SMALL UNMANNED SURFACE VEHICLES (sUSV) FAMILY OF SYSTEMS

 The sUSV Portfolio consists of a family of systems designed to meet an urgent Geographic Combatant Command Requirement.

 sUSV: Platform 1 is an unmanned vehicle that requires a high bandwidth datalink to operate at long distances. Additionally, the platform will be equipped with a radar, infrared devices, and will be capable of
 carrying various payloads. The platform will be delivered with an autonomy ready package, high-definition surveillance cameras, and remote command and control capabilities. The Navy has an emergent need to
 operate sUSVs with autonomous software while operating at long distances. The Navy has defined the emergent need to operate and refine tactics with the sUSVs.

 sUSV Next (Platform 2+): sUSV Next consists of a combination of sUSVs that will be used to conduct Naval operations to meet an urgent Geographic Combatant Command Requirement. sUSV Next is a
 production-ready, inexpensive Maritime capability leveraging the best of commercial technology and innovations at speed and scale.

 A single sUSV consists of a USV, a communications system, a command and control (C2) station, the procurement and integration of autonomy software, and a payload that will be integrated once the USV is
 delivered.

 RRL FORCE PROTECTION

ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                       Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                  Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: N/A
 Ready, Relevant Learning (RRL) is a transformational Navy training initiative designed to accelerate the learning of every Sailor for faster response to our rapidly changing warfighting requirements and to
 achieve higher performance by coupling the timing of training delivery with every Sailor's actual deck plate needs. RRL is developing a continuum-of-learning by offering the right training at the right time so that
 our Sailors are ready to operate their equipment at the extreme technical end of its capability to win the high-end fight. RRL is transforming outdated training content and archaic methodology into a modernized,
 on demand, Fleet-responsive learning system. Modernized training delivery is producing more efficient and effective training by leveraging new technologies and training support via several MyNavy HR initiatives
 including: MyNavy Career Center, My Navy Portal, Learning Stack, and Authoritative Data Environment.

 Multi-Use EOD Response Craft

 The boats are stripped down version of the Multi-Use EOD (Explosive Ordnance Disposal) response craft (MERC) in support of RRL training. The MERC is a highly versatile small boat designed for rapid
 deployment and response to explosive threats in maritime environments. Equipped with advanced EOD tools and technology, it enables precise operations, including detection, neutralization, and disposal of
 underwater and surface explosives, ensuring the safety and security of naval and civilian vessels. Service Life is 15 Years.

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $0.062 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
 "Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $0.072 million for civilian personnel to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, "Implementing the President's Department of Government
 Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative."

 The FY 2026 request for 1210 Standard Boats includes $51.546 million of discretionary and $814.969 million of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $866,515 thousand. Further information for this
 reconciliation request is provided in Sections 20002 (Shipbuilding and 200010 (Readiness) of the Reconciliation Exhibit.

 The FY 2026 request supports provision of boats for Base Operating Forces, Amphibious Assault Ships, Explosive Ordnance Forces, Naval Special Warfare, Support Forces, Fleet Logistics Support, Submarine
 Warfare, Pollution Control Equipment, Training Activities, and RRL Force Protection.

 FY 2025 matches the Base for Reprogramming Actions, DD 1414 and does not include supplemental funding from Ukraine Security Supplemental Appropriations Act, 2024 (Division B of Public Law 118-50). In
 FY 2025, DON has received $24.466 million for the procurement of 7m RIBs, 11m RIBS and Force Protection Small Boats for the replacement of Navy stock. This has results in an increase in FY 2025 quantities
 from 3 to 17 Force Protection Smalls, 4 to 17 11m RIBs and 22 to 29 7m RIBs.

 Description:
 The Operating Forces Industrial Plant Equipment (IPE) budget provides funding for capital equipment procurement in support of naval shipyards and Fleet I-level maintenance activities. These investments
 deliver modern, innovative equipment essential for maintaining submarines, aircraft carriers, and surface ships; and reducing dependence on outdated IPE, weight handling systems, and nuclear support
 infrastructure. By modernizing equipment infrastructure, these procurements enhance maintenance productivity and throughput, reduce costly rework, and help maintain cost and schedule performance across
 submarine, aircraft carrier, and surface ship availabilities.

 The Capital Investment Program (CIP), a component of the Shipyard Infrastructure Optimization Program (SIOP), supports capital equipment modernization efforts at the four public naval shipyards and their
 detachments. This includes support for Virginia-Class repairs at Norfolk Naval Shipyard (NNSY), Portsmouth Naval Shipyard (PNSY), and Pearl Harbor Naval Shipyard (PHNSY), as well as Los Angeles-
 Class Service Life Extension Refueling Overhauls at PNSY. Additionally, the Fleet Maintenance Investment Program (FMIP) funds capital equipment replacement and modernization at the U.S. Navy's regional
 maintenance centers and their detachments.

 This budget also funds the procurement and technical refresh upgrades of all Integrated Condition Assessment System (ICAS) hardware and software aboard Surface Fleet hulls as well as the Navy Maritime
 Maintenance Enterprise Solution (NMMES) which support aircraft carriers, DDGs, Los Angeles, Ohio, and Sea Wolf class vessels. NMMES is a strategic investment that has standardized Naval Sea Systems
 Command (NAVSEA) and fleet ship maintenance functions ashore, controlling and managing all aspects of ship maintenance at the naval shipyards, Regional Maintenance Centers, and Ship Repair Facilities
 worldwide.

 Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) is a maintenance optimization program that can generate significant benefits through maintenance program resets, maintenance effectiveness reviews, and active
 reliability centered maintenance (RCM) strategies at the program level. Employed as an enduring cross-life-cycle maintenance strategy, the program offers a technical solution to reduce maintenance burdens,
 increase operational availability and mission reliability of monitored systems, assists with identifying enterprise-wide cost avoidance, and will reduce overall life cycle costs. As a function of the Rapid Sustainment
 Improvement Process (RSIP), the CBM+ system will be installed on three to five surface combatants for selected shipboard equipment. Sensors for these systems will be defined, procured and installed. Further,
 the CBM+ system will be integrated with the Naval Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (N-MRO) suite of programs onboard ship to facilitate on ship and off ship monitoring.

 [P40A / KN200: NAVY MARITIME MAINTENANCE ENTERPRISE SOLUTION SUSTAINMENT]: This funding supports the Navy Maritime Maintenance Enterprise Solution (NMMES), a strategic information
 technology investment that has standardized NAVSEA and Fleet ship maintenance functions ashore, controlling and managing all aspects of ship maintenance at the Naval Shipyards, Regional Maintenance
 Centers (RMCs), and Ship Repair Facilities. Depot and Intermediate Level maintenance business processes to schedule, execute, monitor, certify work, and pay employees at aircraft carrier, submarine, and

 ship maintenance activities are codified within the NMMES toolset. Additionally, NMMES is utilized to manage finance, supply, shop workflow, configuration management, environmental health & safety, quality
 assurance, up-line management reporting and other functions. NMMES standardizes and institutionalizes the Navy's ship and submarine maintenance business practice processes. These processes are fully
 embedded in the NMMES IT systems to gain efficiencies in the maintenance process. NMMES systems provide the functionality and processes to continually address the maintenance needs of the Fleet, and
 have a critical role in the efficient brokering, planning, material ordering, financial performance, and execution of shore maintenance. This sustainment funding will support procurement of commercial-off-the-
 shelf (COTS) hardware and software in support of NMMES IT applications.

 [P40A / KN300: SHIPYARD CAPITAL INVESTMENT PROGRAM (CIP)]: Provides funding for the SIOP Capital Investment Program (CIP) in support of the consolidated Naval Shipyard and Intermediate
 Maintenance Facilities (IMF) requirements at the four Naval Shipyards. Funds will be used for the procurement and execution of Class 3 & 4 plant and personal property projects to maintain, modernize and
 improve the infrastructure and industrial base at the Naval Shipyard/IMF activities. Funding will allow for the acquisition of equipment and procurement related to Automated Data Processing (ADP) Hardware/
 Software necessary to perform the mission of repairing, conversion, and modernization of fleet ships and submarines in the most economical, efficient, environmentally sound and safe manner possible.
 Continued reliance on an aged capital equipment infrastructure increases submarine and aircraft carrier depot maintenance availability costs and places schedules at risk. Shipyard capital equipment investments
 are in accordance with the January 2023 Shipyard Infrastructure Optimization Plan Report to Congress as well as supporting new mission requirements.

 Budget year equipment requirements provided in this exhibit are based on current Shipyard priorities to best improve submarine and aircraft carrier availability execution within currently projected budget.

 [P40A / 175 TON HEAVY LIFT PORTAL CRANE]: Portsmouth Naval Shipyard's (PNSY) acquisition of a 175-ton heavy lift portal crane under the Shipyard Infrastructure Optimization Program (SIOP)
 recapitalizes an aging asset that is in poor material condition, has exceeded its expected service life, and faces increasing challenges due to parts obsolescence. This new crane is essential to support critical-
 path weight-handling operations during submarine and aircraft carrier (CVN) nuclear defueling evolutions, tasks that cannot be performed by the shipyard's remaining lower-capacity portal cranes. It will also fulfill
 a broad range of depot-level maintenance lifting and handling requirements.

 [P40A / REACTOR SERVICING COMPLEX]: Reactor Servicing Complexes (RSC), previously referred to as the Refueling Complexes, allow the Shipyards to support the Fleet's projected need for simultaneous
 Virginia Class depot level maintenance availabilities and concurrent 688 Class Refuelings. FY26 provides the dock-side Reactor Access Enclosure (RAE) for the FY25 RSC at Portsmouth Naval Shipyard.
 Providing RSCs at Dry Docks is required to support the Shipyard's mission of submarine overhauls and refuelings into the future. The RSC is a freestanding piece of relocatable equipment that processes spent
 nuclear fuel. This equipment allows fuel to be safely removed from a submarine and packaged for transportation to a storage facility, via M-140 railcar. The in-dock equipment performs the reactor servicing
 operation of processing spent nuclear fuel from the submarine into a shielded container. The in-dock complex industrial processing equipment includes two support towers, a reactor access enclosure (RAE)
 bridge, an upper RAE, and passageways leading from the in-dock complex to the dockside passageway stair tower. The dockside equipment performs the reactor servicing operation of processing spent
 nuclear fuel from a shielded container into three M-140 railcars. The dockside complex industrial processing equipment typically includes a dockside railcar enclosure, an equipment storage/staging enclosure, a
 radiological material packaging enclosure, and an enclosed passageway and stair tower leading to the in-dock complex. Both the dockside complex and in-dock complex equipment require concrete foundations
 to safely support equipment operation. The foundations required for the dockside complex do not structurally change existing real property. The foundation is part of the necessary relocatable equipment
 because of the size, mass and function of the equipment.

 [P40A / CONGRESSIONAL ADD - SUBMARINE SEISMIC CRADLES]: This Seismic Cradle Stability project is required to comply with NAVSEA 08 dry dock seismic requirements. In the event of a seismic
 event, the system will prevent the docked vessel from rolling over in the dry dock, thus significantly reducing the risk of ship damage and injury to personnel. FY24 funds cradle requirement at Puget Sound Naval
 Shipyard.

 [P40A / CONGRESSIONAL ADD - OILY WASTE TREATMENT SYSTEM]: This project replaces an aged Oily Waste Treatment System at Puget Sound Naval Shipyard. This system is designed to remove oil
 and other containminants from wastewater produced by industrial and ship operations. The goal of this system is to ensure that the treated water meets environmental standards before being discharged or
 reused.

 [P40A / INDUSTRY 4.0 CAPABILITIES ASSET VISIBILITY MANAGEMENT]: FY24 Industry 4.0 Capabilities - Equipment Monitoring and Advanced Manufacturing:
 As part of a SIOP initiative this project implements industry proven solutions to connect equipment for digital manufacturing, increase throughput, quality, report on machine health. Provides hardware (HW)
 & upgrades drivers for capable equipment, enabling connectivity to accept data electronically versus punching it in at the terminal or using a thumb drive and report on equipment operating status/machine

 [P40A / KN400: FLEET MAINTENANCE INVESTMENT PROGRAM]: Provides funding to support capital investments for the Regional Maintenance Centers (RMCs), including forward deployed RMCs, Trident
 Refit Facility (TRF) Kings Bay (KB), Naval Submarine Support Facility New London (NSSF NL), Navy Base Guam and U.S. Naval Ship Repair Facility and Japan Regional Maintenance Center (SRF-JRMC).
 Funds will be used for the procurement and execution of personal property projects (including Industrial Plant Equipment greater than $350K) to maintain and modernize the ship maintenance industrial base).

 [P40A / KN443: Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+)]: Funds are provided to install integrated digital Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+) / Mission Readiness Systems aboard Navy Combatants.

 [P40A / Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+) (N4)]: Funds are provided to install integrated digital Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+) / Mission Readiness Systems aboard Navy Combatants.

 [P40A / KN44A: Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+)]: Funds are provided to install integrated digital Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+) / Mission Readiness Systems aboard Navy Combatants.

 [P40A / Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+) (N4)]: Funds are provided to install integrated digital Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+) / Mission Readiness Systems aboard Navy Combatants.

 [P40A / Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+) (N98)]: Funds are provided to install integrated digital Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+) / Mission Readiness Systems aboard Navy Combatants.

 [P40A / KN800: INTEGRATED CONDITION ASSESSMENT SYSTEM (ICAS) AND ENTERPRISE REMOTE MONITORING (eRM) NEW INSTALL]: Procures all new installations of the Integrated Condition
 Assessment System (ICAS) and Enterprise Remote Monitoring (eRM) hardware and software aboard Surface Fleet hulls. New Installation include: ICAS/eRM workstation hardware (including Palm Pilot Portable
 Data Terminal (PDTs)), latest version of ICAS/eRM system software, Configuration Data Set (CDS) (including the implementation of developed enhancements), firewall (if applicable), remote monitoring utility
 (RMU), ILS and ship's force training. Manage contractor efforts, prepare installation plans, perform ship checks, procure material, oversee shipboard installation and Quality Assurance (QA), develop/implement
 CDS, install/test all software and CDSs, provide ship's force training.

 [P40A / KN900: INTEGRATED CONDITION ASSESSMENT SYSTEM (ICAS) AND ENTERPRISE REMOTE MONITORING (eRM) TECH REFRESH]: Procures all technical refresh upgrades of the Integrated
 Condition Assessment System (ICAS) and Enterprise Remote Monitoring (eRM) hardware and software aboard Surface Fleet hulls. Upgrades will include: ICAS/eRM workstation hardware (including Palm Pilot
 Portable Data Terminal (PDTs)), latest version of ICAS/eRM system software, Configuration Data Set (CDS) groom (including the implementation of developed enhancements), firewall (if applicable), remote
 monitoring utility (RMU), and ship's force refresher training. Manage contractor efforts, prepare installation plans, perform ship checks, procure material, oversee shipboard installation and Quality Assurance
 (QA), develop/implement CDS updates, install/test all software and CDSs, provide ship's force training.

 [P40A / KN975: PACOM PMTEC STUDIES]: Funds for Pacific Multi-Domain Training and Experimentation Capability (PMTEC) studies, Wargaming Analysis Capabilities, USINDOPACOM training and exercise
 network capabilities, PMTEC program management cell, and establishment of Exercise Experimentation Program and Joint Battle Problem. PMTEC is a joint initiative to integrate and improve elements of
 existing ranges, training areas and test facilities in the Pacific to provide a multi-domain test, training and experimentation capability. This capability will enable USINDOPACOM to strengthen the overall
 readiness and interoperability of U.S. forces across all domains and to test new warfighting concepts to improve joint force lethality. Live Fire Target Support, Joint Force Development and Design (JFDD)
 Capability, Joint Deployable Exercise Control Cell (JDECC), Regional Joint Training Infrastructure and Theater Mobile Sensitive Compartmented Information Facility (SCIF).
 Build Defenses and Security Capability of Allies- Funding supports the continued Zero Trust (ZT) pilot for USINDOPACOM's Mission Partner Environment (MPE).

 Justification:
 The FY 2026 request was reduced by $2.011 million for civilian personnel to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, "Implementing the President's Department of Government
 Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative."


 FY26 funding supports the following efforts:

 KN300 (CIP supporting SIOP) requires $134.356 million to support the procurement of capital equipment for naval shipyards and Fleet I-level maintenance activities. These investments enable the acquisition of
 modern, innovative equipment essential for maintaining submarines, aircraft carriers, and surface ships, reducing dependence on severely aged and obsolete industrial plant equipment (IPE), weight handling
 systems, and nuclear support infrastructure. Projects include, but are not limited to, the procurement of portal cranes, crawler cranes, mobile cranes, hydraulic test stands, pipe benders, plasma cutters,
 generators, bridge mills, and electronic security systems.


 KN200 NMMES requires $6.049 million to support the replacement of the COST application using S4/HANA SAP software. COST is the critical application that provides standardized, current, and accurate
 cost accounting data for all Naval Sea Systems Command (NAVSEA) ship maintenance activities. The existing COST application has reached end-of-life, operates on obsolete software components, and
 is increasingly expensive to maintain. A full system replacement is necessary to resolve sustainability issues, enhance support for current and future ship maintenance requirements, and enable expanded
 capabilities. This modernization effort is being executed under a time-sensitive Other Transaction Authority (OTA) Production contract. The objective is to transition from the legacy COST system to a modern
 platform that improves sustainability, enhances interface capabilities, reduces operational costs, aligns with updated ship maintenance business processes, and meets evolving Department of Defense (DoD)
 standards. (Note: "COST" is the name of the application and is not an acronym or abbreviation).

 KN400 Fleet Maintenance Investment Program funding of $29.214M supports procurement of industrial equipment to maintain and modernize the ship maintenance industrial base at regional maintenance
 centers. Projects include boring mills, test stands, lathes, and laser ablation units.

 KN443 Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+) requires funding of $0.981 million focusing on Naval Sea Systems Command (NAVSEA) integration efforts.

 KN44A Condition Based Maintenance (CBM+) requires funding of $3.184 million focusing on Naval Air Systems Command (NAVAIR) integration efforts.

 KN800 Integrated Condition Assessment System (ICAS) AND eRM (New Install) requires funding of $5.949 million to support the ICAS and eRM program.

 KN900 Integrated Condition Assessment System (ICAS) AND eRM (Tech Refresh) requires $1.804 million to support the ICAS and eRM program.

 KN975 PMTEC Studies requires $27.461 million to support efforts in the areas of warfare-gaming analysis and USINDOPACOM training and exercise network capabilities.

 Footnotes:
 (1)
    Supports procurement of commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS) hardware and software (HW/SW) in support of NMMES Financial Tool Upgrades, including Maritime Systems Environment (MSE) and the Electronic Technical Work Document
 (eTWD) initiative. Provides required development, test, and cybersecurity toolsets; SW/HW required to bring RMCs into eTWD. Provides HW required to deploy mobility solutions assets at maintenance activities with corporate reporting
 capability. Increase also supports efforts to establish networks for Platform IT devices and relevant industrial equipment to address cybersecurity updates, patches, and SW installation.
 (2)
   This list of CESE equipment includes a Rough Terrain Mobile Cranes, 3-Axle Container Chassis, Arial Work Platform, Trailers (Various capacity/configuration), Nitrogen Charging Trailer, Pure Water Trailers, Cask Trailer, emergency
 Response Equipment, Crawler Cranes and Locomotives
 (3)
   Funds will be used for the procurement and execution of personal property projects (including Industrial Plant Equipment greater than $350K) to maintain and modernize the ship maintenance industrial base. SRF-JRMC was realigned to
 FMIP beginning in FY22.
 (4)
     Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) is a maintenance optimization program that can generate significant benefits through maintenance program resets, maintenance effectiveness reviews, and active reliability centered
 maintenance (RCM) strategies at the program level. Employed as an enduring cross-life-cycle maintenance strategy, the program offers a technical solution to reduce maintenance burdens, increase operational availability and mission
 reliability of monitored systems, assists with identifying enterprise-wide cost avoidance, and will reduce overall life cycle costs. As a function of the Rapid Sustainment Improvement Process (RSIP), the CBM+ system will be installed on three
 to five surface combatants for selected shipboard equipment. Sensors for these systems will be defined, procured and installed. Further, the CBM+ system will be integrated with the Naval Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (N-MRO) suite of
 programs onboard ship to facilitate on ship and off ship monitoring.
 (5)
     Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) is a maintenance optimization program that can generate significant benefits through maintenance program resets, maintenance effectiveness reviews, and active reliability centered
 maintenance (RCM) strategies at the program level. Employed as an enduring cross-life-cycle maintenance strategy, the program offers a technical solution to reduce maintenance burdens, increase operational availability and mission
 reliability of monitored systems, assists with identifying enterprise-wide cost avoidance, and will reduce overall life cycle costs. As a function of the Rapid Sustainment Improvement Process (RSIP), the CBM+ system will be installed on three
 to five surface combatants for selected shipboard equipment. Sensors for these systems will be defined, procured and installed. Further, the CBM+ system will be integrated with the Naval Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (N-MRO) suite of
 programs onboard ship to facilitate on ship and off ship monitoring.
 (6)
     Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) is a maintenance optimization program that can generate significant benefits through maintenance program resets, maintenance effectiveness reviews, and active reliability centered
 maintenance (RCM) strategies at the program level. Employed as an enduring cross-life-cycle maintenance strategy, the program offers a technical solution to reduce maintenance burdens, increase operational availability and mission
 reliability of monitored systems, assists with identifying enterprise-wide cost avoidance, and will reduce overall life cycle costs. As a function of the Rapid Sustainment Improvement Process (RSIP), the CBM+ system will be installed on three
 to five surface combatants for selected shipboard equipment. Sensors for these systems will be defined, procured and installed. Further, the CBM+ system will be integrated with the Naval Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (N-MRO) suite of
 programs onboard ship to facilitate on ship and off ship monitoring.
 (7)
    FY26 $12.729M increase for INDOPACOM PACIFIC Multi-Domain Training and Experimentations Capability (PMTEC) is critical to enhance joint and coalition readiness in the Indo-Pacific region. This investment will establish forward
 operating nodes west of the IDL: Providing immediate operational capability for joint training exercises and rehearsals, directly supporting multi-domain training audiences with mission planning and execution. These nodes will also offer
 CONUS reach-back, secure debriefing capabilities, and high-classification collaboration. Establish a redundant DISA-Pacific SDN in the Philippines: Ensuring resilient connectivity to the Common Mission Network Transport (CMNT) for secure
 coalition communications during USINDOPACOM Joint Exercise Program (JEP) events and the PMTEC. This provides vital support for both theater exercises and potential real-world operations. Funding will be used to equip these facilities
 with essential furniture, IT, and communication infrastructure.

Gross/Weapon System Unit Cost ($ in Dollars)                 -              -               -              -              -               -              -               -         -            -     -            -


 Description:
 Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) focused mission packages provide the operational commander with capabilities to perform Mine Countermeasures (MCM) operations, and a Surface Warfare (SUW) detect-to-
 engage capability to counter small boat threats. A Mission Package (MP) is a combination of warfare Mission Modules (MM) with specialized crew, support equipment, and vehicles, including embarked manned
 helicopters, unmanned aircraft and maritime systems. MMs are packaged in a modular fashion so that they can be quickly swapped in and out pier side on any LCS platform.

 The Common Mission Modules Equipment line funds the procurement of MCM and SUW MP support containers and C5I equipment that is common across the mission packages (Mission Package Computing
 Environment (MPCE), Technology Refresh of the Multi-Vehicle Communications System (MVCS), and Training Equipment).

 MCM and SUW support containers do not comprise a MP and are deployable only as part of a Mission Module (MM). A MM is deployable and capable of meeting its focused mission requirements on a LCS only
 when it includes the full complement of Common Mission Modules Equipment combined with all the required mission systems.

 Other Related Budgets: BLIs: OPN 1601, OPN 1602, OPN 1603, OPN 4248, OPN 2622, OPN 2624, WPN 4221, WPN 4225, RDT&E 0603596N, SCN 2127

 [P40A / LM008 - MPCE]: Commercial Off The Shelf (COTS)- Based Common Computing environment will support legacy Mission Package (MP) Command & Control (C2) applications and transition to Modular
 Open Systems Approach (MOSA) & Navy Open Architecture Computing Environment (OACE). It also provides a common computing environment to support the execution of Mission Package Application
 Software (MPAS), the shipboard tactical software associated with each mission package that enables execution of its focused mission. Includes installation and checkout on-board the seaframe. In addition,
 Mission Package Portable Control Stations (MPPCS) are required to support pre-deployment checkouts, to support shore-based and vessels of opportunity operations of mission packages and off-board vehicles.

 [P40A / MPCE 2.X Lethality and Survivability (MPCE 2.0)/Non-L&S (MPCE 2.1)]: MPCE 2.X improves data extraction, data encryption, cybersecurity enhancements, and virtualization capabilities needed for all
 three Mission Packages (MPs).

 [P40A / LM012 - TRAINING EQUIPMENT]: Procurement of LCS Mission Module training systems for LCS crews to learn the safe and effective use of Mission Package equipment (mission systems, support
 equipment, and support containers). LCS MM Training is planned for several training sites including the LCS Training Facility (LTFs) Pacific in San Diego, CA and LFT Atlantic in Mayport, FL for all MPs; the
 Surface Combat Systems Training Command (SCSTC) in Dam neck, VA for the SUW MP; and at the Mine Warfare Training Center (MWTC) in San Diego, CA for the MCM MP.



LI 1600 - LCS Common Mission Modules Equipment                                                          UNCLASSIFIED
                                                                                                                                                                                                    Volume 1 - 549

Exhibit P-40, Budget Line Item Justification: PB 2026 Navy                                                                                                       Date: June 2025
Appropriation / Budget Activity / Budget Sub Activity:                                                     P-1 Line Item Number / Title:
1810N: Other Procurement, Navy / BA 01: Ships Support Equipment / BSA 15:                                  1600 / LCS Common Mission Modules Equipment
Other Ship Support
ID Code (A=Service Ready, B=Not Service Ready): A                      Program Elements for Code B Items: N/A                                  Other Related Program Elements: N/A
Line Item MDAP/MAIS Code: 443
 The LTFs provide tactical watch team training as well as crew certifications using Common Mission Package Trainers (CMPT) in combination with the Integrated Tactical Trainers (ITTs). The LTFs also provide
 full scale replications of LCS mission bays where launch, recovery, and handling procedures for LCS MM systems is taught and practiced. LCS MM training covers various functions including operations,
 maintenance, tactical employment, and hands-on skills to achieve the key performance parameter objective of Train to Qualify and Train to Certify. CMPTs are used to train individuals in a team environment
 (Train to Qualify) and are also used to certify watch teams (TYCOM Training for Certification) during off hull periods at LTF.


The LCS Integrated Tactical Trainer (ITT) uses the CMPT for mission package operations. Six CMPTs were procured and delivered from FY11-FY21to support three ITTs at LTF Mayport (LCS-1 configuration)
and three ITTs at LTF San Diego (LCS-2 configuration). One ITT with CMPT in the LCS-1 configuration was decommissioned in FY 22 following decisions to reduce the overall training requirements.

Full MCM and SUW integrated team training exists at LTF San Diego and full SUW capability in Mayport. MCM ITT functions with USV capability was procured in FY 2019 and delivered to each LTF in FY 2021.
MCM USV with Mine Hunt capability as procured in FY 2022 for delivery to LTF Pacific and Mine Warfare Training Center in FY 2025.

MCM MP: All unmanned vehicles, sensors, and systems in support containers to demonstrate Launch & Recovery (L&R) and maintenance tasks. Training involves operations, maintenance, tactical employment
and physical movement of all unmanned vehicles, sensors, and systems with their associated support containers. Surface Training Advanced Virtual Environment for LCS (STAVE-LCS) (virtual reality) is planned
to allow team training LCS
Launch and Recovery of Unmanned Systems (USV and UUV) in FY 2024. Procurements are aligned to meet Fleet training throughput demands.

[P40A / LM015 - CONTAINERS (SHIPPING & SUPPORT)]: Each MP variant uses a base Support Container (SC) which is an International Organization for Standardization (ISO) compliant shipping container,
and comes in two variants: Type I and Type II. The Type I container is a 20-foot ISO container with nominal dimensions of 20' x 8' x 8'. The Type II container is a 10-foot ISO container with nominal dimensions of
10' x 8' x 8'. In both cases the base SC is constructed of high strength steel or aluminum to reduce the overall weight and features a number of standard configuration items. Mission systems fit inside standard
ten- or twenty-foot ISO SCs, or on ISO compliant flat racks and vehicle cradles. The LCS MP zones inside each seaframe mission bay are configured to accommodate the SCs.

[P40A / MCM SUPPORT CONTAINER]: Unit price for the MCM Support Containers is an average cost that does not reflect the actual cost of each container. Individual containers for each of the different MCM
mission systems have unique requirements and vary in cost. Support containers store all the tools, spares, peculiar support equipment (PSE), consumables and personal protection equipment (PPE) required to
maintain and operate the MP systems while on LCS deployment.

[P40A / LM016 - MVCS]: The Multiple Vehicle Communications System (MVCS) provides the Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) Mission Modules with the capability to simultaneously communicate with multiple
Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USVs) and surfaced Unmanned Undersea Vehicles (UUVs) by providing a common data link and network communication services. These services include both line of sight and
satellite link establishment, link management, communications fail-safes, Emissions Control (EMCON) handling, encryption, data routing and protection, packet shaping, isolation via cross domain solution.
Priority of Service (PoS), and automated communications system configuration. MVCS establishes a common interface for these services in the form of hardware and message definitions to maximize the
usefulness of limited radio equipment onboard the LCS seaframe.

[P40A / LM017 - Mission Module Display Modernization]: The Mission Module Display Modernization mitigates console hardware obsolescence and provides monitor resolution required to support the Mission
Package operations.

[P40A / LM900 - CONSULTING SERVICES]: Provides program management support, engineering and technical support, studies, analysis and evaluation on mission package systems.

Justification:
The FY 2026 request was reduced by $2.424 million for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222,
"Implementing the President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative."

FY 2026 funding in LM008 (MPCE) supports the continued technology refresh of the MPCE. In FY 2026, the program continues the procurement of MPCE 2.X (MPCE 2.0 for Lethality and Survivability (L&S) and
MPCE 2.1 for Non-L&S hulls) to address obsolescence, cyber security enhancements, and performance improvements in the MPCE baseline. Also, MPCE 2.X improves data extraction, data encryption, and
virtualization capabilities which reduces the rack space needed for Post Mission Analysis (PMA) for all Mission Packages (MPs).

LM012 CMPT/MPTS Tech Refresh/Modernization: Funds the technology refresh of five CMPTs at the LCS Training Facilities (LTFs) in San Diego, CA / Mayport, FL. CMPT tech refreshes support the operation
of MPCE version 2.0 software requirements. This line also supports technology refresh for the Mission Package Training System in LTF Atlantic in Mayport, which delivers SUW Mission Package and Surface-to-
Surface Missile Module operations training.

MCM Total Ship Training Capability (TSTC), funded out of Strategic Laydown Decision FY 2024, will deliver an onboard training capability to the LCS 2 Variant ships in the Mine Countermeasures (MCM)
configuration TSTC
funding in FY 2025 will address an initial procurement for shore-based testing and certification prior to installation on the first ship to transition to the Forward Deployed Naval Force (FDNF). FY26 are the
procurements for the remaining fourteen LCS 2 Variant ships with installation to occur in a future FY.

LM015 supports the procurement of MCM Support Containers required to outfit LCS MCM configured Ships. Support containers are deployed on the ship with an embarked MP to store spares, tools, support
equipment, and consumables required to maintain and operate the mission system and aircraft when deployed.

LM016 (MVCS) supports procurement of MVCS Tech Refresh equipment including beyond-line-of-sight communications, software defined radio, and anti-jamming capabilities/approvals to include new Cross
Domain Solution hardware to meet National Security Agency (NSA) mandated security requirements.

LM017 (MP Console Obsolescence) The LCS Mission Package Consoles are a common console used by all of the LCS Mission Packages. Current consoles are provided as permanently installed equipment by
the LCS seaframe vendor. The seven Mission Package consoles provide an interface for MP sailors to operate the mission package tactical software and conduct MP operations. The current Mission Package
consoles are no longer supportable due to lack of available COTS parts. In addition to obsolescence, the tech refresh consoles will provide additional capability required by the MCM MP to conduct post mission
analysis, display resolution and CPU processing that the existing consoles do not support.

FY26 decrease due to five fewer procurements in FY26 than in FY25.

Footnotes:
(1)
    MPCE 2.X support both L&S (MPCE 2.0) and non-L&S (MPCE 2.1) hulls. MPCE 2.x is required to support planned LCS Lethality and Survivability updates and hardware obsolescence. Funding supports procurement of MPCE to remain in
alignment with these updates and ensure future compatibility between LCS combat system and mission modules.

(2)
      Procure CMPT tech refresh for phased maintenance updates in alignment with Fleet configuration changes for installation and check-out with lethality and survivability upgrades to LCS Integrated Tactical Trainers at each LTF learning site.
(3)
      SLD-25 Decision requires On Board Trainer (OBT) to be established in order to accommodate change in CONOPS associated with a new Area of operations (Ao).
(4)
   Cost and scope of MVCS Tech Refreshes varies each year based on the condition of the units that are being refreshed, as well as the components needed for each tech refresh kit due to different MVCS baselines and equipment
(shipboard, lab, radios, etc.).

      Mission Module Display Modernization quantities and unit costs represent per shipset (7 consoles per shipset).

 Description:
 The Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) Mine Countermeasures (MCM) Mission Package (MP) counters bottom, tethered, near surface, and surface mines in the littorals without putting Sailors in the minefield. When
 the MCM MP is embarked, LCS is capable of conducting detect-to-engage operations (hunting, sweeping, and neutralization) against shallow and deep-water sea mine threats. The MCM MP provides these
 capabilities through the use of sensors and weapons deployed from an embarked MH-60S multi-mission helicopter and unmanned offboard vehicles. MCM MP achieved Initial Operational Capability in
 31MAR2023.

 The embarked MH-60S employs the Organic Airborne Mine Countermeasures (OAMCM) systems to provide rapid and long range airborne detection and neutralization to clear operations in littoral zones,
 confined straits, choke points, and the Amphibious Operations Area (AOA) quickly. The Airborne Mine Neutralization (AMN) Module (MH-60S Helo + Airborne Mine Neutralization System) and Near Surface
 Detection (NSD) Module (MH-60S Helo + Airborne Laser Mine Detection System) achieved Initial Operational Capability (IOC) in FY 2017.

 The Unmanned Minesweeping (UMS) Module is composed of the MCM Unmanned Surface Vehicle (USV) and Minesweeping Payload Delivery System (PDS) and Towed Acoustic Generator (TAG). The
 MCM USV and Minesweeping PDS was previously known as Unmanned Influence Sweeping System (UISS) which achieved Milestone C and is currently in Low Rate Initial Production (LRIP). The Remote
 Minehunting (RMH) Module (MCM USV with Minehunting PDS and AN/AQS-20C) detects and identifies bottom and tethered mines. In FY 2020, the MCM USV established a mature Technical Data Package
 (TDP) and was used for the award of the Production contract in FY2022. The Unmanned Influence Sweep System (UISS) TDP is mature and is being leveraged to procure Minesweeping PDS in Q2FY2024
 under the Multiple Award Contracts (MAC) Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) USV Family of Systems Contract. In FY 2022, the Minehunting PDS will be procured via a sole source delivery order on
 the Raytheon IDIQ contract. A competitive production contract will be awarded for future Minehunting PDS procurements in Q3 FY2024.

 The Buried Minehunting (BMH) Module (Knifefish Unmanned Undersea Vehicle) detects and identifies buried mines and mines in a high clutter environment. Knifefish achieved Milestone C in FY 2019 and is
 currently in LRIP. The Navy is executing transition from a Block 0 to Block 1 capability. In FY 2021, Knifefish awarded a Block 1 retrofit contract that will establish a mature TDP in preparation for its production
 contract, and upgrade Block 0 LRIPs to Block 1. The Navy will conduct a limited validation of these capabilities during acceptance testing and sell off to the government in Q1-Q3FY24. Upon delivery of these
 systems to the Navy, they will be available for limited Fleet operations.

 The MCM MP system procurements in this BLI are critical to deliver MCM MPs with the capability of the full detect-to-neutralize sequence and replace legacy MCM platforms.

 the full complement of mission systems that comprise each MCM Mission Package (Line Item Number 1601).

Justification:
The FY 2026 request was reduced by $0.385 million for civilian personnel to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, "Implementing the President's Department of Government
Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative."

FY 2026 funding includes the following mission systems and associated MP support equipment:

In FY 2026 the program will procure six MCM Unmanned Surface Vehicle (USV), two Minesweeping PDS, two Minehunting PDS, two AN/AQS-20C variable depth Minehunting Sonar and will procure MCM MP
support equipment. Additionally, the program will implement Engineering Change Proposals (ECPs) on procured production MCM USVs and perform tech refreshes to address AQS-20C obsolescence. ECPs
will also be implemented on both PDS's and Twin Boom Extensible Crane (TEBC). The support equipment includes; USV redesigned Surface Tow Cradles (STC-Rs), USV Stowage Cradles, LCS PMA Table
Fixture Devices (TFD), and Peculiar Support Equipment (PSE) kit for USV and UISS.

All MCM assets procured support MCM configured ship deployments. System procurements include the limiting factors required to deliver MCM MPs with the full complement of systems needed to perform the
detect-to-neutralize sequence. These systems are critical to complete delivery of MPs to replace legacy MCM platforms.

                                     -               -   680.762             -             -    87.828              -             -   118.247              -               -    91.372              -               -      0.000             -                -    91.372
Cost


Remarks:
[Hardware] MC001 - The Unmanned Minesweeping Module provides an unmanned surface, long endurance bottom sweep capability to clear large areas of mines that may remain after minehunting operations
are complete or in areas of operation where minehunting is less feasible. The UMS Module cost element funds the procurement of the Minesweeping Payload Delivery System to include the magnetic sweep
cable and acoustic MK-104 payload. The USVs are procured in the MC002 USVs cost element.

[Hardware] MC002 - There are two USVs per MCM MP which will be reconfigurable for sweep, hunt, and eventually neutralization missions. Four MCM USVs have been procured as Low Rate Initial Production
(LRIP) units on the UISS contract. The program is incorporating engineering changes proposals (ECPs) kits to upgrade all previously procured UISS assets to the common configuration (enables sweep, hunt,
and neutralization). In April 0f 2022, the program awarded a competitively sought contract to procure MCM USVs in the common configuration. All future procurements will be the common configuration.

[Hardware] MC005 - The Buried Minehunting Module provides capability to detect and identify buried mines and mines in a cluttered ocean bottom environment. This procurement line supports the LCS mine
countermeasures mission package, providing deployable combat capability to the LCS MCM squadron.

[Hardware] MC006 - MCM SUPPORT EQUIPMENT]: Support equipment related to the individual modules and in support of the overall Mission Package.

[Hardware] MC008 - The Remote Minehunting (RMH) Module consists of an Unmanned Surface Vehicle and a Minehunting Payload Delivery System (PDS) for deploying and retrieving the AN/AQS-20
minehunting sonar. The RMH Module cost element funds the procurement of the Minehunting PDS and AN/AQS-20 minehunting sonar payloads. The USVs are procured in the MC002 - USVs cost element.

[Support Cost] MC900 - Provides program management support, engineering and technical support, studies, analysis and evaluation on mission module systems.

[Support Cost] Production Engineering is required in all years of procurement as well as 15-24 months after procurement to support continuing production efforts and fund acceptance testing in year of delivery.

   AMNS Tech Refreshes improve reliability and maintainability to previously procured AMNS units and correct deficiencies that allow the equipment to perform in accordance with current deployment
configuration / operational requirements.
(3)
   Prior Year Knifefish Production Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) funds efforts on the Block 1 Retrofit Contract. This funding is captured in prior years due to a cost avoidance realized by descoping the Block
0 production effort (LRIP systems 3 through 5 [FY19 funding]). Descoped funding is being applied to Block 1 Retrofit contract.
(4)
      FY 2023 funding will complete incorporation of Knifefish Block 1 Engineering Change Proposes (ECPs) onto the Block 0 LRIP systems.
(5)
   The unit cost includes all necessary equipment to outfit the Buried Minehunting (BMH) Module to include the base pallet, launch and recovery device, shipping containers, support equipment (deck tie down
case, ballast transport case, grapple assembly, winch case, and other tools), and the BMH Specialized Container.
